items.how_to_fix: Enable only TLS 1.2 and 1.3, disable SSLv2/v3/TLS1.0/1.1, use ECDHE cipher suites (forward secrecy), disable TLS compression, and drop 3DES/RC4/EXPORT to close POODLE/BEAST/CRIME/Sweet32/FREAK. Patch OpenSSL (Heartbleed). Follow Mozilla's 'Modern/Intermediate' config. VERIFY with testssl.sh / SSL Labs (A/A+, no known attacks).

items.title: End-to-End Encryption
items.description: Verify the implementation of end-to-end encryption for sensitive data.
items.how_to_find:
For apps claiming E2EE (messaging, health, secure notes):
1) Confirm the server never sees plaintext - intercept in Burp and check the payload is ciphertext, and that the server can't decrypt (keys held only by clients).
2) Review the scheme: proper protocol (e.g. Signal/libsodium) vs home-grown; authenticated encryption (AES-GCM); per-message keys/ratcheting; secure key exchange (X25519/ECDH).
3) Red flags: keys generated/stored server-side, key escrow, plaintext in logs/backups, or 'E2E' that actually decrypts at the server.
4) Verify metadata handling and that TLS is still used underneath.
items.how_to_fix: Implement E2EE with vetted libraries/protocols (libsodium, Signal protocol): keys generated and held client-side only, authenticated encryption (AES-GCM/ChaCha20-Poly1305), secure key exchange, and forward secrecy - the server stores only ciphertext. Keep TLS in transit as well. VERIFY the server cannot decrypt intercepted payloads.

items.title: Random Number Generation
items.description: Ensure that cryptographic functions use secure random number generation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect many security-sensitive tokens (session ids, password-reset tokens, API keys, OTPs, CSRF tokens) and analyze for predictability:
   - Use Burp Sequencer on session/reset tokens to measure entropy.
   - Look for sequential, timestamp-based, or short tokens (guessable/brute-forceable).
2) Code review for weak RNG feeding security use:
   grep -riE 'Math.random|rand\(|mt_rand|Random\(\)|new Random' src/
   These are NOT cryptographically secure.
3) Test whether tokens can be predicted from a known one (same seed / low entropy).
items.how_to_fix: Generate all security tokens with a cryptographically secure RNG (CSPRNG): crypto.randomBytes (Node), secrets (Python), java.security.SecureRandom, random_bytes (PHP), crypto/rand (Go) - never Math.random/rand/mt_rand. Use sufficient length (>=128 bits). VERIFY with Burp Sequencer (high entropy) and code review (no weak RNG).

items.title: Deprecated Protocols
items.description: Verify that deprecated protocols (e.g., SSLv3) are disabled.
items.how_to_find:
1) Probe explicitly for deprecated protocol versions:
   nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 target.tld
   openssl s_client -connect target.tld:443 -ssl3   (should FAIL to connect)
   openssl s_client -connect target.tld:443 -tls1    (should FAIL)
   openssl s_client -connect target.tld:443 -tls1_1  (should FAIL)
2) If SSLv3/TLS1.0/1.1 negotiate, the endpoint is exposed to POODLE/BEAST and downgrade attacks.
3) Also check other services/ports (SMTP/IMAP/APIs) and CDN/origin, not just the main site.
4) Watch for older insecure app protocols too (FTP, Telnet, unauthenticated HTTP).
items.how_to_fix: Disable SSLv2/SSLv3/TLS 1.0/TLS 1.1 everywhere (web, mail, APIs, origin) and allow only TLS 1.2+ (prefer 1.3). Retire insecure app protocols (FTP/Telnet -> SFTP/SSH). VERIFY the openssl -ssl3/-tls1/-tls1_1 handshakes all fail.

items.title: HMAC
items.description: Ensure the use of HMAC for integrity checks where applicable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find where the app signs/verifies data (API request signatures, webhook signatures, signed URLs, tamper-proof tokens) and check the MAC design:
   - Is it a real HMAC (HMAC-SHA256) or a naive hash of secret+data (vulnerable to length-extension for MD5/SHA1/SHA256 raw)?
   - Test length-extension with hashpump/hash_extender where H(secret||data) is used.
2) Test verification bypasses: strip the signature, send an empty/garbage MAC, or downgrade the algorithm.
3) Check for non-constant-time comparison (timing side channel) leaking the correct MAC byte-by-byte.
4) For webhooks, try forging/replaying without a valid signature.
items.how_to_fix: Use HMAC (HMAC-SHA256+) with a strong secret for integrity/authenticity - not raw hashes (avoids length-extension). Verify with a constant-time comparison, cover the full canonical message, pin the algorithm, and reject missing/invalid MACs. Sign webhooks and validate them server-side. VERIFY length-extension/forgery/replay attempts fail.

items.title: Secure Cloud Configuration
items.description: Ensure that cloud resources are configured securely (e.g., storage buckets, virtual machines).
items.how_to_fix: Apply CIS Benchmarks/well-architected security: block public access on storage by default, encrypt buckets/volumes, restrict security groups, remove public snapshots/AMIs, and manage everything as IaC scanned in CI (checkov/tfsec). Continuously scan with ScoutSuite/Prowler and remediate. VERIFY the bucket/metadata/exposure tests all fail.
items.how_to_find:
1) EXTERNAL: enumerate public storage and services:
   aws s3 ls s3://target-bucket --no-sign-request        (anonymous list)
   aws s3 cp s3://target-bucket/secret.txt - --no-sign-request
   Guess bucket names (company, app, env) or use tools like s3scanner/cloud_enum; check Azure Blob & GCS equivalents.
2) SSRF -> cloud metadata (very high impact): if the app is SSRF-able, hit the IMDS:
   http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/  (AWS IMDSv1)
   -> steal temporary IAM creds.
3) INTERNAL (with read creds): run an automated posture scan:
   scoutsuite aws   ;   prowler aws
   Flag public buckets, open security groups (0.0.0.0/0 to 22/3389/DB ports), unencrypted volumes, public AMIs/snapshots, no MFA on root.

items.title: Identity and Access Management (IAM)
items.description: Verify that IAM policies follow the principle of least privilege.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review IAM for least-privilege violations (with read access or via scanner):
   prowler aws -c iam ; scoutsuite aws
   aws iam list-policies / get-account-authorization-details
   Flag: wildcard policies (Action:*, Resource:*), AdministratorAccess handed out broadly, no MFA (esp. root), long-lived access keys never rotated, unused roles/users.
2) PRIVILEGE ESCALATION paths: run pmapper / cloudsplaining to find users who can escalate (iam:PassRole + create-EC2, policy-version, etc.).
3) If you obtain any creds (SSRF/leak): enumerate what they can do:
   aws sts get-caller-identity ; enumerate-iam
4) Check cross-account trust policies and overly-trusting AssumeRole conditions.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce least privilege: scope policies to specific actions/resources (no wildcards), require MFA (especially root), prefer short-lived role credentials over static keys, rotate/remove unused keys and identities, and remove privilege-escalation paths (tighten iam:PassRole/policy edits). Use SCPs/permission boundaries. VERIFY with prowler/pmapper (no over-privilege/escalation).

items.title: Data Encryption
items.description: Ensure that data is encrypted at rest and in transit in the cloud.
items.how_to_find:
1) AT REST: check encryption on storage/DB/volumes/snapshots:
   aws s3api get-bucket-encryption --bucket NAME
   aws ec2 describe-volumes --query 'Volumes[?Encrypted==`false`]'
   aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[?StorageEncrypted==`false`]'
   prowler/scoutsuite flag unencrypted buckets, volumes, snapshots, RDS, and default (vs CMK) keys.
2) IN TRANSIT: confirm TLS on endpoints/load balancers and between services (see TLS Configuration).
3) KEYS: check whether a customer-managed KMS key (CMK) with rotation is used vs provider default, and who can use/administer the key.
4) Look for public unencrypted snapshots/backups.
items.how_to_fix: Enable encryption at rest by default on all buckets/volumes/DBs/snapshots using customer-managed KMS keys with rotation, and enforce TLS in transit (incl. service-to-service). Restrict KMS key policies (least privilege) and block unencrypted resource creation via SCP/policy. VERIFY prowler shows no unencrypted resources.

items.title: Network Security
items.description: Check the security configurations of cloud network components (e.g., security groups, firewalls).
items.how_to_find:
1) Audit security groups / NSGs / firewall rules for overly open ingress:
   aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissions[?contains(IpRanges[].CidrIp, '0.0.0.0/0')]]"
   Flag 0.0.0.0/0 to sensitive ports (22 SSH, 3389 RDP, 3306/5432/6379/27017 DBs, 9200 ES).
2) EXTERNAL scan of public IPs/ranges:
   nmap -sV -Pn <cloud-public-ip>
   Look for exposed admin/DB/management services that should be private.
3) Check network segmentation (public vs private subnets), that databases are in private subnets, and default VPC/SG usage.
4) Verify VPC flow logs and a WAF on public entry points.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-exposure networking: no 0.0.0.0/0 to admin/DB ports (restrict to VPN/bastion/known IPs), keep databases/internal services in private subnets, segment tiers, and remove default-VPC/SG sprawl. Enable flow logs and a WAF on public endpoints. VERIFY with nmap (only intended ports exposed) and SG review.

items.title: Monitoring and Logging
items.description: Verify that cloud monitoring and logging are in place and properly configured.
items.how_to_find:
1) Confirm control-plane audit logging is ON and protected:
   aws cloudtrail describe-trails ; aws cloudtrail get-trail-status
   Flag: CloudTrail disabled, single-region only, log-file validation off, or the log bucket public/mutable.
2) Check for GuardDuty / Security Hub / Azure Defender / GCP SCC enabled and alerting.
3) Verify resource-level logs (S3 access logs, VPC flow logs, LB logs) and that logs go to a locked, separate account/bucket.
4) Run prowler -c logging to surface gaps.
items.how_to_fix: Enable multi-region CloudTrail (or equivalent) with log-file validation, ship logs to a dedicated, locked-down logging account/bucket (MFA-delete/WORM), and enable threat detection (GuardDuty/Defender/SCC) with alerting into your SIEM/IR. Turn on flow/access logs. VERIFY prowler/scoutsuite report full logging coverage.

items.title: Backup and Recovery
items.description: Ensure that backup and recovery processes are secure and regularly tested.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check that backups exist, are encrypted, and are NOT public:
   aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self --query 'Snapshots[].{id:SnapshotId,enc:Encrypted}'
   aws ec2 describe-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id snap-xxx --attribute createVolumePermission   (look for 'all' = public!)
   Public RDS snapshots/AMIs are a common breach source.
2) Verify backups are isolated (separate account/region) to survive ransomware/account compromise, and immutable (MFA-delete/object lock).
3) Confirm restores are actually tested and RTO/RPO defined.
4) Check backup access is least-privilege and logged.
items.how_to_fix: Automate encrypted backups stored in an isolated account/region with immutability (object lock/MFA-delete) so they survive compromise; never make snapshots/AMIs public. Restrict and log access, and regularly test restores against RTO/RPO. VERIFY no public snapshots and that a restore succeeds.

items.title: Serverless Security
items.description: Verify the security configurations of serverless functions.
items.how_to_find:
For Lambda/Functions:
1) Check function IAM roles for over-privilege (least privilege per function) - a compromised function inherits its role:
   aws lambda list-functions ; aws iam get-role-policy ...
2) Secrets in env vars: aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name F --query Environment (secrets should be in Secrets Manager/KMS, not plaintext env).
3) Event-injection: functions triggered by untrusted input (API GW, S3, SQS) - test injection via the event (command/NoSQL/SSRF) since input often reaches shell/db calls.
4) Check for vulnerable dependencies in the deployment package, overly long timeouts, and no concurrency limits (cost/DoS).
5) Look for publicly invocable functions / function URLs without auth.
items.how_to_fix: Give each function a minimal-privilege role, store secrets in a secret manager (not env vars), validate/sanitize all event input, pin and scan dependencies, set sane timeouts/concurrency limits, and require auth on function URLs/API triggers. VERIFY least-privilege roles and that event-injection tests fail.

items.title: Cloud Compliance
items.description: Ensure compliance with relevant regulations and standards in the cloud environment.
items.how_to_find:
1) Run a compliance-mapped scan against the relevant framework:
   prowler aws --compliance cis_2.0_aws   (also pci, hipaa, gdpr, soc2 packs)
   scoutsuite / cloud provider's Security Hub / Compliance Center.
2) Check data residency/region constraints, encryption + logging requirements, and access controls mandated by the standard.
3) Verify config is enforced continuously (drift detection) not just at audit time.
4) Confirm evidence (logs, configs) is retained for audits.
items.how_to_fix: Adopt the applicable compliance pack (CIS/PCI/HIPAA/SOC2/GDPR) as continuous guardrails: enforce encryption, logging, least privilege, and data-residency via IaC + SCPs, use Security Hub/Config rules for continuous compliance and drift detection, and retain audit evidence. VERIFY with a prowler compliance scan (passing controls).

items.title: Vulnerability Management
items.description: Ensure that third-party components are regularly checked for vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP A06: Vulnerable & Outdated Components)
1) Software Composition Analysis on dependencies:
   npm audit / yarn audit ; pip-audit ; osv-scanner -r . ; snyk test ; mvn dependency-check
   Client-side libs: retire.js (or the browser extension) against loaded JS.
2) Fingerprint server/framework/CMS versions and match to CVEs:
   whatweb https://target.tld ; nuclei -u https://target.tld -t cves/  ; wpscan --url https://target.tld (WordPress)
3) Check container/OS packages: trivy image myapp:latest ; grype.
4) Prioritize by exploitability (public PoC/known-exploited) and whether the vulnerable code path is reachable.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an SBOM and run SCA (Dependabot/Snyk/osv-scanner) in CI to fail builds on high/critical CVEs, then patch/upgrade promptly (or apply vendor mitigations). Remove unused dependencies, subscribe to advisories, and scan container images. VERIFY by re-running the scanners (no known-exploitable components).

items.title: License Compliance
items.description: Verify that the use of third-party components complies with their licenses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Generate a license inventory of all dependencies:
   license-checker (npm) ; pip-licenses ; syft . -o table (SBOM with licenses)
2) Flag copyleft/viral licenses (GPL/AGPL) that may conflict with proprietary distribution, plus 'unknown'/missing-license packages (legal + often unmaintained/risk signal).
3) Check transitive dependencies, not just direct ones.
4) Note: this is primarily legal/supply-chain hygiene, but 'unknown/unmaintained' packages often correlate with security risk (typosquats, abandoned code).
items.how_to_fix: Track licenses via an SBOM and enforce a license policy in CI (allow-list acceptable licenses; block/flag GPL/AGPL where they conflict, and unknown licenses). Replace non-compliant or unmaintained packages. VERIFY the license report contains only approved licenses.

items.title: Secure Configuration
items.description: Ensure that third-party components are securely configured.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check third-party components (CMS, DB, cache, admin panels, libraries) for insecure defaults:
   - Default/blank credentials (admin/admin) - try them on any exposed panel.
   - Debug/verbose modes left on, sample/test apps installed, default sample data.
   - Exposed management interfaces (phpMyAdmin, Kibana, Actuator, RabbitMQ, Redis with no auth):
     nmap -sV target ; redis-cli -h target ping ; curl http://target:9200/_cat/indices
2) Compare config to the vendor's hardening/security guide (CIS Benchmark where available).
3) Check unneeded features/modules/plugins are disabled.
items.how_to_fix: Harden each component per its security/CIS guide: change all default credentials, disable debug/sample apps, restrict or remove exposed management interfaces (auth + network controls), require auth on data stores (Redis/ES/Mongo), and disable unused features. VERIFY defaults/exposed panels are no longer accessible.

items.title: Update Management
items.description: Check that third-party components are kept up-to-date with the latest security patches.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify outdated components and how far behind they are:
   npm outdated ; pip list --outdated ; check CMS/plugin versions (wpscan for WordPress).
2) Look for END-OF-LIFE software receiving no security patches (old PHP/Node/OS, jQuery 1.x, AngularJS, EOL frameworks) - these are permanently vulnerable.
3) Check whether a patch process exists (cadence, ownership) or whether versions have been static for years.
4) Cross-reference outdated versions with known CVEs (see Vulnerability Management).
items.how_to_fix: Establish a regular patch cadence with automated update PRs (Dependabot/Renovate), test in CI, and prioritize security releases. Upgrade or replace end-of-life components on a schedule. Track versions in an SBOM. VERIFY nothing is EOL and outdated counts trend to zero.

items.title: Integrity Checks
items.description: Verify the integrity of third-party components before use.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP A08: Software & Data Integrity Failures)
1) Client-side: check third-party <script>/<link> for Subresource Integrity (integrity= + crossorigin). Missing SRI means a compromised CDN can inject code:
   grep -RniE '<script[^>]*src="https?://' dist/ | grep -v integrity
2) Dependencies: are they pinned with a lockfile + hashes (package-lock/poetry.lock/go.sum)? Are they fetched over HTTPS from trusted registries? Any risk of dependency confusion (internal package names resolvable on public registry)?
3) Build/CI: is the pipeline pulling unpinned images/scripts (curl | bash), and are release artifacts signed (cosign/Sigstore)?
4) Verify checksums/signatures of downloaded binaries/packages.
items.how_to_fix: Verify integrity end-to-end: add Subresource Integrity to third-party scripts, pin dependencies with lockfiles + hashes over HTTPS, prevent dependency confusion (scope/private registry, reserve internal names), sign and verify build artifacts/images (Sigstore/cosign), and avoid unpinned 'curl | bash' in CI. VERIFY SRI present and signatures/checksums validate.

items.title: Secure Development Lifecycle
items.description: Ensure that security is integrated into the development lifecycle.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review whether security is built into each SDLC phase or bolted on at the end: threat modeling on design, security requirements/user stories, secure-coding standards, security testing gates, and a defined release/sign-off.
2) Ask for evidence: threat models, past pentest reports and whether findings were fixed, security training records, and a documented SSDLC/security champions.
3) Red flags: security only at the end (or never), no threat modeling, findings that recur release after release, no owner for AppSec.
4) Check that security acceptance criteria block releases (not advisory only).
items.how_to_fix: Adopt a Secure SDLC (align to OWASP SAMM/BSIMM): threat model during design, define security requirements, enforce secure-coding standards, automate SAST/DAST/SCA/secret-scanning in CI as release gates, and track/verify remediation. Provide developer security training and assign AppSec ownership. VERIFY security gates block a deliberately vulnerable build.

items.title: Code Reviews
items.description: Conduct regular code reviews to identify and mitigate security vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check that every change is peer-reviewed before merge: branch protection requiring >=1 approval, no direct pushes to main, and required status checks.
   gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/branches/main/protection   (inspect rules)
2) Verify reviews actually cover security (authz checks, input handling, crypto, secrets) - not just style, and that security-sensitive areas get extra scrutiny.
3) Look for self-approval, bypassed reviews, or force-push to protected branches.
4) Confirm reviewers use a security checklist and that SAST/secret-scan results are part of the PR.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce mandatory peer review with branch protection (require approvals, block direct/force pushes, require CI checks), include security-focused review criteria (authz, validation, crypto, secrets), and surface SAST/secret-scan results in the PR. Require extra review for sensitive code. VERIFY protected-branch rules prevent unreviewed merges.

items.title: Static and Dynamic Analysis
items.description: Use static and dynamic analysis tools to identify security issues in the code.
items.how_to_find:
1) SAST: is static analysis run on every build?
   semgrep --config auto . ; codeql ; sonarqube ; bandit (Python) ; gosec (Go)
2) DAST: is the running app scanned?
   zap-baseline.py -t https://staging.target.tld ; nuclei -u https://staging.target.tld
3) Secret scanning: gitleaks/trufflehog in CI and pre-commit.
4) SCA: dependency scanning (see Vulnerability Management).
5) Check that scan results are triaged and that high/critical findings fail the pipeline (not ignored).
items.how_to_fix: Integrate SAST (Semgrep/CodeQL), DAST (ZAP), SCA, and secret scanning into CI with severity thresholds that fail the build, plus pre-commit hooks for secrets. Triage and track findings to closure and tune rules to cut false positives. VERIFY the pipeline flags a seeded vulnerability/secret.

items.title: Secure CI/CD Pipeline
items.description: Verify that the CI/CD pipeline includes security checks and validations.
items.how_to_fix: Harden CI/CD: store secrets in a managed store (masked, short-lived OIDC creds - not static), pin actions/images to hashes, require review for pipeline changes, least-privilege runner/deploy permissions, isolate/ephemeral runners, protect artifacts (sign with cosign), and never echo secrets. Follow SLSA for build integrity. VERIFY secrets aren't exposed and untrusted PRs can't run privileged jobs.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review pipeline config (.github/workflows, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile) for weaknesses:
   - Secrets hardcoded in the pipeline or printed to logs (echo $TOKEN).
   - Untrusted input running with privileges: pull_request_target + checkout of PR code, or script injection via ${{ github.event.* }} (branch/issue titles).
   - Unpinned third-party actions/images (uses: some/action@main) - supply-chain risk.
   - Over-privileged tokens/runners; self-hosted runners reachable by forks.
2) Check who can edit pipelines and whether changes are reviewed.
3) Look for artifact tampering risk (no signing/provenance) and secrets accessible to PR builds.

items.title: Deployment Security
items.description: Ensure that the deployment process follows secure practices to prevent vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the deployed artifact/config: debug mode off in prod, no source maps/.git exposed (curl https://target.tld/.git/config), no test/admin endpoints shipped, and secrets injected at runtime (not baked into the image).
2) Container hygiene: images run as non-root, minimal base image, no secrets in layers (dive/trivy image), read-only FS where possible.
   trivy image myapp:prod
3) Verify deployments are reproducible from version control (IaC) and rolled out with the ability to roll back, and that only signed/approved artifacts deploy.
4) Check file permissions/ownership and that the deploy account is least-privilege.
items.how_to_fix: Ship hardened artifacts: debug off, no source maps/VCS metadata/test endpoints in prod, runtime-injected secrets, non-root minimal containers scanned for vulns/secrets, immutable/reproducible IaC deploys with rollback, and deploy only signed/approved artifacts via a least-privilege pipeline. VERIFY prod has no debug/.git/secrets and images run as non-root.

items.title: Environment Segregation
items.description: Verify the segregation of development, testing, and production environments.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate non-prod environments that may be exposed and weaker:
   subfinder -d target.tld ; check dev./staging./uat./test./qa. hostnames.
   These often have debug on, default creds, no WAF, or real prod data copies.
2) Test whether non-prod can reach prod data/services (shared DB, shared creds/keys) - a staging compromise then pivots to prod.
3) Check for prod (real customer) data in dev/test environments (privacy + exposure risk).
4) Confirm separate credentials/keys/accounts per environment (no shared secrets).
items.how_to_fix: Fully segregate environments: separate networks/accounts, separate credentials/keys per environment (no sharing), and no prod data in dev/test (use masked/synthetic data). Restrict/authenticate access to non-prod (VPN/allow-list) and ensure non-prod cannot reach prod systems. VERIFY non-prod is isolated and holds no real prod data.

items.title: Incident Response Plan
items.description: Ensure there is an incident response plan in place.
items.how_to_find:
1) Confirm a documented IR plan exists and is current: defined phases (prep, detection, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned - NIST SP 800-61), roles/responsibilities, and an up-to-date contact/escalation list (incl. legal, PR, execs).
2) Check severity classification, decision authority, and communication plan (internal + regulatory/customer breach notification timelines, e.g. GDPR 72h).
3) Ask when it was last tested (tabletop/red-team) and whether staff know it exists.
4) Red flags: no plan, stale contacts, no breach-notification process, plan never exercised.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain a documented, regularly tested IR plan (NIST 800-61) with clear roles, escalation paths, severity classification, and breach-notification procedures meeting legal timelines. Keep contact lists current and run periodic tabletop exercises. VERIFY the plan exists, is known to the team, and was recently exercised.

items.title: Detection and Analysis
items.description: Verify that incidents can be detected and analyzed promptly.
items.how_to_find:
1) Test detection capability by running a controlled attack (brute force, web-shell upload, data exfil pattern) and measuring whether/when it's detected (MTTD).
2) Confirm sufficient telemetry exists to investigate: centralized logs, SIEM correlation, EDR, and retention long enough for forensics (see Logging & Monitoring).
3) Check there's a triage process to distinguish false positives from real incidents and to scope impact quickly.
4) Verify alerts reach an on-call responder with runbooks, not an unmonitored inbox.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure detection coverage (SIEM + EDR + app/security logs with adequate retention), correlation rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and an on-call triage workflow with runbooks to confirm, scope, and prioritize incidents quickly. Regularly test detection with purple-team exercises. VERIFY a simulated attack is detected and triaged within target MTTD.

items.title: Containment and Eradication
items.description: Ensure that procedures for containment and eradication of threats are in place.
items.how_to_find:
1) Verify documented, practiced procedures exist to CONTAIN (isolate hosts, disable accounts, revoke tokens/keys, block IPs, take services offline) without destroying forensic evidence.
2) Check the ability to rotate all secrets/credentials quickly and to revoke sessions org-wide after compromise.
3) Confirm ERADICATION steps (remove malware/web shells/persistence, patch the exploited flaw, rebuild from known-good images) and how root cause is confirmed before recovery.
4) Red flags: no isolation runbook, cannot rotate secrets fast, rebuilds from possibly-compromised images.
items.how_to_fix: Prepare containment/eradication runbooks: host/account isolation, mass secret/session revocation, and evidence preservation; then remove attacker persistence, patch the root-cause vulnerability, and rebuild from known-good, verified images before restoring. VERIFY via tabletop/live drill that containment and secret rotation work quickly.

items.title: Recovery and Post-Incident Activities
items.description: Verify the recovery processes and post-incident review activities.
items.how_to_fix: Define recovery criteria (clean/patched before restore), validate integrity and monitor closely post-restore, and run a blameless post-incident review that produces tracked corrective actions with owners/deadlines. Meet breach-notification obligations. VERIFY tested restores work and a recent post-mortem drove real fixes.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check recovery procedures: verified clean/patched restore from trusted backups, integrity validation, and heightened monitoring after returning to production (attackers often retry).
2) Confirm a post-incident review (blameless post-mortem) process exists that captures root cause, timeline, and CORRECTIVE ACTIONS with owners/deadlines - and that actions are actually tracked to completion.
3) Verify breach-notification/regulatory reporting is handled within required timelines.
4) Red flags: restore without confirming eradication, no post-mortem, lessons never turned into fixes.

items.title: 2FA response manipulation
items.description: Server trusts client-side success flag in JSON response after OTP step.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in with valid credentials to reach the OTP/2FA step, and submit a WRONG OTP with Burp intercepting the RESPONSE.
2) The OTP-verify response often contains a flag the client uses to decide success, e.g. {"success":false,"verified":false}.
3) Edit the response on the fly to {"success":true,"verified":true} (or 2FA:1, status:'ok') and forward it.
4) If you land authenticated, the server trusted the client to enforce 2FA - critical bypass. Also try this on password-reset/step-up flows.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce the 2FA decision entirely server-side: the OTP must be validated against server state and the authenticated session only issued after that server-side check passes - never trust a client-readable success flag. Rate-limit and expire OTPs, and bind them to the pending-login session. VERIFY response tampering no longer grants access.

items.title: 2FA status code manipulation
items.description: Application treats HTTP 200 as auth success regardless of body content.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit an incorrect OTP and observe the verify response's STATUS CODE (often 4xx for failure) while the client keys success off the status code.
2) In Burp, intercept the failing response and change the status line from e.g. 401/400 to 200 OK (keep/blank the body), then forward.
3) If the client proceeds to the authenticated area, the app relies on status code alone - bypass.
4) Also try the inverse tricks: force a 302 to the post-login page, or replay a previously-seen 200 verify response.
items.how_to_fix: Make authentication depend on a server-validated OTP and a server-issued session token, not on the HTTP status code the client sees. Return access only after the server confirms the OTP; the client must not infer auth from 200/302. VERIFY that forging the status code does not authenticate.

items.title: CAPTCHA token reuse
items.description: Same CAPTCHA response token accepted multiple times.
items.how_to_find:
1) Solve the CAPTCHA once and capture the request containing the CAPTCHA token (e.g. g-recaptcha-response / h-captcha-response).
2) In Burp Repeater, resend that SAME token on many subsequent requests (login/register/contact).
3) If repeated requests succeed with the one token, the CAPTCHA isn't invalidated after use -> attacker solves once and automates brute force/spam.
4) Also test: is the token verified server-side at all? (drop it and see) and does an old/expired token still work?
items.how_to_fix: Verify the CAPTCHA token server-side with the provider on every submission and mark it single-use (reject reused/expired tokens); tie it to the specific action/session. Combine with server-side rate limiting so a solved CAPTCHA can't be replayed. VERIFY the same token is rejected on the second use.

items.title: CAPTCHA disabled via parameter removal
items.description: Backend skips CAPTCHA validation when parameter omitted.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture a request that includes the CAPTCHA parameter (e.g. g-recaptcha-response=...).
2) In Burp Repeater, DELETE the CAPTCHA parameter entirely (not just blank it) and resend.
3) If the request still succeeds, the backend only validates the CAPTCHA when the field is present - so omitting it disables the check, enabling automated brute force/spam.
4) Variations: send empty value, null, an array, or a bogus value; try alternate content-types.
items.how_to_fix: Require and validate the CAPTCHA server-side for every protected action - treat a missing/empty/invalid token as failure (deny by default), not as a skip. Don't gate the check on parameter presence. Back it with server-side rate limiting. VERIFY that removing/emptying the parameter blocks the request.

items.title: Identify web server and version from banners
items.description: Web servers and reverse proxies often leak product and version via Server, X-Powered-By, and Via response headers, narrowing the vulnerability search.
items.how_to_find:
1) Read response headers for product/version banners:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld | grep -iE 'server|x-powered-by|via|x-aspnet-version|x-runtime'
2) Automate fingerprinting:
   whatweb https://target.tld ; nmap -sV -p80,443 target.tld ; wafw00f https://target.tld
3) Diff error pages / default pages across paths, and check non-standard ports.
4) Map the exact version to public CVEs (searchsploit / nuclei -t cves/) to narrow attacks.
items.how_to_fix: Suppress version banners: remove/override Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, X-Runtime, Via (e.g. server_tokens off; expose_php Off; ServerTokens Prod). Present generic error pages and keep components patched (obscurity is not a fix, just reduces easy targeting). VERIFY headers no longer leak versions.

items.title: Detect the application framework
items.description: Frameworks reveal themselves through cookie names, default routes, template syntax, and characteristic headers, guiding framework-specific attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fingerprint the framework from tell-tale signs: cookie names (PHPSESSID, JSESSIONID, laravel_session, csrftoken/Django, _rails), default routes/paths, error-page style, and headers (X-Powered-By, Set-Cookie).
   whatweb -a 3 https://target.tld ; wappalyzer / the browser extension.
2) Probe framework-specific artifacts: /actuator (Spring), /_next (Next.js), /__debug__ (Django debug), default admin paths.
3) Trigger errors to reveal stack traces naming the framework/version.
4) Use the framework to pick targeted attacks (e.g. SSTI syntax, deserialization, known CVEs).
items.how_to_fix: Reduce framework fingerprinting: rename/standardize session cookies, remove framework headers, disable debug/verbose errors and default routes in prod, and remove sample/debug endpoints. Keep the framework patched. VERIFY the obvious framework tells (cookies/headers/debug routes) are gone.

items.title: Detect CMS and its version/plugins
items.description: CMS platforms such as WordPress, Drupal, and Joomla expose version and plugin data that maps directly to public exploits.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify the CMS and enumerate version/plugins/themes/users:
   WordPress: wpscan --url https://target.tld --enumerate vp,vt,u
   Generic: whatweb https://target.tld ; droopescan scan drupal -u https://target.tld ; joomscan -u https://target.tld
2) Check version markers: /readme.html, meta generator tag, /wp-content/plugins/*/readme.txt, changelog files, and CSS/JS ?ver= query strings.
3) Map plugin/theme versions to public exploits (wpscan uses a vuln DB; also searchsploit).
4) Look for exposed admin (/wp-admin, /administrator) and xmlrpc.php.
items.how_to_fix: Keep CMS core + all plugins/themes patched (and remove unused ones), hide version markers (remove generator meta, block readme/changelog, strip ?ver=), restrict/limit admin login (auth, IP allow-list, disable xmlrpc if unused), and enforce strong admin creds + 2FA. VERIFY wpscan no longer reports known-vulnerable versions and version markers are gone.

items.title: Find version disclosure in errors and pages
items.description: Verbose error pages, comments, and metadata frequently leak exact component versions even when headers are stripped.
items.how_to_find:
1) Force and read errors that leak versions: request bad routes/params, wrong methods, and malformed input to trigger stack traces / debug pages naming exact component versions.
2) Grep client-side sources and metadata:
   curl -s https://target.tld | grep -iE 'generator|version|build|@version'
   Check HTML comments, JS bundles/source maps, /package.json, /composer.lock, ?ver= asset strings.
3) Look at default/verbose pages (phpinfo.php, /server-status, framework debug pages).
4) Map leaked versions to CVEs.
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug/verbose errors in prod (generic error pages), strip version metadata (generator tags, comments, ?ver=, source maps) from responses, and remove diagnostic endpoints (phpinfo, server-status). Keep components patched. VERIFY errors and page metadata no longer disclose versions.

items.title: Detect client-side JS frameworks
items.description: SPA frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue change the attack surface (DOM-based issues, client routing) and reveal build tooling.
items.how_to_find:
1) Detect SPA frameworks (React/Angular/Vue) via global objects, bundle names, and DOM attributes:
   - DevTools console: window.React, window.ng, window.Vue; look for data-reactroot, ng-version attribute, __NEXT_DATA__.
   - retire.js / Wappalyzer on loaded scripts to get versions.
2) Grab source maps (.js.map) if present to recover original source and routes:
   curl -s https://target.tld/static/js/main.js.map -o main.js.map
3) Note the changed attack surface: DOM-based XSS sinks, client-side routing/authz, and secrets accidentally bundled.
4) Match framework/lib versions to known CVEs.
items.how_to_fix: Fingerprinting of client frameworks can't be fully prevented (code runs in the browser), so focus on the real risks: don't ship source maps to prod, keep client libs patched (retire.js clean), never put secrets/authz logic solely client-side, and mitigate DOM XSS (safe sinks + CSP). VERIFY no source maps in prod and no vulnerable client libraries.

items.title: Inventory third-party components and CDNs
items.description: Loaded libraries, analytics, CDNs, and SaaS widgets expand the supply-chain attack surface and may carry known vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inventory every externally loaded resource (scripts, CSS, fonts, analytics, widgets, CDNs):
   - Burp/DevTools Network tab -> list all third-party origins.
   - Grep the DOM: curl -s https://target.tld | grep -oiE 'src="https?://[^"]+' | sort -u
2) Version and vuln-check each library: retire.js against loaded JS.
3) Check integrity: are third-party <script> tags using SRI? (missing SRI = CDN compromise -> XSS on your site).
4) Note supply-chain risk: abandoned CDNs, expired domains, and trackers with broad DOM access.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an inventory/SBOM of third-party front-end components, remove unneeded ones, keep them patched, add Subresource Integrity (+ CSP) to third-party scripts, and self-host or pin critical libraries to trusted CDNs. Monitor for compromised/abandoned dependencies. VERIFY retire.js is clean and SRI is present on external scripts.

items.title: Brute force directories and files
items.description: Unlinked directories and files often expose admin panels, debug tools, and sensitive content not intended to be public.
items.how_to_find:
1) Content-discovery brute force with a good wordlist:
   ffuf -u https://target.tld/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt -mc 200,204,301,302,307,401,403 -recursion
   gobuster dir -u https://target.tld -w wordlist.txt -x php,bak,zip,txt,old
   feroxbuster -u https://target.tld
2) Look for admin panels, debug/dev tools, /backup, /old, /test, config files, and status endpoints.
3) Note 403s (exists but blocked) - worth authz/bypass testing; and 200s that shouldn't be public.
4) Cross-check found paths against auth requirements.
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on obscurity: put sensitive tools/panels behind authentication + authorization (and IP allow-list where possible), remove unused/dev/test/backup files from web roots, return consistent 404 for non-public paths, and add rate limiting/WAF to slow enumeration. VERIFY discovered sensitive paths now require auth or 404.

items.title: Hunt for backup and temporary files
items.description: Editors and deploys leave .bak, .old, ~, and .swp copies of source and config that can disclose code and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fuzz for editor/deploy leftovers of known files (index.php, config, app.js):
   ffuf -u https://target.tld/FUZZ -w list -e .bak,.old,.orig,.save,.swp,.tmp,~,.zip,.tar.gz,.copy
   Try patterns: config.php.bak, .env.bak, index.php~, app.js.old, database.sql, backup.zip, web.config.bak.
2) Also probe common archive names: backup.zip, site.tar.gz, db_backup.sql, dump.sql.
3) Download any hits and inspect for source code, DB creds, API keys, and secrets.
4) Check editor swap files (.swp/.swo) which can reveal source.
items.how_to_fix: Never leave backup/temp/source copies in web-accessible roots: exclude them from deploys, block these extensions at the web server (deny .bak/.old/.swp/.sql/.zip etc.), store real backups outside the docroot, and scan releases for stray files. Rotate any secrets exposed this way. VERIFY the backup/temp URLs now 404 and contain no secrets.

items.title: Discover hidden parameters
items.description: Undocumented GET/POST parameters can unlock debug behavior, mass assignment, and access-control bypasses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Brute force undocumented GET/POST/JSON params:
   arjun -u https://target.tld/endpoint ; ffuf -u 'https://target.tld/page?FUZZ=test' -w params.txt -fs <baseline-size>
   x8 -u https://target.tld -w params.txt
2) Mine JS files, HTML comments, and old/archived URLs for parameter names.
3) Test discovered params for effect: debug=true, admin=1, test=1, isAdmin, role, redirect, id (IDOR), file (LFI), and mass-assignment fields.
4) A change in response size/behavior signals the param is processed.
items.how_to_fix: Explicitly define and validate expected parameters server-side (allow-list/schema); ignore unknown params, disable debug/admin toggles in prod, and guard sensitive fields against mass assignment (bind only permitted fields). Enforce authz on any param that changes behavior. VERIFY hidden params like debug/admin no longer alter behavior.

items.title: Discover API endpoints
items.description: API routes referenced in JS, swagger files, or predictable patterns expose backend functionality outside the visible UI.
items.how_to_find:
1) Extract API routes from client code and specs:
   - Grep JS bundles for /api/, fetch(, axios, URL literals (LinkFinder, katana, gau).
   - Look for swagger/openapi: /swagger.json, /openapi.json, /api-docs, /graphql (introspection).
2) Fuzz API paths and versions: ffuf -u https://target.tld/api/vFUZZ/users -w numbers ; try /api, /api/v1, /v2, /internal.
3) Import findings into Postman/Burp and enumerate methods (OPTIONS) and undocumented actions.
4) Test each endpoint for authz (see BOLA/BFLA) - hidden != protected.
items.how_to_fix: Treat every API endpoint as public-reachable: enforce authentication + object/function-level authorization on all of them (documented or not), disable GraphQL introspection and public swagger in prod, remove dead/internal endpoints from exposed builds, and apply rate limiting. VERIFY discovered endpoints require proper authz.

items.title: Enumerate virtual hosts
items.description: A single IP may serve multiple sites by Host header; undiscovered vhosts can expose staging or internal apps.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fuzz the Host header against the target IP to find name-based vhosts:
   ffuf -u http://TARGET_IP/ -H 'Host: FUZZ.target.tld' -w subdomains.txt -fs <baseline>
   gobuster vhost -u http://TARGET_IP --domain target.tld -w list
2) Also enumerate DNS subdomains (subfinder, amass) and resolve to the same IP.
3) Look for staging./dev./internal./admin. vhosts that expose weaker or internal apps.
4) Confirm each responds differently (size/title) than the default vhost.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict virtual-host exposure: serve only intended hostnames (reject unknown Host with a default deny/404), keep internal/staging vhosts off public interfaces (VPN/allow-list/separate infra), and ensure the default vhost doesn't reveal other apps. VERIFY unknown/internal Host values don't reach sensitive apps.

items.title: Collect archived and historical URLs
items.description: Wayback Machine and URL archives reveal deprecated endpoints, parameters, and pages still reachable on the live host.
items.how_to_find:
1) Pull historical URLs from public archives:
   gau target.tld ; waybackurls target.tld ; curl 'http://web.archive.org/cdx/search/cdx?url=target.tld/*&output=text&fl=original&collapse=urlkey'
2) Filter for interesting old endpoints/params: grep -Ei 'admin|api|token|key|debug|\.(bak|sql|zip|env)'
3) Replay each against the LIVE host - deprecated pages/params are often still reachable and unpatched (old vuln endpoints, leaked tokens in query strings).
4) Feed archived params into hidden-parameter and injection testing.
items.how_to_fix: Decommission deprecated endpoints properly (remove or 410/redirect, don't just unlink them), rotate any secrets that ever appeared in archived URLs/query strings, and stop putting sensitive data in URLs. Ensure old routes enforce current authz. VERIFY archived endpoints are gone or protected on the live host.

items.title: Review robots.txt for disallowed paths
items.description: robots.txt frequently lists admin and sensitive paths the owner wants hidden from crawlers — a free directory map.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fetch and read the file: curl -s https://target.tld/robots.txt
2) Every Disallow: entry is a path the owner wants hidden - treat it as a to-visit list (admin panels, /backup, /api, /internal, /old).
3) Manually browse each disallowed path and check whether it's actually protected (auth) or just unlinked.
4) Compare against sitemap.xml and archived URLs for more paths.
items.how_to_fix: robots.txt is not access control - never list sensitive paths there (it advertises them). Protect those resources with real authentication/authorization instead, and only use robots.txt for genuine crawler hints. VERIFY the previously-listed paths require auth even when requested directly.

items.title: Parse sitemap.xml for endpoints
items.description: Sitemaps enumerate intended URLs, surfacing pages and sections that may not be linked in the main navigation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fetch sitemap(s): curl -s https://target.tld/sitemap.xml (also sitemap_index.xml, /sitemap/, and any listed in robots.txt).
2) Extract all URLs: curl -s https://target.tld/sitemap.xml | grep -oE '<loc>[^<]+' | sed 's/<loc>//'
3) Add these to your crawl - they surface pages/sections not in the nav, plus parameterized URLs to test.
4) Look for stale/deprecated URLs and admin/section pages.
items.how_to_fix: Only include public, intended URLs in the sitemap; never list admin/internal/deprecated endpoints. Protect sensitive pages with authorization regardless of sitemap contents, and remove dead URLs. VERIFY the sitemap exposes no sensitive routes and any sensitive page requires auth.

items.title: Check .well-known and security.txt
items.description: The .well-known directory and security.txt expose policy, contacts, and provider configuration useful for scoping.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate the .well-known directory:
   curl -s https://target.tld/.well-known/security.txt
   Also check: /.well-known/openid-configuration (OIDC endpoints/scopes), /.well-known/assetlinks.json, /apple-app-site-association, /.well-known/change-password, /.well-known/mta-sts.txt.
2) security.txt gives contact/policy/scope (useful for responsible disclosure and program scope).
3) openid-configuration reveals auth/token endpoints, supported flows, and JWKS URL - feeds OAuth/OIDC/JWT testing.
4) Note any provider/config details that expand the attack surface.
items.how_to_fix: Publish a helpful security.txt (contact, policy, expiry) but ensure .well-known files reveal only intended config. Don't expose internal endpoints via well-known documents beyond what the protocol requires, and secure the referenced services. VERIFY .well-known contents disclose nothing sensitive beyond required protocol metadata.

items.title: Detect exposed .git/.svn directories
items.description: A deployed .git or .svn folder lets an attacker reconstruct full source code and recover committed secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Probe for exposed VCS metadata:
   curl -s https://target.tld/.git/config ; curl -s https://target.tld/.git/HEAD ; curl -s https://target.tld/.svn/entries
2) If /.git/ is reachable, dump the whole repo and recover source + history:
   git-dumper https://target.tld/.git/ ./loot   (or GitTools/Dumper)
3) Mine recovered history for secrets: gitleaks detect --source ./loot ; git log -p | grep -Ei 'password|secret|api[_-]?key|token'
4) Recovered source enables whitebox review of other vulns.
items.how_to_fix: Never deploy VCS directories to web roots: exclude .git/.svn from builds/deploys and block them at the web server (deny /.git, /.svn -> 404). Rotate every credential/secret found in the exposed history. VERIFY /.git/config and /.svn/entries return 404 and no repo can be dumped.

items.title: Find exposed .env and config files
items.description: Web-accessible .env, config.php, web.config, and similar files frequently leak database creds, API keys, and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request common config/secret files directly:
   for f in .env .env.local .env.backup config.php config.php.bak web.config appsettings.json wp-config.php.bak docker-compose.yml .aws/credentials; do echo $f; curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://target.tld/$f; done
2) Any 200 with real contents = leak; open and harvest DB creds, API keys, SMTP, JWT secrets, cloud keys.
3) Also fuzz with a config wordlist (ffuf) and check archived URLs.
4) Validate/abuse any live keys found (in scope) to prove impact.
items.how_to_fix: Keep config/secret files outside the web-accessible root, block their extensions/names at the server (deny .env, *.bak, config files), and load secrets from env vars/secret manager - never commit them. Rotate any exposed secrets immediately. VERIFY the config URLs return 404/403 and hold no live secrets.

items.title: Detect exposed JavaScript source maps
items.description: Published .map files reconstruct original, unminified source — revealing comments, internal paths, and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) For each loaded bundle, request its map: curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://target.tld/static/js/main.js.map (or read the //# sourceMappingURL comment at the end of the JS).
2) If present, reconstruct original source:
   npx source-map-explorer / unwebpack-sourcemap main.js.map -o ./src
3) Review recovered source for comments, internal paths/hostnames, hardcoded secrets, feature flags, and client-side authz logic that reveals server behavior.
4) Feed discovered endpoints/secrets into further testing.
items.how_to_fix: Don't publish source maps to production (disable/upload them privately to your error-tracking tool only), remove sourceMappingURL comments from prod bundles, and never embed secrets in client code. Rotate any leaked secrets. VERIFY .map files return 404 in prod.

items.title: Search JS bundles for secrets
items.description: Frontend bundles often contain hardcoded credentials, tokens, and internal hostnames committed by mistake.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect all JS the app loads (DevTools Network, or katana/gau to list .js URLs), then download them.
2) Grep for secrets/patterns:
   grep -rEi 'api[_-]?key|secret|token|password|authorization|bearer|aws_access|AKIA|-----BEGIN|firebase|apikey' ./js/
   Automate: trufflehog filesystem ./js ; nuclei -t exposures/ ; SecretFinder.py -i main.js -o cli
3) Also look for internal hostnames, staging URLs, and debug flags.
4) Validate any live secret (in scope) to confirm impact.
items.how_to_fix: Never ship secrets in client-side code - anything in the bundle is public. Move secrets to the server, use short-lived per-user tokens for the browser, and add secret scanning (gitleaks/trufflehog) to CI to block commits. Rotate every exposed secret. VERIFY bundles contain no credentials and scanners are clean.

items.title: Extract and validate API keys from JS
items.description: Cloud and third-party API keys embedded in JS may be live and abusable, or scoped too broadly.
items.how_to_find:
1) Extract API keys from JS (Google Maps, Firebase, Stripe pk/sk, AWS, SendGrid, etc.) via grep/SecretFinder.
2) Validate whether each key is live and over-scoped:
   - Google Maps: try the key against the various Maps APIs (keyhacks recipes) to test for unrestricted billing abuse.
   - AWS AKIA: aws sts get-caller-identity with the creds.
   - Use the 'keyhacks' repo for per-provider validation commands.
3) Note keys that lack HTTP-referrer/IP/scope restrictions - those are abusable.
4) Distinguish publishable keys (OK client-side) from secret keys (must never be there).
items.how_to_fix: Only put publishable/restricted keys in the browser; keep secret keys server-side. Apply provider restrictions (HTTP referrer/IP allow-list, minimal API scopes, quotas) to any client key. Rotate leaked/secret keys immediately and monitor usage/billing. VERIFY no secret keys are client-side and client keys are scope/referrer-restricted.

items.title: Extract endpoints and routes from JS
items.description: JavaScript references the full set of backend routes the app uses, including ones never reached via normal navigation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Parse JS for routes/paths/API calls:
   LinkFinder -i https://target.tld/main.js -o cli ; katana -jc -u https://target.tld ; grep -oE '"/[a-zA-Z0-9_/-]+"' main.js
2) Build the full endpoint list including admin/internal/dev routes never linked in the UI.
3) Hit each endpoint directly (Burp) and test for missing authz, IDOR, injection - JS knowing a route doesn't mean it's protected.
4) Note API base URLs, versions, and parameter names for further fuzzing.
items.how_to_fix: Assume all routes referenced in JS are attacker-known: enforce authentication/authorization server-side on every one (including admin/internal/dev), remove dead or privileged routes from public bundles, and don't rely on the UI hiding them. VERIFY each JS-discovered endpoint enforces proper access control.

items.title: Identify dangerous DOM sinks
items.description: Sinks like innerHTML, eval, and document.write fed by attacker-controllable sources enable DOM-based XSS.
items.how_to_find: 1) Grep JS for dangerous sinks and trace them back to sources: grep -nE 'innerHTML|outerHTML|document.write|eval\(|setTimeout\(|\.html\(|insertAdjacentHTML|src=|location|dangerouslySetInnerHTML' *.js 2) Check whether user-controllable SOURCES (location.hash/search, document.referrer, postMessage, URL params, localStorage) flow into those sinks without sanitization. 3) Use DevTools breakpoints / Burp DOM Invader to auto-trace source-to-sink and confirm DOM XSS. 4) PoC: put a payload in the source (e.g. #) and see it execute.
items.how_to_fix: Eliminate DOM XSS: avoid dangerous sinks, render untrusted data with safe APIs (textContent, not innerHTML), sanitize with DOMPurify when HTML is required, use framework auto-escaping, enable Trusted Types, and add a strict CSP as defense-in-depth. VERIFY the source-to-sink payload no longer executes.

items.title: Flag vulnerable JS dependencies
items.description: Outdated client libraries (jQuery, lodash, Angular) carry known CVEs exploitable from the browser.
items.how_to_fix: Upgrade vulnerable client libraries to patched versions (remove unused ones), pin with a lockfile, and add SCA + retire.js to CI to block known-vulnerable dependencies. Add SRI/CSP as defense-in-depth. VERIFY retire.js/SCA report no known-vulnerable client libraries.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify client libraries and versions from loaded JS:
   retire.js (or the browser extension) ; the Wappalyzer/whatweb output ; check filenames/version comments (jquery-1.12.4.min.js).
2) Map each version to known CVEs (retire.js flags them; also Snyk DB / searchsploit) - e.g. old jQuery XSS, lodash prototype pollution, AngularJS sandbox escapes.
3) Confirm the vulnerable code path is actually usable in the app before rating impact.
4) Note EOL libs (AngularJS, jQuery 1.x) that will never be patched.

items.title: Enumerate subdomains passively
items.description: Passive sources (CT logs, scrapers, APIs) reveal subdomains without touching the target, widening the attack surface.
items.how_to_find:
1) Pull subdomains from passive sources (no traffic to target):
   subfinder -d target.tld -all -silent ; amass enum -passive -d target.tld ; assetfinder --subs-only target.tld
   Also query crt.sh, SecurityTrails, VirusTotal, and Shodan.
2) Consolidate + dedupe, then resolve live hosts: dnsx -silent < subs.txt ; httpx -silent < subs.txt
3) Prioritize dev/staging/uat/admin/vpn/internal-looking hosts - often weaker.
4) Screenshot with gowitness/aquatone to triage quickly.
items.how_to_fix: You can't remove public OSINT, so reduce exposed attack surface instead: decommission unused subdomains, keep non-prod hosts off the public internet (VPN/allow-list), apply the same hardening/auth to every subdomain, and remove dangling DNS records to prevent takeover. VERIFY discovered hosts are either intended-public-and-hardened or unreachable.

items.title: Brute force subdomains actively
items.description: DNS brute forcing and permutation find hosts that passive sources miss, including internal-naming conventions.
items.how_to_find:
1) DNS brute force with a good wordlist + fast resolvers:
   puredns bruteforce subdomains-top1m.txt target.tld -r resolvers.txt
   shuffledns -d target.tld -w list.txt -r resolvers.txt ; gobuster dns -d target.tld -w list.txt
2) Generate permutations of known subs and resolve them: dnsgen subs.txt | dnsx -silent (or altdns/gotator).
3) Test for zone transfer: dig AXFR target.tld @ns1.target.tld.
4) Probe found hosts with httpx and screenshot for triage.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DNS zone transfers to untrusted resolvers, avoid predictable internal naming on public DNS, keep internal hosts on split-horizon/private DNS, and decommission stale records. Ensure every resolvable host is hardened and dangling records are removed. VERIFY AXFR is refused and brute-forced hosts are hardened or non-public.

items.title: Mine certificate transparency logs
items.description: CT logs record every issued certificate, exposing hostnames including staging and internal subdomains.
items.how_to_find:
1) Query CT logs for every hostname a cert was issued for:
   curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%25.target.tld&output=json' | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u
   Also: subfinder (uses CT), and https://ui.ctsearch.entrust.com / censys.
2) CT reveals internal/staging names even if DNS is private (cert was still logged) - e.g. jenkins.internal.target.tld.
3) Resolve and probe the discovered hosts (dnsx/httpx).
4) Note wildcard certs and pre-launch/new hosts.
items.how_to_fix: CT is public and mandatory, so don't rely on it staying secret: use wildcard certs for internal names (so hostnames aren't logged individually) where appropriate, keep internal hosts off public networks, harden every host that appears in CT, and monitor CT logs for unexpected certs. VERIFY CT-discovered internal hosts aren't publicly reachable/unprotected.

items.title: Discover cloud storage and assets
items.description: Public buckets, blobs, and cloud endpoints tied to the org can leak data or be writable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find cloud buckets/blobs tied to the org (guess names from company/app/domain):
   S3: cloud_enum -k target ; check https://<name>.s3.amazonaws.com and try aws s3 ls s3://<name> --no-sign-request
   Azure blobs, GCP storage: same via cloud_enum; also grep found names from JS/CT.
2) For each bucket test READ (list/download) and WRITE (upload a harmless file) with anonymous creds.
3) Public read = data leak; public write = defacement/malware hosting/supply-chain.
4) Note buckets referenced by the app (in JS/HTML) and check their ACLs.
items.how_to_fix: Make cloud storage private by default: block public access at the account level, remove anonymous/AllUsers ACLs and overly-broad bucket policies, require authentication/signed URLs, and enable access logging. Inventory and audit all buckets. VERIFY anonymous read AND write are denied on every bucket.

items.title: Enumerate DNS records
items.description: A, AAAA, MX, TXT, NS, and SRV records reveal mail providers, third-party services, and infrastructure clues.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate record types:
   dig ANY target.tld ; dig MX target.tld ; dig TXT target.tld ; dig NS target.tld ; dnsrecon -d target.tld
2) Read the data: MX (mail provider), TXT/SPF/DKIM/DMARC (email posture, third-party SaaS verification tokens), NS (DNS provider), SRV/CNAME (services, CDN, SaaS).
3) CNAMEs pointing to unclaimed SaaS = subdomain-takeover candidates.
4) SPF/DMARC gaps = email spoofing risk.
items.how_to_fix: Publish only necessary DNS records, remove stale CNAMEs pointing at decommissioned services (takeover risk), and harden email DNS (strict SPF -all, DKIM, DMARC p=reject). Avoid leaking internal infrastructure hints in TXT records. VERIFY no dangling CNAMEs and email records enforce anti-spoofing.

sections.title: Information Gathering — WAF & Defensive Tech Detection

items.title: Identify the WAF in use
items.description: Knowing the specific WAF lets you anticipate filtering rules and select known bypass techniques.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fingerprint the WAF:
   wafw00f https://target.tld ; nmap --script http-waf-detect,http-waf-fingerprint -p443 target.tld
2) Send a benign malicious-looking probe and watch the block signature: curl 'https://target.tld/?x=<script>alert(1)</script>' - note the block page, headers (Server, cf-ray, X-Sucuri-ID, X-Akamai), cookies, and status (403/406/429/501).
3) Match signatures to the product (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS WAF, Imperva, F5, ModSecurity).
4) Knowing the WAF guides tamper/bypass technique selection later.
items.how_to_fix: A WAF is defense-in-depth, not a fix: don't let it leak its identity unnecessarily (custom generic block pages, strip vendor headers), keep rules tuned/updated, and - critically - fix the underlying app vulnerabilities so the WAF isn't the only barrier. VERIFY the app is secure even assuming the WAF is fingerprinted/bypassed.

items.title: Map rate-limit and IP-block behavior
items.description: Understanding throttle thresholds and ban triggers prevents lockouts and informs request pacing during testing.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send a controlled burst to a cheap endpoint and find the threshold/window:
   for i in $(seq 1 200); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://target.tld/login; done | sort | uniq -c
2) Note when responses flip to 429/403, whether a Retry-After header appears, and how long bans last.
3) Test the limit's scope/bypass: rotate X-Forwarded-For/X-Real-IP, change casing/paths, use HTTP/2, or distribute across IPs to see if the limit is per-IP and spoofable.
4) Record safe pacing so later testing doesn't trigger lockouts.
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit sensitive actions (login/OTP/reset/API) server-side keyed on account + a trustworthy client identifier (not a spoofable header); return 429 with backoff, and add lockout/anomaly alerts. Don't derive the limit from client-controlled IP headers. VERIFY the limit can't be bypassed via X-Forwarded-For or path/case tricks.

items.title: Fingerprint error pages and block responses
items.description: Distinct error and block pages distinguish app errors from WAF blocks and can leak backend product details.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger different failure classes and compare responses: 404 (missing), 500 (app error), and WAF block (send a payload like ?q=<script> or ' OR 1=1).
2) Diff status codes, bodies, headers, and timing to tell an APP error from a WAF BLOCK - block pages often carry vendor markers (cf-ray, Sucuri, Incapsula) and support/ray IDs.
3) Note whether 500s leak stack traces/versions (info disclosure) vs generic pages.
4) Use the distinction to know when you're hitting the WAF vs the app during later injection testing.
items.how_to_fix: Return consistent, generic error/block pages that reveal no backend product, version, stack trace, or internal path; log details server-side only. Make WAF block pages non-identifying. Keep debug off in prod. VERIFY error and block responses disclose no technology or sensitive details.

items.title: Map origin exposure behind the WAF
items.description: If the origin IP is reachable directly, the WAF can be bypassed entirely, nullifying its protections.
items.how_to_find:
1) Try to find the real origin IP behind the CDN/WAF:
   - Historical DNS/IP: securitytrails, viewdns, shodan 'ssl.cert.subject.cn:target.tld', censys.
   - CT + direct connect: resolve non-proxied subdomains (mail., ftp., direct., origin.), and check SPF/MX for infra IPs.
   - favicon hash / page-title pivot on Shodan/Censys.
2) Once you have a candidate IP, connect directly with the correct Host header: curl -k -H 'Host: target.tld' https://ORIGIN_IP/ and see if you get the same app WITHOUT the WAF.
3) If yes, the WAF is fully bypassable at the origin.
items.how_to_fix: Lock the origin so it only accepts traffic from the CDN/WAF: firewall/allow-list the CDN IP ranges (or use authenticated origin pull / mTLS / a shared secret header), rotate the origin IP after it leaked, and don't expose the origin via non-proxied DNS records. VERIFY direct-to-origin requests are refused.

items.title: Detect CDN and reverse-proxy layers
items.description: CDNs and proxies shape caching, header handling, and routing — relevant to cache poisoning and request smuggling later.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify CDN/proxy layers from headers and DNS:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld | grep -iE 'server|via|x-cache|cf-ray|x-served-by|x-amz-cf-id|akamai|fastly|age'
   dig +short target.tld (CNAME to cloudfront/fastly/akamai/cloudflare).
2) Note caching behavior (X-Cache HIT/MISS, Age) and multiple proxy hops (Via) - relevant to cache poisoning and request smuggling.
3) Probe how the front-end vs back-end parse ambiguous headers (feeds smuggling tests).
4) Map which layer terminates TLS and normalizes requests.
items.how_to_fix: Keep CDN/proxy and origin request-parsing consistent (mitigates request smuggling), define an explicit cache key that excludes unkeyed user-influenced inputs (mitigates cache poisoning), don't cache authenticated/personalized responses, and keep proxy software patched. VERIFY parsing is consistent across hops and sensitive responses aren't cached.

items.title: Assess password strength requirements
items.description: Weak policies undermine every downstream control.
items.how_to_find:
1) At signup/change-password, intercept the request in Burp and try weak values: '123456', 'password', a single char 'a', and the username/email itself.
2) Bypass client-side JS validation by sending the request straight to the server (Repeater) - confirm the SERVER, not just the browser, enforces the rule.
3) Test min length, and whether extremely long passwords (>128) are accepted/handled.
4) A weak/short/common password being accepted server-side is the finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce password policy server-side per NIST 800-63B: minimum 8+ chars (12+ recommended), allow long passphrases and all characters/spaces, block common/breached passwords, and don't impose forced-rotation or silly composition rules that push users to weak patterns. VERIFY weak/common passwords are rejected when posted directly to the server.

items.title: Test for breached-password rejection
items.description: Reusing known-breached passwords is the top credential-stuffing enabler.
items.how_to_find:
1) At registration/change-password, try a well-known breached password (e.g. 'Password1!', 'qwerty123') that still meets complexity rules.
2) If it's accepted, the app doesn't check against breach corpora - the top enabler of credential stuffing.
3) Confirm server-side (send via Repeater, not just the UI).
4) Note whether the app warns on known-breached passwords at all.
items.how_to_fix: Screen new/changed passwords against a breached-password list (e.g. Have I Been Pwned's k-anonymity range API, or a local Pwned Passwords dataset) and reject matches with clear guidance. Combine with rate limiting and MFA to blunt credential stuffing. VERIFY a known-breached password is rejected.

items.title: Check for silent password truncation
items.description: Servers that truncate passwords silently weaken entropy without telling the user.
items.how_to_find:
1) Register with a long password, e.g. 'A'x30 + 'CorrectTail'.
2) Then try to LOG IN with just the first N characters (e.g. 'A'x20) or the long value minus the tail.
3) If login succeeds with the truncated value, the server silently truncated the password (often a legacy 8/16/72-char limit, e.g. bcrypt's 72-byte cap) - reducing entropy without telling the user.
4) Also test multibyte/emoji passwords for byte-vs-char truncation.
items.how_to_fix: Don't silently truncate: set an explicit, high max length (e.g. 64-128), reject over-length input with a clear message instead of trimming, and pre-hash (e.g. SHA-256 then bcrypt) if you must support very long passphrases past bcrypt's 72-byte limit. VERIFY a truncated prefix of a long password no longer authenticates.

items.title: Test the password-change flow
items.description: Password change must re-verify the current password and invalidate other sessions.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the change-password request, check whether the CURRENT password is required and actually validated - remove/blank/wrong it in Burp and see if the change still succeeds (lets an attacker with a hijacked session/XSS set a new password).
2) Check CSRF protection on the endpoint (missing token -> forced password change).
3) After a successful change, verify OTHER active sessions are invalidated (log in on browser B, change password in browser A, see if B stays authenticated).
4) Also test for user_id parameter to change another user's password (IDOR).
items.how_to_fix: Require and verify the current password on change, protect the endpoint with anti-CSRF, bind the operation to the authenticated user (no client-supplied user id), and invalidate all other sessions + notify the user by email on success. VERIFY change fails without the correct current password and other sessions are killed.

items.title: Test for MFA step bypass
items.description: A skippable second factor provides no real protection.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in with valid creds to reach the 2FA prompt, then try to skip it: directly request a post-login/authenticated URL (e.g. /dashboard, /account) using the session cookie issued before OTP - if it works, MFA is bypassable.
2) Check whether the pre-2FA session is already fully authenticated (forced browsing past the OTP page).
3) Try changing a 'mfa_required=true' flag, removing the 2FA step parameter, or replaying the login response.
4) Test whether backup/social-login/API paths skip MFA entirely.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce MFA server-side as a hard gate: the session must remain in a 'pending-2FA' unauthorized state until a valid OTP is verified, and every protected route/API must reject pending sessions. Apply MFA consistently across all login paths (SSO, API, mobile). VERIFY authenticated URLs are unreachable until OTP is validated.

items.title: Test OTP for brute force and rate limiting
items.description: A 6-digit OTP without rate limiting has only 1,000,000 combinations.
items.how_to_find:
1) Reach the OTP-verify step, then in Burp Intruder brute the 6-digit code (000000-999999) against the same pending session.
2) Watch for a lack of rate limiting / lockout / OTP-rotation: if you can keep guessing, a 6-digit OTP is only 1,000,000 tries (minutes with concurrency).
3) Test bypasses of any limit: new OTP request to reset the counter, X-Forwarded-For rotation, or race/parallel requests.
4) Success on any code = broken.
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit and lock OTP verification server-side: cap attempts per code (e.g. 3-5) then invalidate the code, add per-account/per-IP throttling and exponential backoff, expire codes quickly, and alert on repeated failures. Prefer TOTP/WebAuthn over SMS. VERIFY brute forcing the OTP is blocked after a few attempts and can't be reset by re-requesting.

items.title: Check OTP reuse and expiry
items.description: OTPs must be single-use and short-lived to resist replay.
items.how_to_find:
1) Verify a valid OTP successfully, then try to REUSE the same code on another login/action - it must be rejected (single-use).
2) Request an OTP, wait past its stated lifetime (e.g. 5-10 min), and test whether the old code still works (expiry check).
3) Request multiple OTPs and see whether older ones remain valid (only the latest should work).
4) Reuse/late-acceptance = replay vulnerability.
items.how_to_fix: Make OTPs strictly single-use and short-lived: invalidate a code immediately after successful use (and invalidate older codes when a new one is issued), enforce a short TTL server-side, and bind the code to the pending session/action. VERIFY a used or expired OTP is rejected.

items.title: Test for broken MFA-to-account binding
items.description: If the OTP step trusts a client-supplied user id, an attacker can verify their own OTP against a victim account.
items.how_to_find:
1) Start login as the ATTACKER account to the OTP step, capturing the verify request (which may contain a user id/email/phone parameter and the OTP).
2) Change that identifier to the VICTIM while submitting the attacker's own (or a self-triggered) OTP - if the server verifies the OTP against the wrong account or trusts the client-supplied id, you authenticate as the victim.
3) Also try: request victim's OTP but submit it while your session is bound to attacker; swap the pending-session token.
4) Landing in the victim account = broken MFA-to-account binding.
items.how_to_fix: Bind the OTP strictly server-side to the pending-login session/account - never accept a client-supplied user id to decide whose OTP to check. Validate the submitted code against the exact account that initiated login. VERIFY you cannot verify an OTP against a different account by tampering identifiers.

items.title: Test for weak or missing signature validation
items.description: If the server fails to verify the signature, any forged token is accepted.
items.how_to_find:
1) Grab a JWT (Authorization: Bearer / cookie), decode it in Burp's JWT Editor or jwt_tool.
2) Tamper a claim (e.g. change sub/role/user id) but LEAVE the signature untouched, and replay - if accepted, the server isn't verifying the signature at all.
3) Also try stripping the signature entirely (header.payload.) and sending.
4) jwt_tool -t https://target.tld -rh 'Authorization: Bearer <jwt>' -M pb runs these checks automatically.
items.how_to_fix: Always cryptographically verify the signature server-side with a vetted library before trusting any claim; reject tokens with a missing/invalid signature and never decode-without-verify. Pin the expected algorithm. VERIFY a token with a modified payload is rejected.

items.title: Test for "none" algorithm acceptance
items.description: Tokens with alg=none have no signature; accepting them lets anyone forge claims.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take a valid JWT and set the header alg to 'none' (also try 'None','NONE','nOnE' to defeat naive filters).
2) Keep/modify the payload (e.g. escalate role), and remove the signature so the token is header.payload. (trailing dot).
3) jwt_tool <jwt> -X a  (alg:none exploit) or Burp JWT Editor 'none' attack; replay the token.
4) If the server accepts the unsigned token, anyone can forge arbitrary claims.
items.how_to_fix: Explicitly allow-list only the expected asymmetric/symmetric algorithm(s) and reject 'none' (and any unexpected alg) server-side; use libraries that require an algorithm argument on verify. VERIFY an alg=none token is rejected.

items.title: Test for algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256)
items.description: Switching an asymmetric token to a symmetric one lets the public key be used as the HMAC secret.
items.how_to_fix: Bind verification to one specific algorithm (don't let the token's header choose): use RS256-only verification with the public key, and never feed the public key into an HMAC verify path. Prefer libraries where you pass the exact expected alg. VERIFY an HS256 token signed with the public key is rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Obtain the RS256 public key (from /jwks.json, /.well-known/openid-configuration, a TLS cert, or recover it from two tokens with jwt_tool/rsa_sign2n).
2) Forge a token with header alg=HS256 and sign it using the PUBLIC KEY bytes as the HMAC secret.
   jwt_tool <jwt> -X k -pk public.pem   (algorithm-confusion attack)
3) Replay - if accepted, the server used the public key to verify an HMAC token (RS256->HS256 confusion) = full forgery.

items.title: Brute-force a weak HMAC secret
items.description: HS256 tokens signed with a guessable secret can be forged offline.
items.how_to_find:
1) Confirm the token uses HS256/384/512, then crack the secret offline:
   hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 token.jwt jwt.secrets.list   (or john, or jwt_tool <jwt> -C -d wordlist.txt)
2) If a secret is found, forge arbitrary tokens (change role/sub) signed with it and replay.
3) Weak/guessable/default secrets (e.g. 'secret', 'changeme') crack in seconds.
4) Prove impact by minting an admin token.
items.how_to_fix: Use a long, high-entropy random secret (>=256 bits) stored in a secret manager, never a dictionary word or committed default; rotate it (and rotate on suspicion of leak). Consider asymmetric signing (RS/ES) so no shared secret exists. VERIFY the secret can't be cracked from a captured token.

items.title: Verify expiration (exp) is enforced
items.description: Tokens that never expire (or whose exp is ignored) extend the window for stolen-token abuse indefinitely.
items.how_to_fix: Always validate exp (and nbf) server-side with a small clock-skew allowance and reject expired tokens; keep access-token lifetimes short and use refresh tokens for renewal. VERIFY an expired token is rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the token and check for an exp claim - if absent, tokens never expire.
2) If exp exists, capture a token, wait until after exp (or set the payload's exp to the past and re-sign if you have the secret), and replay - it must be rejected.
3) Also try removing the exp claim entirely (if signature not enforced/weak secret) to test whether the server requires it.
4) A still-valid expired/exp-less token = broken lifetime enforcement.

items.title: Verify audience (aud) validation
