items.description: If a JSON endpoint also accepts simple content types, it may be CSRF-able from a form.
items.how_to_find:
1) For a JSON API endpoint, test whether it also accepts a form-friendly Content-Type (application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, or text/plain) - these can be sent by an HTML form without CORS preflight.
2) Replay the request as text/plain with the JSON as the body (or key=value trick) and see if the server still parses it.
3) If it does and there's no token, build a form-based CSRF PoC.
4) JSON endpoint acting on a simple-content-type cross-site form = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Only accept application/json for JSON endpoints (reject simple content types), and require CSRF tokens/custom headers. Add a regression test. VERIFY form-content-type requests are rejected.

items.title: Test for login and logout CSRF
items.description: Login CSRF can force a victim into an attacker-controlled session; logout CSRF disrupts sessions.
items.how_to_find:
1) Login CSRF: build a form that submits the attacker's credentials to /login without a token - if a victim visits, they're silently logged into the attacker's account (then their activity/payment data is captured).
2) Logout CSRF: a tokenless /logout GET/POST from an attacker page forcibly logs the victim out (annoyance/session disruption).
3) Confirm each fires cross-site without a token.
4) Login/logout action forgeable = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Protect login and logout with anti-CSRF tokens too (pre-session token for login), and use POST + SameSite. Add a regression test. VERIFY login/logout can't be forged cross-site.

items.title: Identify client-side prototype-pollution sources
items.description: User-controllable input merged into objects via __proto__/constructor.prototype pollutes Object.prototype.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for input parsed into objects: URL query/hash parsed to an object, JSON merged with a deep-merge/extend/clone, or libraries like jQuery.extend/lodash.merge.
2) Inject prototype keys via the source, e.g. ?__proto__[polluted]=yes or #a[__proto__][polluted]=yes or JSON {"__proto__":{"polluted":"yes"}} / {"constructor":{"prototype":{"polluted":"yes"}}}.
3) In the console, check Object.prototype.polluted or ({}).polluted - if it returns 'yes', you polluted the global prototype.
4) A settable arbitrary Object.prototype property = prototype-pollution source. Tools: DOM Invader.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid unsafe recursive merges of untrusted input; reject/strip __proto__/constructor/prototype keys, use Map or Object.create(null), and freeze prototypes. Add a regression test. VERIFY prototype keys can't set Object.prototype.

items.title: Chain a pollution source to a gadget (DOM XSS)
items.description: A polluted property consumed by a sink can escalate to DOM XSS or config override.
items.how_to_fix: Fix the pollution source (reject prototype keys, safe merge), and harden gadgets (validate config, avoid reading undefined props into sinks). Add a regression test. VERIFY pollution can't reach a sink. Use DOM Invader to auto-find gadgets.
items.how_to_find: 1) After confirming a pollution source, find a 'gadget' - code that reads a property that defaults from the prototype into a dangerous sink (e.g. options.html into innerHTML, src/url into a script tag, or a config flag that disables escaping). 2) Pollute that property, e.g. __proto__[src]=data:,alert(1) or __proto__[innerHTML]=, then trigger the code path. 3) Watch for JS execution / config override. 4) Pollution -> sink executing = DOM XSS via prototype pollution (critical).

items.title: Test for server-side prototype pollution
items.description: Node.js apps that recursively merge user JSON can have logic/auth bypass or RCE via gadgets.
items.how_to_find:
1) On Node.js JSON endpoints, send a body with a prototype key: {"__proto__":{"polluted":"yes"}} (or constructor.prototype).
2) Detect impact indirectly: a reflected/added property in later responses, changed behavior (e.g. injecting an unexpected field that the app now honors), status/JSON differences, or DoS.
3) Known gadgets can bypass auth checks or reach RCE (e.g. polluting spawn options / template settings).
4) Server behavior changed by a prototype key = server-side prototype pollution (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Reject __proto__/constructor/prototype keys in JSON parsing, use safe merge utilities, Object.create(null), and schema validation. Add a regression test. VERIFY prototype keys in request bodies have no effect.

items.title: Skipping required workflow steps
items.description: Checkout, onboarding, or KYC flows that gate logic only in the UI can be completed by calling a later endpoint directly.
items.how_to_find:
1) Walk a multi-step flow (checkout, onboarding, KYC, password reset) normally while recording requests in Burp.
2) Then, in a fresh session, call a later-step endpoint directly (e.g. POST /checkout/confirm) without completing the earlier steps (payment, verification).
3) If the server processes it, the step gating is UI-only.
4) Reaching completion without required steps = finding (e.g. order without payment, access without KYC).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce step completion server-side via a state check on every step (don't trust UI order). Add a regression test. VERIFY later steps fail until prerequisites are met.

items.title: Out-of-order request submission
items.description: Submitting workflow requests in an unexpected order can reach a state the application assumed unreachable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture all requests in a workflow, then replay them in an unexpected order (e.g. apply a discount after the total is finalized, ship before payment capture, submit approval before review).
2) Observe whether the app reaches a state it assumed unreachable.
3) Compare the resulting record/response to a normal run.
4) A valid-but-inconsistent state from reordering = finding (critical if it grants value/privilege).
items.how_to_fix: Model the workflow as a server-side state machine and reject transitions that aren't valid from the current state. Add a regression test. VERIFY out-of-order requests are rejected.

items.title: Replaying completed steps
items.description: Re-submitting a step that should be one-time (e.g. redeeming a coupon or claiming credit) can double its effect if the server isn't idempotent.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify steps that should be one-time (redeem coupon, apply gift card/credit, claim reward, submit vote/payment).
2) Re-send the same completed request (or replay it in a new tab) and see if it's processed again.
3) Watch for doubled credit, stacked discounts, or repeated payouts.
4) A one-time action processed more than once = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Make one-time actions idempotent server-side (mark consumed, use idempotency keys/nonces, DB uniqueness). Add a regression test. VERIFY replays are rejected.

items.title: Forced state transition
items.description: Directly setting a status field (e.g. status=paid, approved=true) in a request can jump the record to a privileged state if the server trusts client input.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for status/state fields in requests or responses (status=pending, isPaid=false, role=user, approved=false).
2) Add/modify that field in a request to jump straight to a privileged state (status=paid, approved=true, kycVerified=true).
3) If the server accepts the client-set status, you've forced an unauthorized transition.
4) Setting a status field to skip the process = finding (treat as a release blocker).
items.how_to_fix: Never accept client-supplied status; derive state server-side from verified events and authorize each transition. Add a regression test. VERIFY client-set status fields are ignored.

items.title: Negative quantity abuse
items.description: Negative quantities can invert charges or credit a balance when the server multiplies price by an unvalidated quantity.
items.how_to_fix: Validate quantity server-side as a positive integer within sane bounds, and recompute totals server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY negative/zero quantities are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) In an add-to-cart / order / transfer request, change the quantity to a negative value (qty=-1) or a huge value.
2) If the server computes price * qty without validation, a negative qty can produce a negative line total that credits the balance or reduces the overall amount due.
3) Check the cart total / final charge for the effect.
4) Negative qty crediting or lowering the total = finding.

items.title: Decimal / rounding abuse
items.description: Fractional cents, many-decimal inputs, or favorable rounding can be exploited to reduce totals or accumulate value.
items.how_to_fix: Use fixed-precision decimal/minor-unit integer math server-side, validate decimal places, and round consistently (banker's/half-up per policy). Add a regression test. VERIFY fractional/rounding tricks don't change totals.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit prices/quantities with extra decimals or fractional cents (e.g. 0.001, 1.005, or currency amounts with more precision than the currency supports).
2) Watch whether the server rounds in your favor or lets sub-cent amounts accumulate (e.g. many tiny transfers rounding up).
3) Compare charged vs expected across repeated operations.
4) Favorable rounding/precision reducing charges or accruing value = finding.

items.title: Currency confusion
items.description: Mismatching the currency parameter from the price source (e.g. paying a USD price in a weaker currency) lets an attacker pay far less than the real amount.
items.how_to_find:
1) In checkout/payment requests, look for a currency parameter and try changing it (e.g. from USD to a lower-value currency like IDR/JPY, or vice versa) while the price value stays the same.
2) If the server charges the numeric amount in your chosen currency without converting, you pay a fraction of the real price.
3) Also test currency in one step, price in another (mismatch).
4) Charging the wrong currency = finding (critical - direct financial loss).
items.how_to_fix: Bind currency to the server-side price source; never accept a client currency that contradicts the catalog price, and convert authoritatively server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY currency can't be mismatched with the price.

items.title: Client-supplied price trust
items.description: When the price or total is taken from the request rather than recomputed server-side, an attacker simply sets the amount they want to pay.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect add-to-cart/checkout requests for a price, amount, or total field sent from the client.
2) Change it to an arbitrary low value (price=0.01, total=0) and complete the purchase.
3) If the order is accepted at your price, the server trusts client-supplied pricing.
4) Server honoring a client-set price = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Recompute all prices/totals server-side from the authoritative catalog; ignore any client-supplied amount. Add a regression test. VERIFY the charged amount comes from the server, not the request.

items.title: Integer overflow on totals
items.description: Very large quantities or prices can overflow integer arithmetic, wrapping a total to a small or negative value.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit very large quantities or prices (e.g. qty near 2^31/2^63, or a huge unit price) so that price * qty overflows the server's integer type.
2) Watch whether the total wraps to a small or negative number (paying almost nothing) or errors out.
3) Try values just above common int/bigint boundaries.
4) A total that wraps due to overflow = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use arbitrary-precision/decimal money types, cap quantity/price bounds, and check for overflow server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY oversized values are rejected rather than wrapping.

items.title: TOCTOU (time-of-check to time-of-use)
items.description: A gap between validating a condition and acting on it lets concurrent requests slip through with stale state.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find an action that checks a condition then acts on it (balance >= amount then debit; stock > 0 then reserve; not-yet-claimed then claim).
2) Fire many identical requests concurrently (Burp Repeater > send group in parallel, or Turbo Intruder) so several pass the check before any commits.
3) Watch for over-withdrawal, negative balance, or over-allocation.
4) Concurrent requests slipping through a stale check = TOCTOU finding.
items.how_to_fix: Make check-and-act atomic server-side (DB transactions with row locks / SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, atomic conditional updates, or unique constraints). Add a regression test. VERIFY concurrent requests can't bypass the check.

items.title: Limit overrun (coupon / gift-card double spend)
items.description: Single-use or limited-quantity operations can be exceeded by submitting many requests before the first commits.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take a single-use or limited operation (redeem a one-time coupon, withdraw a gift-card balance, claim a limited reward).
2) Send N copies of the redeem request in parallel (single-packet / Turbo Intruder) before the first commits.
3) If more than the allowed number succeed (coupon applied twice, balance spent multiple times), the limit is racy.
4) Exceeding a single-use/quantity limit via concurrency = finding (critical - direct value loss).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce the limit atomically (decrement-and-check in one transaction, unique constraint on redemption, optimistic locking). Add a regression test. VERIFY parallel redemptions can't exceed the limit.

items.title: Parallel request abuse
items.description: Bulk parallel requests can race shared state (counters, inventory, rate buckets) into an inconsistent result.
items.how_to_fix: Serialize updates to shared counters with atomic DB operations/locks (avoid read-modify-write in app code). Add a regression test. VERIFY concurrent updates stay consistent.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify shared state updated on request (inventory counts, like/vote counters, rate buckets, points balances).
2) Send a burst of parallel requests and compare the final value to the expected count.
3) Lost updates (final count lower than it should be) or over-counting indicate a read-modify-write race.
4) Inconsistent shared state under parallel load = finding.

items.title: Single-packet attack
items.description: HTTP/2 single-packet (last-byte synchronization) eliminates network jitter, exposing narrow race windows that timing alone would miss.
items.how_to_find:
1) For a suspected narrow race that timing alone can't hit, use the HTTP/2 single-packet attack (Burp Repeater 'Send group in parallel', or Turbo Intruder engine=HTTP2) to deliver ~20-30 requests' last bytes in one TCP packet, removing network jitter.
2) This synchronizes server-side arrival, exposing sub-millisecond windows (e.g. one-time redemption, balance check).
3) Compare results to a normal sequential run.
4) A race only exploitable with single-packet sync = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Same as other races: atomic transactions/locks and idempotency keys so simultaneous arrival can't bypass checks. Add a regression test. VERIFY single-packet bursts don't cause inconsistency.

items.title: Missing rate limits
items.description: Sensitive endpoints with no throttling allow unbounded automated attacks (brute force, scraping, spam).
items.how_to_find:
1) Pick a sensitive endpoint (login, OTP, password reset, search, API).
2) Send a rapid burst (Burp Intruder / ffuf, e.g. 100+ requests) and watch for 429/lockout vs all 200s.
3) If all succeed with no throttling/CAPTCHA, it's unthrottled - enabling brute force, credential stuffing, scraping, or spam.
4) Unbounded automated requests accepted = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Add server-side rate limiting/throttling (per-account and per-IP), CAPTCHA/backoff on abuse, and lockouts on auth endpoints. Add a regression test. VERIFY bursts get 429/blocked.

items.title: Bypass via header / IP rotation
items.description: Rate limits keyed on spoofable headers (X-Forwarded-For) or source IP can be reset by rotating those values.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger the rate limit, then retry while rotating spoofable identifiers: X-Forwarded-For, X-Real-IP, X-Client-IP, Forwarded, or a changing source IP.
2) If adding/changing X-Forwarded-For: <random> resets the counter and lets requests through again, the limit is keyed on a client-controlled value.
3) Confirm you can sustain volume by rotating the header.
4) Rate limit reset via spoofable header/IP = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Key rate limits on trusted identifiers (authenticated account, verified client IP from the real edge), not client-supplied headers. Add a regression test. VERIFY header rotation doesn't reset limits.

items.title: Per-account vs per-IP limit gaps
items.description: Limits scoped only per-IP miss distributed attacks; limits only per-account miss credential stuffing across many accounts.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine how the limit is scoped: per-IP only or per-account only.
2) If per-IP only: attack one account from many IPs (distributed brute force).
3) If per-account only: spread one password across many accounts from one IP (credential stuffing / password spraying) - each account stays under its limit.
4) Successfully evading the limit by switching the unscoped dimension = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Apply limits on BOTH dimensions (per-account and per-IP/global) plus anomaly detection for spraying/stuffing. Add a regression test. VERIFY both distributed and cross-account attacks are throttled.

items.title: OTP / 2FA brute force
items.description: Short numeric OTPs without attempt limits or token rotation can be brute-forced before expiry.
items.how_to_find:
1) On the OTP/2FA verification step, capture the verify request and brute-force the code with Intruder (e.g. 000000-999999 for a 6-digit OTP).
2) Check for missing attempt limits, no code rotation on failure, a long validity window, or no lockout - any of these makes brute force feasible before expiry.
3) A correct code eventually accepted = bypass.
4) OTP guessable due to no throttling/rotation = finding (critical - full 2FA bypass).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce MFA on sensitive roles, lock down recovery flows, and rate-limit OTP attempts.

items.title: Resource-exhaustion abuse
items.description: Expensive operations (report generation, exports, complex queries) without limits enable denial-of-service through volume.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify expensive operations (report/PDF generation, large exports, complex search, image processing, bulk API).
2) Invoke them repeatedly/concurrently or with large parameters (huge date ranges, big page sizes) and watch response times, CPU/memory, and whether the service degrades.
3) If a modest request volume causes DoS, there's no cost control.
4) Cheap-to-send, expensive-to-serve requests degrading the service = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit and queue expensive operations, cap input sizes/ranges, add timeouts, and offload heavy work to async jobs. Add a regression test. VERIFY heavy operations are bounded and throttled.

items.title: Reuse of single-use codes
items.description: Single-use coupons that are not atomically marked as consumed can be redeemed repeatedly.
items.how_to_find:
1) Redeem a single-use coupon on an order, then apply the same code again on a new order (or remove and re-add it in the same cart).
2) If the discount applies a second time, the code isn't marked consumed atomically.
3) Also try replaying the redeem request in parallel (race) to double-spend.
4) A one-time code redeemable more than once = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Mark codes consumed atomically at redemption (DB transaction/unique constraint) and re-check on every apply. Add a regression test. VERIFY a used code is rejected on reuse and under concurrency.

items.title: Coupon stacking
items.description: Applying multiple discounts that should be mutually exclusive can drive prices below intended floors.
items.how_to_find:
1) Apply multiple coupons that are meant to be mutually exclusive - via the UI if it allows, or by sending repeated apply-coupon requests with different codes.
2) Check whether the discounts stack (add up) and drive the price below the intended floor (or to zero/negative).
3) Also try stacking the same code type or a percentage + fixed combo.
4) Multiple exclusive discounts stacking = finding (critical - value loss).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce coupon exclusivity/limits server-side and clamp the final price to a floor (never below zero). Add a regression test. VERIFY conflicting coupons can't stack.

items.title: Coupon code enumeration
items.description: Predictable or short coupon codes without rate limits can be brute-forced to discover valid discounts.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fuzz the apply-coupon endpoint with a wordlist/pattern of likely codes (SAVE10, common formats, short/predictable codes) using Intruder/ffuf.
2) Distinguish valid vs invalid by response (discount applied, different message/status).
3) If there's no rate limit and codes are guessable, you can harvest valid discounts.
4) Enumerable valid coupons = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use long, random, high-entropy coupon codes; rate-limit and monitor the apply endpoint; return uniform responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY codes can't be brute-forced.

items.title: Case-sensitivity / normalization bypass
items.description: Inconsistent normalization of coupon codes can let a redeemed code be reused in a different case or with surrounding whitespace.
items.how_to_find:
1) Redeem a single-use code, then retry it with different casing (save10 vs SAVE10), surrounding whitespace ( SAVE10 ), or unicode look-alikes.
2) If the code matches (applies the discount) but the consumed-marker was stored under a different normalized form, you can reuse it.
3) This indicates inconsistent normalization between validation and consumption.
4) Reusing a code via case/whitespace variation = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize codes (trim, canonical case, unicode normalize) consistently at both validation and consumption, keyed on the same value. Add a regression test. VERIFY case/whitespace variants map to one redemption.

items.title: Hidden-field tampering
items.description: Hidden form fields (price, user id, discount, role) are fully attacker-controlled and must never be trusted server-side.
items.how_to_find:
1) View the page source / intercept a form submit and find hidden inputs (price, item_id, user_id, discount, role, redirect).
2) Change their values in the request (e.g. price=1, user_id=<victim>, role=admin) and submit.
3) If the server acts on the tampered value, it trusts client-controlled hidden fields.
4) Server honoring a tampered hidden field = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Never trust hidden fields; recompute/authorize server-side from session and authoritative data (sign/validate if they must round-trip). Add a regression test. VERIFY tampered hidden fields are ignored.

items.title: Trusting client-supplied flags
items.description: Boolean flags from the client (isPremium, hasPaid, emailVerified) used in server decisions allow trivial privilege or feature unlocking.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for boolean flags in requests/responses (isPremium, hasPaid, emailVerified, isAdmin, trial=false).
2) Set them to your advantage (isPremium=true, hasPaid=true, emailVerified=true) and replay.
3) If features unlock or checks pass, the server trusts client flags.
4) A client-set flag changing server behavior = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Derive entitlement/verification state server-side from authoritative records; ignore client-supplied flags. Add a regression test. VERIFY client flags don't grant access.

items.title: Parameter pollution affecting logic
items.description: Supplying a parameter multiple times can cause different tiers (proxy, framework, app) to read different values, altering logic decisions.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize duplicate parameters consistently across all tiers (proxy, framework, app) and reject unexpected duplicates. Add a regression test. VERIFY HPP can't change the logic decision.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send the same parameter multiple times in a request (e.g. ?role=user&role=admin, or duplicated in query and body).
2) Different tiers may pick different occurrences (first/last/concatenated) - observe which value the app acts on vs what a WAF/proxy validated.
3) If the security check sees one value and the app uses another, you bypass logic (HTTP Parameter Pollution).
4) A logic/authz decision changed via duplicate params = finding.

items.title: Detect weak protocols and ciphers
items.description: Legacy SSL/TLS versions and weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES, export suites) enable decryption and downgrade attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Scan the host with testssl.sh, sslscan, or nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 <host> (or Qualys SSL Labs).
2) Look for enabled SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES, DES, EXPORT, NULL) and weak DH/key exchange.
3) Any legacy protocol or weak cipher offered = downgrade/decryption risk.
4) Report the exact protocols/ciphers accepted.
items.how_to_fix: Disable SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers; allow only TLS 1.2+ with modern AEAD suites and strong key exchange. Add a regression test/scan. VERIFY legacy protocols and weak ciphers are refused.

items.title: Validate certificate, expiry, and hostname
items.description: Expired, self-signed, or hostname-mismatched certificates undermine trust and can mask MITM conditions.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the cert: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -servername <host> (or the browser cert viewer).
2) Check notAfter (expiry), issuer (self-signed vs trusted CA), and that the SAN/CN matches the hostname.
3) Flag expired, self-signed, wrong-hostname, or weak-key (RSA<2048) certs - these enable MITM or erode trust.
4) Any cert validation failure = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy a valid CA-issued cert with correct SANs and strong key, and automate renewal (e.g. ACME) before expiry. Add a regression test/monitor. VERIFY the cert is trusted, current, and hostname-matched.

items.title: Check HSTS configuration
items.description: Missing or weak Strict-Transport-Security lets attackers force HTTP and strip TLS via downgrade.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request an HTTPS page and inspect the Strict-Transport-Security response header.
2) Missing header, short max-age, or no includeSubDomains means browsers can be forced to HTTP and TLS stripped (sslstrip).
3) Also verify HTTP redirects to HTTPS before setting cookies.
4) Missing/weak HSTS = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains (and preload if eligible) on all HTTPS responses, and redirect HTTP->HTTPS. Add a regression test. VERIFY the HSTS header is present and strong.

items.title: Detect mixed content
items.description: HTTPS pages loading HTTP resources expose those requests to interception and tampering.
items.how_to_fix: Serve all resources over HTTPS, fix hard-coded http:// URLs, and add a CSP with block-all-mixed-content / upgrade-insecure-requests. Add a regression test. VERIFY no mixed-content warnings remain.
items.how_to_find:
1) Load HTTPS pages and watch the browser console/DevTools for 'Mixed Content' warnings.
2) Grep responses for http:// resource URLs (scripts, images, iframes, XHR).
3) Active mixed content (scripts) is interceptable/tamperable; passive (images) is still a leak.
4) Any HTTP resource loaded on an HTTPS page = finding.

items.title: Test for downgrade and renegotiation flaws
items.description: Lack of TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV, insecure renegotiation, and compression flaws enable downgrade and known attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Run testssl.sh / sslscan and check for: missing TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV (downgrade protection), insecure/client-initiated renegotiation, and TLS compression (CRIME).
2) Test known issues (e.g. openssl s_client -fallback_scsv, -no_tls1_2 to force downgrade).
3) Any of these enable downgrade or known attacks.
4) Report the specific flaw detected.
items.how_to_fix: Enable TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV, disable insecure renegotiation and TLS compression, and prefer TLS 1.2+ only. Add a regression scan. VERIFY downgrade/renegotiation protections are in place.

sections.title: Configuration & Deployment — Error Handling & Information Leakage

items.title: Trigger and review stack traces
items.description: Unhandled exceptions leak file paths, framework internals, query fragments, and sometimes secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Force errors: send malformed input, wrong types, oversized values, bad JSON, or hit endpoints with missing params.
2) Look in responses for stack traces, file paths, framework/version details, SQL fragments, or config in the body.
3) Compare 500 responses to see what internals leak.
4) A stack trace / internal detail in the response = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Return generic error pages/messages to clients; log full details server-side only and disable debug mode in production. Add a regression test. VERIFY errors expose no stack traces or internals.

items.title: Identify verbose application errors
items.description: Detailed application error messages disclose logic, validation rules, and backend technology.
items.how_to_fix: Use generic, non-revealing error messages for clients; keep detailed diagnostics in server logs. Add a regression test. VERIFY errors don't disclose logic/validation/tech details.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger validation and application errors (invalid values, out-of-range, duplicate submits) and read the messages.
2) Look for disclosures like exact validation rules, backend technology, table/column names, or internal logic hints.
3) These help an attacker refine further attacks.
4) Overly verbose application errors = finding.

items.title: Find exposed debug endpoints
items.description: Debug consoles, actuator endpoints, and profilers left enabled in production allow inspection and sometimes RCE.
items.how_to_find:
1) Probe for debug/management endpoints: /actuator, /actuator/env, /debug, /__debug__, /_profiler, /console, /trace, /heapdump, /phpinfo.php, framework debug toolbars.
2) Use a wordlist (ffuf) and check responses for exposed config, env vars, or interactive consoles.
3) An enabled debug console can leak secrets or allow RCE.
4) Any reachable debug/actuator endpoint in prod = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug consoles/profilers in production and restrict management endpoints (auth + network allowlist). Add a regression test. VERIFY debug endpoints are absent or locked down.

items.title: Detect version banners in responses
items.description: Server and middleware version banners help attackers select version-specific exploits.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect response headers (Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, X-Generator) and default error pages for product/version strings.
2) Use curl -I or Burp to capture them across endpoints.
3) Version banners let attackers look up version-specific exploits.
4) Exposed version banners = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Suppress/normalize version headers and banners (remove Server/X-Powered-By, generic error pages). Add a regression test. VERIFY responses don't reveal product versions.

items.title: Check for sensitive data in responses
items.description: Responses sometimes include PII, tokens, internal IDs, or debug fields not rendered in the UI.
items.how_to_find:
1) Compare API/JSON responses to what the UI actually renders - inspect the raw body in Burp/DevTools.
2) Look for extra fields: other users' PII, tokens/secrets, internal IDs, password hashes, debug flags.
3) Over-fetching / verbose serializers often leak data not shown on screen.
4) Sensitive data present in a response = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Return only the fields the client needs via explicit response DTOs/serializers; never expose secrets or other users' data. Add a regression test. VERIFY responses contain no sensitive extra fields.

items.title: Test for unrestricted file type upload
items.description: Accepting executable or script types lets an attacker upload dangerous content the server may run or serve.
items.how_to_find:
1) At each upload feature, try uploading disallowed/executable types (.php, .jsp, .aspx, .svg, .html, .phtml).
2) If the server accepts them without whitelist validation, dangerous content can be stored/served.
3) Note where the file lands and whether it's web-accessible.
4) Any executable/script type accepted = finding (pair with execution test).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a server-side allowlist of safe extensions AND content types, reject everything else. Add a regression test. VERIFY disallowed types are rejected.

items.title: Bypass content-type and magic-byte checks
items.description: Validation based only on Content-Type or magic bytes can be spoofed to smuggle dangerous content.
items.how_to_fix: Validate with multiple layers (allowlist extension + verified content type + re-encode/sanitize where possible); don't rely on client Content-Type alone. Add a regression test. VERIFY spoofed uploads are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload a script but spoof the Content-Type header to an allowed value (e.g. image/png) in the multipart request.
2) Also prepend valid magic bytes (GIF89a;) to a php file to pass magic-byte checks.
3) If the upload succeeds because only Content-Type/magic bytes were checked, the validation is bypassable.
4) Dangerous content accepted via spoofed type = finding.

items.title: Test double-extension and null-byte tricks
items.description: Filenames like shell.php.jpg or shell.php%00.jpg can defeat naive extension filters on some servers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload filenames with double extensions (shell.php.jpg, shell.jpg.php) and null-byte tricks (shell.php%00.jpg) against the extension filter.
2) On misconfigured Apache/older stacks, shell.php.jpg may execute as PHP; null bytes can truncate the name before the real extension check.
3) Check whether the stored file is executable/served as code.
4) A naive extension filter defeated = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the final extension against a strict allowlist, reject multiple extensions/null bytes, and store with a server-generated safe name. Add a regression test. VERIFY these tricks are rejected.

items.title: Test path traversal in filename
items.description: Traversal sequences in the filename can write uploads outside the intended directory, overwriting files.
items.how_to_find:
1) Set the upload filename to a traversal path (../../var/www/html/shell.php, ..\..\ on Windows).
2) If the server uses the raw filename to build the storage path, the file may be written outside the intended directory, overwriting system/web files.
3) Check where the file actually landed.
4) Upload written outside the target dir = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Ignore the client filename for pathing; generate your own name and write into a fixed, non-executable directory (canonicalize and validate). Add a regression test. VERIFY traversal filenames can't escape the upload dir.

items.title: Attempt web-shell upload and execution
items.description: Uploading server-executable code into a web-accessible path yields remote code execution.
items.how_to_find:
1) Combine the earlier bypasses to store server-executable code (e.g. a PHP/JSP web shell) in a web-accessible path.
2) Browse to the uploaded file's URL and pass a command (?cmd=id).
3) If it executes, you have remote code execution.
4) Any command execution via uploaded file = finding (critical - RCE).
items.how_to_fix: Store uploads outside the web root or on object storage, serve via a handler that never executes them, and disable script execution in the upload dir. Add a regression test. VERIFY uploaded files can't be executed.

items.title: Test SVG/HTML stored XSS via upload
items.description: SVG and HTML files served inline can execute script in the origin, producing stored XSS.
items.how_to_find: Map reflection and storage points in Configuration & Deployment — File Upload. Inject context-appropriate payloads for HTML, attribute, and JS sinks. Confirm encoding and CSP block execution.
items.how_to_fix: Encode output per context, deploy a strict CSP, and use framework auto-escaping. Retest with the same payloads after the fix ships.

items.title: Test image-parsing exploits (ImageTragick)
items.description: Server-side image processors can be exploited (e.g. ImageMagick/ImageTragick CVE-2016-3714) via crafted images to achieve SSRF, file read, or RCE.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify uploads that get server-side processing (thumbnailing, conversion) - often ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick.
2) Upload crafted payloads: an ImageTragick .mvg/.svg (CVE-2016-3714) attempting command exec or url fetch, or files triggering XXE/SSRF in the parser.
3) Watch for outbound callbacks (use a collaborator/OAST), command output, or file disclosure.
4) Any parser-triggered SSRF/read/RCE = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Patch/upgrade image libraries, apply a restrictive ImageMagick policy.xml, disable dangerous coders, and process in a sandbox. Add a regression test. VERIFY crafted images can't trigger exec/SSRF.

items.title: Test size limits and decompression DoS
items.description: Missing size limits, zip bombs, and pixel-flood images can exhaust storage, memory, or CPU.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload very large files to check for a max-size limit.
2) Upload a zip bomb (small archive expanding to GBs) or a pixel-flood image (tiny file, huge declared dimensions) to endpoints that decompress/process.
3) Watch storage, memory, and CPU - if the server exhausts resources, there's a decompression/size DoS.
4) Resource exhaustion from a small upload = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce upload size limits, cap decompressed size/dimensions, and stream-process with timeouts and resource quotas. Add a regression test. VERIFY oversized/bomb uploads are rejected.

items.title: Test antivirus and malware-scan bypass
items.description: Weak or absent malware scanning lets malicious files pass through and reach other users or systems.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload the EICAR test string to confirm whether any AV scanning exists.
2) If scanning exists, try evasions: archive/encrypt the payload, split it, or use uncommon formats to see if it passes.
3) If malicious files reach storage or other users unscanned, the control is weak/absent.
4) Malware passing through to users/systems = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scan uploads with an updated AV/sandbox before making them available, quarantine on detection, and don't serve unscanned files. Add a regression test (EICAR). VERIFY known-malicious files are blocked.

items.title: Test overwrite of existing files
items.description: Predictable storage names without collision handling let an attacker overwrite other users’ or system files.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload two files with the same name (or a predictable name like avatar.jpg / a known system path) and see if the second overwrites the first.
2) If storage names are predictable and there's no collision handling, you can overwrite other users' or system files.
3) Confirm by retrieving the target after your upload.
4) Overwriting another file via naming = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Store with server-generated unique names (UUIDs) scoped per user/tenant, and never allow client-controlled paths. Add a regression test. VERIFY uploads can't overwrite existing files.

sections.title: Configuration & Deployment — Default Files & Directory Listing

items.title: Find default install and welcome pages
items.description: Default server/app pages confirm technology and may expose configuration or setup wizards.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request common default pages: /, /index.html default Apache/Nginx/IIS welcome pages, /setup, /install, /installer, /wp-admin/install.php.
2) These confirm the tech stack and may expose live setup wizards (which can let you reconfigure the app).
3) Use a wordlist (ffuf) to enumerate.
4) Any reachable default/welcome/setup page = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove default pages and disable/lock installers after setup; return your own content. Add a regression test. VERIFY default and setup pages are gone.

items.title: Detect sample and example applications
items.description: Bundled sample apps and examples often contain known vulnerabilities and unprotected functionality.
items.how_to_fix: Remove all bundled sample/example apps from production. Add a regression test. VERIFY no sample apps are reachable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Probe for bundled samples/examples: /examples/, /samples/, /docs/, Tomcat /examples/servlets, /manager/html, phpMyAdmin setup, etc.
2) These often ship with known CVEs or unprotected functionality (info disclosure, SSRF, XSS).
3) Enumerate with a wordlist and check each hit.
4) Any reachable sample/example app = finding.

items.title: Locate exposed admin consoles
items.description: Management consoles reachable from the internet are high-value targets, especially with default credentials.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for management consoles: /admin, /manager/html (Tomcat), /jenkins, /phpmyadmin, /wp-admin, /actuator, cloud/db dashboards.
2) If reachable from the internet, try default credentials (admin/admin) and check for auth.
3) Exposed consoles are high-value, especially with default creds.
4) An internet-reachable admin console (esp. with weak/default auth) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict consoles to VPN/allowlisted IPs, require strong auth/MFA, and change default credentials. Add a regression test. VERIFY consoles aren't publicly reachable.

items.title: Check for directory listing enabled
items.description: Autoindex/directory listing exposes file inventories, including backups and sensitive resources.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request directories without an index file (/uploads/, /backup/, /assets/, /.git/) and see if the server returns an autoindex listing of files.
2) A listing exposes file inventories - backups, configs, source, and other sensitive resources.
3) Browse the listed files for anything useful.
4) Directory listing enabled = finding (critical - info disclosure).
items.how_to_fix: Disable autoindex/directory listing (Options -Indexes / autoindex off) and add index files. Add a regression test. VERIFY directories don't return listings.

items.title: Find exposed backup archives
items.description: Web-accessible .zip/.tar.gz/.sql archives can leak full source, databases, and credentials.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fuzz for web-accessible archives/backups: backup.zip, site.tar.gz, db.sql, www.zip, <appname>.bak, config.php~, .env.bak (ffuf with a backup wordlist).
2) Download any hit and inspect for full source, DB dumps, or credentials.
3) These leak everything needed to compromise the app.
4) Any downloadable backup archive = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Store backups outside the web root, block archive extensions at the server, and remove any published ones. Add a regression test. VERIFY backups aren't web-accessible.

items.title: Find exposed CI/CD configuration
items.description: Deployed CI files (.gitlab-ci.yml, workflows, Jenkinsfile) reveal build logic, internal hosts, and sometimes tokens.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request CI/CD config files that may have been deployed: /.gitlab-ci.yml, /.github/workflows/, /Jenkinsfile, /.circleci/config.yml, /azure-pipelines.yml, /.env, /docker-compose.yml.
2) These reveal build logic, internal hostnames, registry URLs, and sometimes embedded tokens.
3) Download and review any that resolve.
4) A web-accessible CI/CD config = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Keep CI/CD config out of the web root/deploy artifact and block dotfiles at the server. Add a regression test. VERIFY these files aren't served.

items.title: Detect hardcoded secrets in the repo
items.description: Committed credentials, tokens, and keys in source or history are directly abusable and persist in git history.
items.how_to_find:
1) If source is reachable (exposed .git, published repo, JS bundles), scan it and its history with gitleaks/trufflehog for API keys, DB passwords, tokens, and private keys.
2) Check an exposed /.git/ dir (dump with git-dumper) and grep client-side JS for embedded secrets.
3) Validate any found secret is live.
4) Any hardcoded, valid secret = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Remove secrets from code AND git history (rotate them - history persists), and load from a secrets manager/env. Add secret scanning to CI. VERIFY no live secrets remain in source or history.

items.title: Find exposed pipeline/build endpoints
items.description: Internet-facing CI dashboards and build servers can leak logs and may allow job execution.
items.how_to_fix: Put CI dashboards behind VPN/auth, disable anonymous access, and restrict job triggering. Add a regression test. VERIFY build endpoints aren't publicly reachable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for internet-facing CI/build systems: Jenkins (/jenkins, port 8080), GitLab, TeamCity, Bamboo, Drone.
2) Check for anonymous access to build logs/configs and whether jobs can be triggered without auth.
3) Exposed dashboards leak logs (with secrets) and may allow arbitrary job execution.
4) A reachable, weakly-authed CI dashboard = finding (critical).

items.title: Check for leaked build artifacts
items.description: Published build artifacts (maps, debug symbols, internal packages) can expose source and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for published build artifacts: source maps (*.js.map), debug symbols, internal packages, or artifact directories.
2) Fetch a .js.map and use it to reconstruct original source (revealing logic, endpoints, secrets).
3) Check artifact registries/paths for internal packages leaking code.
4) Any artifact exposing source/secrets = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Don't ship source maps/debug symbols to production (or restrict them), and keep internal artifacts in private registries. Add a regression test. VERIFY no revealing artifacts are public.

items.title: Assess dependency-confusion risk
items.description: Internal package names referenced in manifests can be hijacked on public registries to achieve code execution in builds.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect package names from exposed manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, pom.xml) - especially internal-looking scoped names.
2) Check whether those names are unclaimed on the public registry (npm/PyPI/etc.).
3) If a build can resolve an internal name from the public registry, an attacker could publish a malicious package and get code execution in the build (dependency confusion).
4) An unclaimed internal package name = finding (high).
items.how_to_fix: Claim/reserve internal names on public registries, pin to your private registry with scoped configs, and verify package sources in CI. Add a regression check. VERIFY builds can't pull internal names from public registries.

items.title: Detect dangling DNS records
items.description: DNS entries pointing to deprovisioned resources can be reclaimed by an attacker to serve content under the domain.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate all subdomains (amass, subfinder, crt.sh) and resolve their DNS records.
2) Look for records pointing to deprovisioned resources - a CNAME/A to an IP or host that no longer responds or returns a provider 'not found' page.
3) Confirm with dig/nslookup and check the target still exists.
4) A DNS record pointing to a deprovisioned resource = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Remove or repoint dangling DNS records and add continuous DNS/asset monitoring. Add a regression check. VERIFY no record points to an unowned resource.

items.title: Find CNAMEs to unclaimed providers
items.description: CNAMEs to SaaS providers with no active account allow an attacker to claim the resource and take over the host.
items.how_to_find:
1) For each subdomain CNAME, identify the SaaS provider (GitHub Pages, Heroku, S3, Azure, Shopify, Fastly, etc.).
2) Visit the host and look for provider fingerprint error pages ('There isn't a GitHub Pages site here', 'NoSuchBucket').
3) Cross-check with a takeover fingerprint tool (subjack, nuclei takeover templates).
4) A CNAME to an unclaimed provider resource = finding (critical - takeover possible).
items.how_to_fix: Remove the CNAME or re-claim the resource on the provider; monitor for takeover fingerprints. Add a regression check. VERIFY the subdomain no longer points to an unclaimed resource.

items.title: Verify takeover safely and responsibly
items.description: Confirming a takeover requires careful, non-destructive proof to avoid disrupting or hijacking live services.
items.how_to_find:
1) To prove a takeover without disrupting anything, claim the resource on the provider and serve only a benign, unique marker (e.g. a random string in a text file) - never real-looking content.
2) Confirm the marker loads over the target subdomain, screenshot, then release the resource.
3) Stay within scope and get authorization; avoid hijacking live services or capturing others' traffic.
4) A confirmed benign proof of control = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Report with the non-destructive proof, remove/repoint the record, and reclaim provider resources. Add a regression check. VERIFY the takeover is no longer possible after remediation.

items.title: Check for delegated NS takeover
items.description: Subdomains delegated to nameservers on an unclaimed/expired zone can be fully controlled by an attacker.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check NS delegation for subdomains (dig NS sub.example.com) and see if any delegate to a nameserver on an unclaimed/expired zone or deprovisioned DNS provider account.
2) If the delegated zone can be registered/claimed by anyone, an attacker controls all records under that subdomain.
3) Confirm the NS target is unowned/claimable.
4) A delegation to a claimable nameserver/zone = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove stale NS delegations and reclaim the zone with your DNS provider. Add a regression check. VERIFY delegations only point to zones you control.

items.title: Detect takeover of cloud storage assets
items.description: Records pointing to released cloud buckets/endpoints let an attacker register the same name and serve content.
items.how_to_fix: Remove records for released buckets/endpoints or recreate the asset under your account; monitor for takeover fingerprints. Add a regression check. VERIFY no record points to a claimable storage name.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find records pointing to cloud storage/endpoints (S3 *.s3.amazonaws.com, Azure Blob *.blob.core.windows.net, GCS).
2) Request the host and look for 'NoSuchBucket' / not-found responses indicating the bucket/endpoint was released.
3) If the same name can be re-registered in that cloud, an attacker can serve content under your domain.
4) A record pointing to a claimable released storage name = finding.

sections.title: Configuration & Deployment — HTTP Methods & Server Config

items.title: Test dangerous HTTP methods (PUT/DELETE)
items.description: Enabled PUT/DELETE can let an attacker write, replace, or remove server files and resources.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send OPTIONS to see the Allow header, then try PUT and DELETE against a path (e.g. PUT /test.txt with a body, DELETE /test.txt).
2) If PUT writes a file or DELETE removes one, the server allows unauthenticated file manipulation.
3) Try uploading a script via PUT and requesting it (possible RCE).
4) Working PUT/DELETE = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Disable WebDAV/PUT/DELETE unless required; restrict write methods to authenticated, authorized paths. Add a regression test. VERIFY PUT/DELETE are rejected.

items.title: Test HTTP method override
items.description: Override headers (X-HTTP-Method-Override) and verb tunneling can reach restricted methods through allowed ones.
items.how_to_fix: Ignore method-override headers unless explicitly needed, and enforce authz on the effective method, not the outer verb. Add a regression test. VERIFY overrides can't reach restricted methods.
items.how_to_find:
1) If PUT/DELETE are blocked directly, send an allowed verb (POST) with an override header: X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE (or _method=DELETE in the body).
2) Some frameworks/proxies honor the override, letting you reach a restricted method through an allowed one.
3) Check whether the restricted action executes.
4) Reaching a restricted method via override = finding.

items.title: Test TRACE method and XST
items.description: An enabled TRACE method can enable Cross-Site Tracing, reflecting headers and aiding credential theft.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send a TRACE request to the server and check whether it reflects your request headers back in the response.
2) If enabled, TRACE can be abused for Cross-Site Tracing (XST) to read otherwise-protected headers (e.g. cookies) via a client-side vector.
3) Confirm the reflected headers.
4) TRACE enabled = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable the TRACE method at the web server. Add a regression test. VERIFY TRACE returns 405/not allowed.

items.title: Note CORS misconfiguration (see Client-Side)
items.description: Server CORS configuration governs cross-origin access; permissive policies are a common misconfiguration.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send requests with an Origin header (e.g. Origin: https://evil.com) and inspect Access-Control-Allow-Origin / Access-Control-Allow-Credentials in the response.
2) Flag permissive configs: reflecting arbitrary Origin, ACAO: * with credentials, or trusting null/regex-matchable origins.
3) See the Client-Side CORS checks for full exploitation.
4) A permissive CORS policy = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Allow only an explicit origin allowlist, never reflect arbitrary origins, and don't combine wildcard with credentials. Add a regression test. VERIFY untrusted origins are refused.

items.title: Audit server security headers
items.description: Missing headers (CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy) weaken defense-in-depth.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture response headers (curl -I / Burp / securityheaders.com) and check for CSP, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors), Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy.
2) Note which are missing or weak (e.g. no CSP, missing nosniff enabling MIME sniffing, missing frame protection enabling clickjacking).
3) These weaken defense-in-depth.
4) Missing/weak security headers = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set a strong CSP, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, frame-ancestors/X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy on all responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY the headers are present and correct.

items.title: Conduct Search Engine Discovery Reconnaissance for Information Leakage
items.description: Identify what sensitive design and configuration information of the application, system, or organization is exposed directly (on the organization's site) or indirectly (via third-party services).
items.how_to_find:
1) Use search-engine dorks against the target (site:target.com, filetype:pdf/xls/env, inurl:admin, intitle:index.of) on Google/Bing/DuckDuckGo.
2) Check cached pages, Google Groups, GitHub, Pastebin, and archive.org for leaked configs, credentials, internal docs, or emails.
3) Note anything sensitive exposed directly or via third parties.
4) Sensitive info discoverable via search = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove sensitive data from public pages, request cache/index removal, restrict indexing of sensitive paths, and rotate any leaked secrets. VERIFY the exposed data is no longer discoverable.

items.title: Fingerprint Web Server
items.description: Determine the version and type of a running web server to enable further discovery of any known vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the Server header and response quirks with curl -I, and run whatweb / nmap -sV / Nikto against the host.
2) Compare error page fingerprints and header ordering to identify server type and version.
3) Look up known CVEs for the detected version.
4) A precise server/version banner enabling exploit selection = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Suppress/normalize the Server header and version banners, and keep the server patched. Add a regression test. VERIFY responses don't reveal exact server version.

items.title: Review Webserver Metafiles for Information Leakage
items.description: Identify hidden or obfuscated paths and functionality through the analysis of metadata files.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fetch metafiles: /robots.txt, /sitemap.xml, /.well-known/, /security.txt, /crossdomain.xml, /clientaccesspolicy.xml.
2) robots.txt Disallow entries often reveal hidden/admin paths; sitemaps enumerate endpoints.
3) Request the disclosed paths to see what's reachable.
4) Metafiles exposing hidden/sensitive paths = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Don't list sensitive paths in metafiles (protect them with auth instead), and remove unneeded policy files. VERIFY metafiles don't leak sensitive locations.

items.title: Attack Surface Identification
items.description: Enumerate all web applications within scope.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate hosts/subdomains (amass, subfinder, crt.sh), then discover apps/endpoints per host (ffuf, gobuster, spidering in Burp).
2) Catalog every reachable app, API, admin panel, and legacy/staging host in scope.
3) Flag unexpected or forgotten apps (staging, old versions) that widen the attack surface.
4) Unmanaged/unexpected exposed apps = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Inventory and decommission unused/legacy apps, and restrict non-production hosts. Add asset monitoring. VERIFY only intended applications are exposed.

items.title: Review Web Page Content for Information Leakage
items.description: Review web page comments, metadata, and redirect bodies to find any information leakage.
items.how_to_fix: Strip developer comments/metadata from production output, remove secrets from client code, and review redirect bodies. Add a regression check. VERIFY page content leaks nothing sensitive.
items.how_to_find:
1) View source and JS bundles; grep for HTML comments, TODOs, internal URLs, credentials, API keys, and email addresses.
2) Inspect redirect response bodies (they sometimes render full content before redirecting).
3) Check metadata (EXIF, generator tags) and source maps.
4) Any secret/internal detail in page content = finding.

items.title: Identify Application Entry Points
items.description: Identify possible entry and injection points through request and response analysis.
items.how_to_find:
1) Proxy the app through Burp and catalog every request: URLs, methods, query/body params, headers, and cookies.
2) Mark parameters that influence responses - these are candidate injection points for later testing.
3) Include hidden fields, JSON keys, and file uploads.
4) A documented entry-point map guiding injection testing = deliverable (not a vuln itself).
items.how_to_fix: Ensure all entry points enforce validation and authorization; minimize unnecessary parameters. VERIFY each entry point is covered by input validation and authz tests.

items.title: Map Execution Paths Through Application
items.description: Map the target application and understand the principal workflows.
items.how_to_find:
1) Walk the primary workflows (login, checkout, admin) while proxying, and diagram the sequence of requests/state transitions.
2) Identify multi-step flows, hidden states, and conditional paths that testing must cover.
3) Note where authz/state checks occur across steps.
4) A complete execution-path map ensuring full coverage = deliverable.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure server-side state/authz checks exist at each step of every workflow. VERIFY no workflow step can be skipped or reordered.

items.title: Fingerprint Web Application Framework
items.description: Fingerprint the components used by the web applications.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for framework fingerprints: cookie names (PHPSESSID, JSESSIONID, csrftoken), headers (X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version), URL patterns, and error pages.
2) Run whatweb / Wappalyzer and check static asset paths (/wp-content/, /_next/).
3) Map to known versions and CVEs.
4) Framework/version disclosure enabling targeted exploits = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove framework version headers and revealing cookie names where possible, and keep frameworks patched. VERIFY the framework version isn't disclosed.

items.title: Fingerprint Web Application
items.description: Identify the specific web application (and version) in use - e.g. a known CMS or off-the-shelf product - to find version-specific vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify off-the-shelf apps (WordPress, Joomla, Drupal, GitLab) via default paths, readme/CHANGELOG files, meta generator tags, and asset hashes.
2) Use WPScan / whatweb to pin the exact version.
3) Cross-reference the version against public CVEs.
4) A known app version with published vulns = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Keep the application updated, remove version-disclosing files (readme, CHANGELOG), and hide generator tags. VERIFY the app version isn't disclosed and is patched.

items.title: Map Application Architecture
items.description: Understand the architecture of the application and the technologies in use.
items.how_to_find:
1) Infer the architecture from responses: presence of a WAF/CDN (headers, block pages), load balancers (cookies), reverse proxies, and backend tiers.
2) Note API gateways, microservice hints, and third-party integrations.
3) Build a component/technology map to guide targeted testing.
4) An architecture map (and any exposed internal component) = deliverable/finding.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure internal components aren't directly exposed and intermediary devices (WAF/proxy) are correctly configured. VERIFY only intended tiers are reachable.

sections.title: OWASP WSTG — Configuration and Deployment Management Testing

items.title: Test Network Infrastructure Configuration
items.description: Review the applications' configurations set across the network and validate that they are not vulnerable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Port-scan the host (nmap) to find exposed services beyond 80/443 (DB ports, admin ports, SSH, RDP).
2) Check for management interfaces, default configs, and outdated services on those ports.
3) Verify network controls (firewalls, segmentation) actually restrict access.
4) Any unnecessary exposed/misconfigured service = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Close unneeded ports, put management services behind VPN/allowlist, and segment networks. Add monitoring. VERIFY only required services are reachable.

items.title: Test Application Platform Configuration
items.description: Ensure that default and known files have been removed.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for default/sample files, docs, and test scripts left on the platform (server manuals, /test.php, phpinfo, default apps).
2) Review platform config for verbose logging, debug mode, and permissive defaults.
3) Confirm each is reachable in production.
4) Default/known files or insecure platform defaults present = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove default/sample files and harden the platform config (disable debug, tighten logging) per a hardening baseline. VERIFY defaults are removed and config is hardened.

items.title: Test File Extensions Handling for Sensitive Information
items.description: Enumerate sensitive file extensions that might contain raw data such as scripts or credentials Validate that no system framework bypasses exist for the rules that have been set.
items.how_to_fix: Configure the server to deny or correctly handle sensitive extensions, store source/config outside the web root, and close parser bypasses. VERIFY sensitive extensions aren't served as raw content.
items.how_to_find:
1) Request files with revealing extensions: .bak, .old, .inc, .config, .zip, .java, .cs, .sql, .txt, .swp, and source with a trailing ~.
2) Check whether the server returns raw source/config instead of executing/blocking it.
3) Try framework bypasses (e.g. adding a null byte or alternate extension) to defeat handling rules.
4) Raw source/config returned via extension = finding.

items.title: Review Old Backup and Unreferenced Files for Sensitive Information
items.description: Find and analyse unreferenced files that might contain sensitive information.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fuzz for backups/unreferenced files based on known names (index.php.bak, login.php~, config.old, .env.save) and directory listings.
2) Also derive candidates from spidered filenames (append .bak/.old/.zip).
3) Download hits and inspect for source, credentials, or old vulnerable code.
4) A reachable backup/unreferenced file with sensitive data = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove backup/unreferenced files from web-accessible paths and block their extensions at the server. Add a regression check. VERIFY no such files are reachable.

items.title: Enumerate Infrastructure and Application Admin Interfaces
items.description: Identify hidden administrator interfaces and functionality.
items.how_to_find:
1) Brute-force common admin paths (/admin, /administrator, /manage, /console, /cpanel) with a wordlist and check JS/comments for hidden admin links.
2) Try alternate ports and vhosts.
3) For each interface found, test whether it's reachable without auth or with default creds.
4) A discoverable, weakly-protected admin interface = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin interfaces to trusted networks, require strong auth/MFA, and avoid predictable paths. Add a regression test. VERIFY admin interfaces aren't openly reachable.

items.title: Test HTTP Methods
items.description: Enumerate supported HTTP methods. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send OPTIONS to enumerate allowed methods, then test each (PUT, DELETE, TRACE, CONNECT, PATCH) against sensitive paths.
2) Check whether dangerous methods are enabled and whether authz is enforced per method.
3) Try method override headers to reach blocked verbs.
4) Dangerous/unneeded methods enabled = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict to the methods each endpoint needs and disable WebDAV/TRACE/CONNECT. Add a regression test. VERIFY only required methods are allowed.

items.title: Test HTTP Strict Transport Security
items.description: Review the HSTS header and its validity. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Send Strict-Transport-Security with a long max-age (>= 1 year), includeSubDomains, and consider preload. Add a regression test. VERIFY HSTS is present and correct on HTTPS responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check HTTPS responses for the Strict-Transport-Security header (curl -I).
2) Verify max-age is adequate and includeSubDomains/preload are set as appropriate.
3) Missing or weak HSTS (short max-age, no includeSubDomains) leaves users open to SSL-strip/downgrade.
4) Absent/weak HSTS = finding.

items.title: Test File Permission
items.description: Review and identify any rogue file permissions.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where you have host/container access, review permissions on config files, keys, logs, and web root (ls -la; look for world-writable/world-readable sensitive files).
2) Check that the web server runs as a low-privileged user and can't write to code dirs.
3) Overly permissive files (e.g. 777, world-readable secrets) enable tampering/disclosure.
4) Rogue file permissions = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege file permissions (restrict secrets to the owning user, non-writable code dirs) and run services as unprivileged users. VERIFY permissions follow least privilege.

items.title: Test for Subdomain Takeover
items.description: Enumerate all possible domains (previous and current).
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate current and historical subdomains (amass, subfinder, crt.sh) and resolve them.
2) Look for CNAMEs to SaaS providers with fingerprint 'not found' pages (subjack, nuclei takeover templates).
3) Confirm the resource is claimable; prove non-destructively with a benign marker.
4) A claimable dangling subdomain = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove/repoint dangling records and reclaim provider resources; add continuous DNS monitoring. VERIFY no subdomain points to an unclaimed resource.

items.title: Test Cloud Storage
items.description: Assess that the access control configuration for the storage services is properly in place.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify cloud storage URLs (S3/GCS/Azure Blob) from the app, then test access: list the bucket, read objects, and try writes anonymously and with low-priv creds.
2) Check bucket ACLs/policies for public read/write or overly broad principals.
3) Public/writable buckets leak or allow tampering of data.
4) Misconfigured bucket permissions = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Make buckets private by default, use least-privilege IAM policies, block public access, and enable logging. Add a regression check. VERIFY buckets aren't publicly listable/readable/writable.

items.title: Testing for Content Security Policy
items.description: Review the Content-Security-Policy header or meta element to identify misconfigurations.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy a strict CSP (no unsafe-inline/unsafe-eval, tight source allowlists, nonce/hash for scripts) and set frame-ancestors. Test with CSP Evaluator. VERIFY the policy blocks injected/inline scripts.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture the Content-Security-Policy header/meta and analyze it (Google CSP Evaluator).
2) Flag weaknesses: missing CSP, unsafe-inline/unsafe-eval, wildcard sources, or overly broad allowlists that permit XSS.
3) Confirm whether the policy actually mitigates injected script.
4) Missing/weak CSP = finding.

items.title: Test Path Confusion
items.description: Make sure application paths are configured correctly.
items.how_to_find:
1) Append path-confusion payloads to URLs: extra path segments, matrix params (;), encoded slashes (%2f), and cache-deceiving suffixes (/nonexistent.css).
2) See whether the server/CDN misroutes, caches sensitive responses, or bypasses access rules.
3) Test web cache deception (auth page served under a .css/.js path may get cached publicly).
4) Any misrouting/cache-deception/authz bypass = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize/canonicalize paths, configure the cache to only store safe content types, and match access rules on the canonical path. Add a regression test. VERIFY path tricks don't bypass routing/cache/authz.

items.title: Test Other HTTP Security Header Misconfigurations
items.description: Identify improperly configured security headers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review all response headers for misconfigurations: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, Cache-Control on sensitive pages, and CORS headers.
2) Flag missing, contradictory, or overly permissive values.
3) Confirm impact (e.g. missing nosniff enables MIME sniffing; missing Cache-Control caches sensitive data).
4) Any misconfigured security header = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set correct values for each header and add Cache-Control: no-store on sensitive responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY all security headers are present and correct.

items.title: Test Role Definitions
items.description: Identify and document roles used by the application.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate every role the app uses (anonymous, user, staff, admin, API/service) from docs, UI, and behavior.
2) Create/obtain an account per role and map which functions/data each can access.
3) Look for overlapping or overly broad roles that grant more than intended.
4) Roles that violate least privilege (or undocumented privileged roles) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Define least-privilege roles with a clear permission matrix and enforce it server-side. VERIFY each role can only access its intended functions.

items.title: Test User Registration Process
items.description: Verify that the identity requirements for user registration are aligned with business and security requirements.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce identity verification proportional to risk, rate-limit and de-duplicate registrations, and validate all fields server-side (including role). VERIFY registration can't create privileged or duplicate accounts.
items.how_to_find:
1) Register accounts and probe the process: can you register without email/identity verification? register the same identity twice? set your own role/tenant via hidden params?
2) Check whether registration meets the business's identity requirements and enforces uniqueness.
3) Try mass/automated registration (bot abuse).
4) Weak identity proof, role tampering, or unlimited registration = finding.

items.title: Test Account Provisioning Process
items.description: Verify which accounts may provision other accounts and of what type.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization on all provisioning/deprovisioning actions, restrict who can create privileged accounts, and de-provision promptly. VERIFY only authorized roles can provision accounts and the account type they set is enforced.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify who can create/modify/delete accounts and what account types they can grant.
2) As a lower-privileged user, try to provision accounts or escalate the type of a provisioned account (e.g. tamper a role param).
3) Check whether deprovisioned/expired accounts are actually disabled.
4) Unauthorized provisioning or privilege escalation via provisioning = finding.

items.title: Testing for Account Enumeration and Guessable User Account
items.description: Review processes that pertain to user identification (*e.g.* registration, login, etc.).
items.how_to_find:
1) Compare responses for valid vs invalid usernames on login, registration, and password reset (error text, HTTP status, response timing).
2) If they differ (e.g. 'user not found' vs 'wrong password'), you can enumerate valid accounts.
3) Also check for sequential/guessable usernames or IDs.
4) Any oracle distinguishing valid accounts = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Return identical, generic responses (and comparable timing) regardless of account existence across login/registration/reset. Add a regression test. VERIFY valid and invalid usernames are indistinguishable.

items.title: Testing for Weak or Unenforced Username Policy
items.description: A predictable or leak-prone username policy (e.g. usernames derived from names/emails) aids account enumeration and targeted attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine how usernames are formed (email, firstname.lastname, sequential IDs) - predictable schemes let attackers guess valid accounts.
2) Check whether the app confirms/reveals valid usernames anywhere (registration 'already taken', profile URLs).
3) Combine with the enumeration test above.
4) A predictable/leak-prone username policy = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid revealing whether a username exists, don't derive usernames from guessable data where it aids attacks, and pair with rate limiting/lockout. VERIFY usernames can't be trivially guessed or confirmed.

items.title: Testing for Credentials Transported over an Encrypted Channel
items.description: Check whether testing for credentials transported over an encrypted channel is implemented and enforced in production.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit the login form while proxying and confirm credentials are sent over HTTPS, not HTTP.
2) Check whether the login page itself is served over HTTP (allowing MITM before submit) and whether HTTP is redirected to HTTPS.
3) Look for credentials placed in the URL/query string or mixed-content requests.
4) Credentials over cleartext or a HTTP login page = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Serve the entire auth flow over HTTPS, redirect HTTP to HTTPS, enable HSTS, and never put credentials in URLs. VERIFY credentials only travel over TLS.

items.title: Testing for Default Credentials
items.description: Determine whether the application has any user accounts with default passwords.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify the app/appliance/CMS, then try its documented default credentials (admin/admin, admin/password, vendor defaults) on login and any admin panel.
2) Check new-account or setup flows that may create predictable default passwords.
3) Use a default-creds list per detected technology.
4) Any working default credential = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Force a password change on first use, remove/disable default accounts, and block known default passwords. VERIFY no default credentials work.

items.title: Testing for Weak Lock Out Mechanism
items.description: Evaluate the account lockout mechanism's ability to mitigate brute force password guessing.
items.how_to_fix: Add lockout/exponential backoff and rate limiting keyed to account+IP, add CAPTCHA/MFA, and alert on brute force - while avoiding easy lockout-based DoS. Add a regression test. VERIFY repeated failures are throttled.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit many wrong passwords for one account and see if it locks out or throttles after N attempts.
2) Test whether lockout can be bypassed by rotating IPs/headers (X-Forwarded-For) or by password spraying across many accounts.
3) Confirm there's rate limiting on the login endpoint.
4) No/weak lockout enabling brute force = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing for Bypassing Authentication Schema
items.description: Ensure that authentication is applied across all services that require it.
items.how_to_find:
1) Try to reach authenticated pages/APIs directly by URL without logging in (forced browsing).
2) Test bypass tricks: parameter tampering (auth=1, admin=true), SQLi in login, session/token forgery, and skipping steps in a multi-stage login.
3) Try accessing a resource with another user's/no token.
4) Any access to protected functionality without valid auth = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authentication server-side on every protected route (deny by default) and validate session/token on each request. Add a regression test. VERIFY protected resources require valid auth.

items.title: Testing for Vulnerable Remember Password
items.description: Validate that the generated session is managed securely and do not put the user's credentials in danger.
items.how_to_fix: Use opaque, server-side, revocable remember-me tokens (Secure/HttpOnly cookies) - never store credentials client-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY no credentials are persisted in the browser.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enable 'remember me' and inspect what gets stored (cookies, localStorage): look for plaintext/encoded passwords or reversible credentials.
2) Check whether the persistent token is opaque, tied to the session, and revocable, or whether it can be replayed/forged.
3) Test token lifetime and reuse after logout/password change.
4) Credentials stored client-side or a replayable persistent token = finding.

items.title: Testing for Browser Cache Weaknesses
items.description: Review if the application stores sensitive information on the client-side.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in, then use the browser Back button / view cached pages after logout to see if sensitive pages are still shown.
2) Inspect Cache-Control/Pragma/Expires headers on authenticated responses - missing no-store lets the browser/proxy cache sensitive data.
3) Check disk cache for cached sensitive responses.
4) Sensitive pages cached client-side = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send Cache-Control: no-store (and no-cache) on all authenticated/sensitive responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY sensitive pages aren't cached.

items.title: Testing for Weak Authentication Methods
items.description: Determine the resistance of the application against brute force password guessing using available password dictionaries by evaluating the length, complexity, reuse, and aging requirements of passwords.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a strong password policy (length >= 12, block breached/common passwords), rate limit, and offer MFA. Add a regression test. VERIFY weak/breached passwords are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
