1) Try to set weak passwords (123456, password, the username) at registration/change and see if the policy accepts them.
2) Check length/complexity/reuse/aging enforcement and whether breached-password lists are blocked.
3) Combined with weak lockout, attempt a dictionary attack against a test account.
4) Weak password policy allowing guessable passwords = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing for Weak Security Question Answer
items.description: Determine the complexity and how straight-forward the questions are.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review the security questions offered: are answers guessable, publicly discoverable (mother's maiden name, birth city), or few-valued?
2) Test whether question-based recovery alone can reset a password (weak second factor), and whether answer attempts are rate-limited.
3) Try brute-forcing an answer.
4) Guessable questions or unthrottled answers enabling account takeover = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Avoid security questions as a sole recovery factor; prefer email/MFA-based reset, and if used, rate-limit and treat answers like passwords. VERIFY questions can't be used to take over accounts.

items.title: Testing for Weak Password Change or Reset Functionalities
items.description: Determine whether the password change and reset functionality allows accounts to be compromised.
items.how_to_find:
1) On password change, check whether the current password is required and whether you can change another user's password (IDOR on user id).
2) On reset, analyze the token: is it random, single-use, short-lived, and bound to the account? Try predicting/reusing it, host-header poisoning of reset links, and skipping verification.
3) Check no user enumeration in reset responses.
4) Any flaw enabling account takeover via change/reset = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require current password to change it, use random single-use expiring reset tokens bound to the account, ignore untrusted Host headers, and invalidate sessions after reset. Add a regression test. VERIFY reset can't take over accounts.

items.title: Testing for Weaker Authentication in Alternative Channel
items.description: Identify alternative authentication channels.
items.how_to_fix: Apply the same authentication strength (MFA, lockout, password policy) across all channels. Add a regression test. VERIFY alternative channels are as strong as the primary.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate alternative auth entry points: mobile app APIs, legacy endpoints, partner/SSO logins, and admin subdomains.
2) Test whether these enforce the same controls (MFA, lockout, password policy) as the main site - weaker channels are the easy way in.
3) Try logging in via the weakest channel to bypass stronger controls.
4) A weaker alternative auth channel = finding.

items.title: Testing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
items.description: Identify the type of MFA used by the application.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify the MFA type (TOTP, SMS OTP, push) and where it's enforced.
2) Test bypasses: skipping the MFA step by requesting the post-MFA page directly, brute-forcing OTP (no rate limit), reusing/replaying OTPs, backup-code weaknesses, and 'remember device' abuse.
3) Check whether MFA is enforced on ALL sensitive flows and channels (see 2FA bypass reference).
4) Any MFA bypass = finding (high).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce MFA server-side on every sensitive action, rate-limit and expire OTPs, make them single-use, and secure backup codes/recovery. Add a regression test. VERIFY MFA can't be skipped or brute-forced.

items.title: Testing Directory Traversal File Include
items.description: Identify injection points that pertain to path traversal.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params that reference files/paths (?file=, ?page=, ?template=, download endpoints).
2) Inject traversal payloads: ../../../etc/passwd, encoded (..%2f), and Windows (..\) variants; try null bytes and absolute paths.
3) Watch for file contents (e.g. /etc/passwd) or included source in the response.
4) Any file read/include outside the intended dir = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid passing filenames from user input; use allowlists/IDs, canonicalize and confine to a base dir, and reject traversal sequences. Add a regression test. VERIFY traversal payloads can't escape the base path.

items.title: Testing for Bypassing Authorization Schema
items.description: Assess if unauthenticated, horizontal, or vertical access is possible.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization server-side on every request (deny by default), check ownership on each object, and never rely on hidden UI. Add a regression test. VERIFY horizontal/vertical/unauth access is blocked.
items.how_to_find:
1) Map functions per role, then as a low-priv (or unauthenticated) user request higher-priv endpoints directly.
2) Test horizontal access (act on another same-level user's data) and vertical access (reach admin functions).
3) Replay an admin-only request with a normal user's session/token.
4) Any access beyond your role = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing for Privilege Escalation
items.description: Identify injection points related to privilege manipulation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for params/fields that carry privilege (role=user, isAdmin=false, group_id, plan) in requests, cookies, or JWT claims.
2) Tamper them (role=admin, isAdmin=true) and resubmit; try mass-assignment on profile/update endpoints to set privileged fields.
3) Check whether new privileges are honored server-side.
4) Any elevation of privilege = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Derive privileges server-side from the authenticated identity (never trust client-supplied role fields) and use allowlisted bindable fields. Add a regression test. VERIFY privilege can't be raised via tampering/mass assignment.

items.title: Testing for Insecure Direct Object References
items.description: Identify points where object references may occur.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find requests that reference objects by identifier (id=123, /orders/123, uuid, filename).
2) As one user, change the identifier to another user's object and see if you can read/modify it.
3) Try sequential IDs, guessable/predictable references, and IDs in different params (path, body, header).
4) Accessing another user's object = finding (IDOR, high).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-object ownership/authorization checks server-side on every reference; use unguessable IDs as defense-in-depth. Add a regression test. VERIFY users can only access their own objects.

items.title: Testing for OAuth Weaknesses
items.description: Determine if OAuth2 implementation is vulnerable or using a deprecated or custom implementation.
items.how_to_fix: Use exact-match redirect_uri allowlists, PKCE, the state param, short-lived single-use auth codes, and validated scopes/tokens. Add a regression test. VERIFY redirect_uri and state can't be abused.
items.how_to_find:
1) Map the OAuth2 flow and test classic weaknesses: redirect_uri validation (open redirect / account takeover via attacker-controlled callback), missing/weak state (CSRF on the flow), missing PKCE on public clients, and auth-code reuse.
2) Try leaking codes/tokens via referer or open redirect, and scope escalation.
3) Confirm token validation (audience, expiry, signature).
4) Any OAuth flaw enabling token/account takeover = finding.

items.title: Testing for Session Management Schema
items.description: Gather session tokens, for the same user and for different users where possible.
items.how_to_find:
1) Collect session tokens across logins for the same and different users and analyze them: length, entropy, structure (is state encoded?), and predictability.
2) Check whether a new token is issued at login and whether tokens are random/unpredictable.
3) Try to infer or predict another user's token.
4) Predictable/low-entropy or client-decodable session tokens = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use the framework's proven session manager with long, high-entropy, opaque tokens; issue fresh tokens at login and invalidate server-side on logout. VERIFY tokens are unpredictable.

items.title: Testing for Cookies Attributes
items.description: Ensure that the proper security configuration is set for cookies.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect Set-Cookie for session/auth cookies and check attributes: Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Path, Domain, and expiry.
2) Missing HttpOnly (XSS can steal it), missing Secure (sent over HTTP), or SameSite=None without need (CSRF) are weaknesses.
3) Confirm sensitive cookies aren't overly scoped.
4) Missing/weak cookie attributes = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite (Lax/Strict) on session cookies with a tight scope. Add a regression test. VERIFY all sensitive cookies carry correct attributes.

items.title: Testing for Session Fixation
items.description: Analyze the authentication mechanism and its flow.
items.how_to_find:
1) Note the session cookie value before login, then log in and check whether the same value is kept (no rotation).
2) If so, an attacker who sets a victim's pre-auth cookie can hijack the post-auth session (fixation).
3) Test by fixing a known session id, having the victim authenticate, then reusing it.
4) Session id not rotated on login = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Regenerate the session id on every authentication and privilege change, and reject server-unknown session ids. Add a regression test. VERIFY the id changes at login.

items.title: Testing for Exposed Session Variables
items.description: Ensure that proper encryption is implemented.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for session tokens exposed in insecure places: URL/query strings, logs, Referer headers, or transmitted over HTTP.
2) Check that tokens travel only in cookies over TLS, not in GET params (which leak via history/referer/proxies).
3) Confirm no session data is stored unencrypted client-side.
4) Session tokens exposed in URLs/logs/cleartext = finding (high).
items.how_to_fix: Transmit session tokens only via Secure cookies over HTTPS, keep them out of URLs/logs, and set Referrer-Policy. Add a regression test. VERIFY tokens aren't exposed.

items.title: Testing for Cross Site Request Forgery
items.description: Determine whether it is possible to initiate requests on a user's behalf that are not initiated by the user.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE: change email, transfer, delete).
2) Check for anti-CSRF protection: a per-request token, SameSite cookies, or origin/referer checks.
3) Build a proof HTML page that auto-submits the request from another origin using the victim's cookies; if it succeeds, CSRF exists.
4) A state-changing action executable cross-site = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use per-session/per-request anti-CSRF tokens validated server-side and SameSite cookies; check Origin/Referer on sensitive actions. Add a regression test. VERIFY cross-site requests are rejected.

items.title: Testing for Logout Functionality
items.description: Assess the logout UI. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Invalidate the session server-side on logout (don't just clear the cookie), expose a clear logout, and require re-auth afterwards. Add a regression test. VERIFY the token is dead after logout.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture the session token, log out, then replay an authenticated request with the old token.
2) If it still works, logout only cleared the client cookie and didn't invalidate the server session.
3) Check for a visible logout control and whether back-button reaches authenticated pages after logout.
4) Session usable after logout = finding.

items.title: Testing Session Timeout
items.description: Validate that a hard session timeout exists.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in, stay idle, and after a period try an authenticated request to see if the session still works (idle timeout).
2) Check for an absolute timeout regardless of activity.
3) Confirm expiry is enforced server-side, not just client-side.
4) Overly long or absent session timeout = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce server-side idle and absolute session timeouts appropriate to risk, and require re-auth on expiry. Add a regression test. VERIFY sessions expire as configured.

items.title: Testing for Session Puzzling
items.description: Identify all session variables. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate session variables and probe flows that set/read them out of order (e.g. start password reset, then hit a different endpoint that reuses the same variable).
2) Session puzzling occurs when one variable is set in one context and trusted in another, enabling auth bypass or impersonation.
3) Try interleaving multi-step flows to reach an authenticated state without credentials.
4) Any auth bypass via reused session variables = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scope session variables to their intended flow, don't reuse variable names across contexts, and validate state transitions server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY flows can't be interleaved to bypass auth.

items.title: Testing for Session Hijacking
items.description: Identify vulnerable session cookies. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Copy a valid session token to a different browser/IP/user-agent and see if it still works (no binding).
2) Check whether the session is tied to any client attributes and whether tokens can be captured via XSS, sniffing (no Secure/TLS), or exposed URLs.
3) Replay the stolen token to access the account.
4) A token usable from another context (hijackable) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Protect tokens with Secure/HttpOnly cookies over TLS, rotate on privilege change, and consider binding/anomaly detection; fix any XSS. Add a regression test. VERIFY stolen tokens are hard to reuse.

items.title: Testing JSON Web Tokens
items.description: Determine whether the JWTs expose sensitive information.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a fixed strong algorithm server-side (reject alg=none and key confusion), verify signature/exp/aud, keep secrets strong, and don't store sensitive data in the payload. Add a regression test. VERIFY forged/altered tokens are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Decode the JWT (jwt.io) and inspect header/claims for sensitive data and the algorithm.
2) Test classic attacks: alg=none, HS256/RS256 key confusion, weak HMAC secret brute force, unverified signature, and missing exp/aud checks.
3) Tamper claims (e.g. role/admin) and see if the server accepts them.
4) Any accepted forged/altered token or leaked sensitive claim = finding.

items.title: Testing for Concurrent Sessions
items.description: Evaluate the application's session management by assessing the handling of multiple active sessions for a single user account.
items.how_to_find:
1) Log in as the same user from two browsers/devices simultaneously and see if both sessions stay valid.
2) Check whether the app limits/notifies concurrent sessions and whether a password change or logout invalidates other sessions.
3) Confirm there's a way to view/revoke active sessions.
4) Uncontrolled concurrent sessions (or password change not killing them) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Track active sessions, invalidate all sessions on password change/reset, and optionally limit/notify concurrent logins with a revoke UI. Add a regression test. VERIFY other sessions die after a credential change.

items.title: Testing for Reflected Cross Site Scripting
items.description: Identify variables that are reflected in responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params reflected in the response (search results, error messages, echoed input).
2) Inject a probe like <script>alert(1)</script> or context-aware payloads (', ", >, event handlers) and view the rendered page.
3) If your script executes, it's reflected XSS; note the exact param and context (HTML, attribute, JS).
4) Script execution from a request param = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output-encode all reflected data, validate input, and add a strong CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY the payload no longer executes.

items.title: Testing for Stored Cross Site Scripting
items.description: Identify stored input that is reflected on the client-side.
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output-encode stored data on render, validate/sanitize on input (HTML sanitizer for rich text), and add CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY the stored payload renders inert.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit an XSS payload into fields that get saved and later shown (profile, comments, names).
2) Then visit every page that displays that data - including admin views - to see if it executes.
3) Stored XSS is high impact because it hits other users/admins without interaction.
4) Saved payload executing on view = finding.

items.title: Testing for HTTP Verb Tampering
items.description: Check whether testing for http verb tampering is implemented and enforced in production.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take a protected request and resend it with different verbs (GET vs POST vs HEAD vs arbitrary methods like FOO).
2) Some access rules only cover GET/POST, so a HEAD or unusual verb can bypass authz or reach the handler.
3) Check whether the alternate verb performs the action or skips the security check.
4) A verb that bypasses access control = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization independent of HTTP method (deny by default) and only allow required verbs per endpoint. Add a regression test. VERIFY alternate verbs don't bypass checks.

items.title: Testing for HTTP Parameter Pollution
items.description: Identify the backend and the parsing method used.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize/reject duplicate parameters, be explicit about which occurrence is used, and validate server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY duplicate params can't bypass validation/authz.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send the same parameter twice (?id=1&id=2 or duplicate body fields) and observe which value the app/WAF/backend uses.
2) Divergent parsing between layers can bypass WAF rules, validation, or authorization.
3) Try polluting security-relevant params (role, amount, filter).
4) Duplicate-param handling that bypasses a control = finding.

items.title: Testing for SQL Injection
items.description: Identify SQL injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject SQL metacharacters (', ", --, ') into params/JSON/headers and watch for DB errors, changed results, or auth bypass (' OR '1'='1).
2) Confirm with boolean, error-based, UNION, and time-based (SLEEP) payloads; automate with sqlmap.
3) Prove impact by extracting a harmless value (DB version) - don't dump real data.
4) Any query-altering injection = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements (or ORM bindings), validate input, and apply least-privilege DB accounts. Add a regression test. VERIFY the payloads no longer alter the query.

items.title: Testing for LDAP Injection
items.description: Identify LDAP injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Escape LDAP special characters per RFC 4515, use safe filter-building APIs, validate input, and bind with least privilege. Add a regression test. VERIFY filter metacharacters are neutralized.
items.how_to_find:
1) On features backed by LDAP (login, directory search), inject LDAP metacharacters: *, (, ), |, &, and filter fragments like *)(uid=*.
2) Watch for auth bypass (matching all entries) or extra directory data returned.
3) Confirm the filter is being manipulated.
4) A manipulable LDAP filter = finding.

items.title: Testing for XML Injection
items.description: Identify XML injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) On endpoints accepting XML, inject metacharacters (<, >, &, ]]>) and malformed tags to see if you can alter the XML structure.
2) Test for XXE by declaring an external entity (e.g. reading file:///etc/passwd or an OOB URL) if the parser resolves entities.
3) Watch for reflected file content, errors, or OOB callbacks.
4) Structure manipulation or entity resolution = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable external entities/DTDs in the XML parser, validate against a schema, and encode inserted data. Add a regression test. VERIFY entities aren't resolved and structure can't be altered.

items.title: Testing for SSI Injection
items.description: Identify SSI injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Disable SSI where not needed, don't render user input through SSI, and HTML-encode output. Add a regression test. VERIFY SSI directives aren't executed.
items.how_to_find:
1) On servers that process Server-Side Includes, inject SSI directives such as <!--#exec cmd="id"--> or <!--#include virtual=...--> into stored/reflected fields.
2) Load the page and see if the directive is executed (command output or included file appears).
3) SSI can lead to command execution.
4) Executed SSI directive = finding.

items.title: Testing for XPath Injection
items.description: Identify XPATH injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) On features querying XML via XPath (login, search), inject XPath metacharacters: ', ", or ' or '1'='1.
2) Watch for auth bypass or extra nodes returned; use blind boolean/timing techniques to extract data.
3) Confirm the XPath expression is being manipulated.
4) A manipulable XPath query = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized XPath / variable binding, escape input, and validate. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected metacharacters can't change the query.

items.title: Testing for IMAP SMTP Injection
items.description: Identify IMAP/SMTP injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Reject CR/LF and control characters in fields used to build mail/IMAP commands, use safe mail APIs, and validate. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected commands/headers aren't honored.
items.how_to_find:
1) On mail features (contact forms, webmail), inject CRLF sequences and extra commands/headers into fields (subject, recipient) - e.g. adding Bcc: or IMAP command fragments.
2) See if you can inject headers, extra recipients, or IMAP/SMTP commands.
3) Watch for command manipulation or header injection.
4) Injected mail commands/headers = finding.

items.title: Testing for Code Injection
items.description: Identify injection points where you can inject code into the application.
items.how_to_fix: Never pass user input to eval/dynamic execution; use safe APIs, allowlists, and sandboxing. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected code is not executed.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find inputs that may reach a dynamic evaluator (eval, include, deserialization, template).
2) Inject language-specific probes (e.g. ;phpinfo(); , ${7*7}, arithmetic) and watch for evaluated output or side effects.
3) Escalate carefully to a benign proof (print a constant) - avoid destructive commands.
4) Any code execution from input = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing for Command Injection
items.description: Identify and assess command injection points.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params that may reach an OS command (ping/lookup tools, file conversion, export).
2) Inject shell metacharacters: ; id, | id, `id`, $(id), and && sleep 5; use timing or OOB (DNS/HTTP callback) for blind cases.
3) Confirm with a benign command (id/whoami) - never destructive commands.
4) Any OS command execution = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Avoid shelling out; use native APIs, pass arguments as arrays (no shell), allowlist input, and run least-privilege. Add a regression test. VERIFY metacharacters don't execute commands.

items.title: Testing for Format String Injection
items.description: Assess whether injecting format string conversion specifiers into user-controlled fields causes undesired behavior from the application.
items.how_to_fix: Never pass user input as a format string; use fixed format strings with input as arguments. Add a regression test. VERIFY conversion specifiers are treated as literal data.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject format specifiers (%s, %x, %n, {0}, {{...}}) into fields that may be used as a format string in logging/output.
2) Watch for crashes, memory leakage, unexpected values, or errors indicating the input is parsed as a format string.
3) Confirm the specifier is interpreted, not printed literally.
4) Interpreted format specifiers = finding.

items.title: Testing for Incubated Vulnerability
items.description: Identify injections that are stored and require a recall step to the stored injection.
items.how_to_find:
1) Plant a malicious payload via one channel (file upload, stored field, import) that is only triggered later when another user/process consumes it (recall step).
2) Example: upload a file with a stored XSS/webshell that executes when an admin views/processes it.
3) Trace the store-then-recall path and confirm execution downstream.
4) A stored payload that fires on a later recall = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/sanitize data both when stored and when consumed, and encode at every output sink. Add a regression test. VERIFY the payload is inert at the recall step.

items.title: Testing for HTTP Response Splitting
items.description: Identify user-controlled input that is reflected into HTTP response headers.
items.how_to_fix: Strip/reject CR/LF in any user data placed in headers, use framework header APIs that encode, and validate. Add a regression test. VERIFY CRLF can't inject headers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find user input reflected into response headers (redirect Location, Set-Cookie, custom headers).
2) Inject CRLF (%0d%0a) to add your own headers or split the response (e.g. injecting a second body).
3) Watch for the injected header/body appearing in the raw response.
4) CRLF header/response injection = finding (high).

items.title: Testing for HTTP Request Smuggling
items.description: Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where a front-end proxy and back-end server sit in front of the app, craft requests with ambiguous Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding (CL.TE / TE.CL / TE.TE).
2) Use Burp's HTTP Request Smuggler to detect desync via timing/differential responses.
3) Prove non-destructively (e.g. capture your own follow-up request) - avoid affecting other users.
4) A confirmed request desync = finding (high).
items.how_to_fix: Normalize/reject ambiguous length headers, use HTTP/2 end-to-end, and keep front-end/back-end parsing consistent. Add a regression test. VERIFY no desync is possible.

items.title: Testing for Host Header Injection
items.description: Assess if the Host header is being parsed dynamically in the application.
items.how_to_fix: Use a server-side allowlist of expected hosts, don't build absolute URLs/links from the Host header, and configure the web server to reject unknown hosts. Add a regression test. VERIFY a spoofed Host is ignored.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send requests with a spoofed/extra Host header (and X-Forwarded-Host) and see if the app reflects it into links, redirects, or password-reset emails.
2) A trusted Host header enables reset-link poisoning (account takeover) and web cache poisoning.
3) Confirm the injected host appears in a security-relevant place.
4) Host header used unsafely = finding.

items.title: Testing for Server-side Template Injection
items.description: Detect template injection vulnerability points.
items.how_to_fix: Don't render user input as a template; use logic-less templates or sandboxing and pass data as context variables. Add a regression test. VERIFY template expressions aren't evaluated.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject template expressions into inputs that may be rendered by a server-side engine: {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7}, <%= 7*7 %>.
2) If the response returns 49, the expression is evaluated (SSTI) - identify the engine and escalate carefully toward RCE with a benign proof.
3) Avoid destructive payloads.
4) Evaluated template expression = finding.

items.title: Testing for Server-Side Request Forgery
items.description: Identify SSRF injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params that make the server fetch a URL (webhooks, image/URL preview, PDF/import from URL, proxies).
2) Point them at an OOB collaborator URL to confirm the server calls out, then at internal targets (http://169.254.169.254/ cloud metadata, http://localhost/, internal IPs).
3) Watch for internal responses or metadata/credentials - stop at proof, don't exfiltrate.
4) Server fetching attacker-chosen internal URLs = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist permitted destinations, resolve+validate hosts (block private/link-local ranges), disable unused URL schemes/redirects, and use metadata protections (IMDSv2). Add a regression test. VERIFY internal targets are unreachable.

items.title: Testing for Mass Assignment
items.description: Identify requests that modify objects Assess if it is possible to modify fields never intended to be modified from outside.
items.how_to_fix: Bind only allowlisted fields (DTOs/strong params) and never map request bodies directly to privileged model fields. Add a regression test. VERIFY unexpected fields are ignored.
items.how_to_find:
1) Take an object-update request (profile, order) and add extra fields the UI doesn't expose: isAdmin, role, balance, verified, user_id.
2) If the server binds them to the model, you can change fields never meant to be user-editable.
3) Confirm the injected field persisted.
4) An accepted unexpected field = finding.

items.title: Testing for CSV Injection
items.description: Identify CSV/spreadsheet export features that include untrusted input.
items.how_to_fix: Prefix cells that begin with =, +, -, @, tab, or CR with a single quote (or wrap/escape), and validate on export. Add a regression test. VERIFY exported cells don't execute as formulas.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enter payloads starting with =, +, -, or @ (e.g. =cmd|'/c calc'!A1 or =HYPERLINK(...)) into fields that later appear in CSV/XLSX exports.
2) Download and open the export - if the cell is interpreted as a formula, it's CSV/formula injection (can run commands in some spreadsheet apps).
3) Confirm the formula executes on open.
4) Untrusted input exported as a live formula = finding.

items.title: Testing for Prototype Pollution
items.description: Identify functions and libraries that recursively merge, clone, or assign user-controlled properties.
items.how_to_find:
1) On JS/Node apps that merge/clone user JSON, inject __proto__, constructor, or prototype keys (e.g. {"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}} or ?__proto__[polluted]=1).
2) Check whether a base-object property becomes globally set (window.polluted or Object.prototype.isAdmin), which can escalate to XSS, auth bypass, or RCE.
3) Confirm the prototype was polluted.
4) Successful prototype pollution = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Reject __proto__/constructor/prototype keys, use Map or Object.create(null), freeze prototypes, and use safe merge libraries. Add a regression test. VERIFY these keys can't pollute the prototype.

sections.title: OWASP WSTG — Testing for Error Handling

items.title: Testing for Improper Error Handling
items.description: Identify existing error output. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger errors deliberately: malformed input, wrong types, huge values, missing params, bad routes, and forced exceptions.
2) Inspect responses for verbose messages that leak internals (SQL errors, file paths, framework/version, stack details) or inconsistent status codes.
3) Note anything that aids further attacks.
4) Error output that discloses internals = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Return generic error pages to users, log details server-side only, and handle exceptions uniformly. Add a regression test. VERIFY errors don't leak internals.

items.title: Testing for Stack Traces
items.description: Check whether testing for stack traces is implemented and enforced in production.
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug/verbose mode in production and return generic errors; log stack traces server-side only. Add a regression test. VERIFY no stack traces reach clients.
items.how_to_find:
1) Force exceptions (malformed input, unexpected types, broken routes) and look for full stack traces in the response.
2) Stack traces reveal frameworks, versions, file paths, and code structure that aid targeted attacks - often a sign debug mode is on in production.
3) Confirm the trace is returned to the client.
4) A client-visible stack trace = finding.

sections.title: OWASP WSTG — Testing for Weak Cryptography

items.title: Testing for Weak Transport Layer Security
items.description: Validate the service configuration. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Scan TLS config with testssl.sh / sslscan / SSL Labs.
2) Flag weaknesses: old protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1), weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES, export), missing forward secrecy, expired/mismatched/self-signed certs, and no HSTS.
3) Confirm the weak protocol/cipher is actually negotiable.
4) Any weak TLS configuration = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Support only TLS 1.2+/1.3 with strong forward-secret ciphers, use valid certificates, and enable HSTS. Re-scan to VERIFY only strong protocols/ciphers are offered.

items.title: Testing for Padding Oracle
items.description: Identify encrypted messages that rely on padding.
items.how_to_fix: Use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM or encrypt-then-MAC), return uniform errors, and don't expose padding-validity differences. Add a regression test. VERIFY no padding oracle exists.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find CBC-mode encrypted, client-visible tokens/params (base64 blobs in cookies/URLs).
2) Tamper the ciphertext bytes and observe whether the app distinguishes 'bad padding' from 'valid padding, bad data' via different errors/timing.
3) If so, a padding oracle lets you decrypt/forge; confirm with padbuster non-destructively.
4) A distinguishable padding response = finding.

items.title: Testing for Sensitive Information Sent via Unencrypted Channels
items.description: Identify sensitive information transmitted through the various channels.
items.how_to_find:
1) Watch all traffic (proxy/tcpdump) for sensitive data (credentials, tokens, PII, card data) sent over HTTP or other cleartext channels.
2) Check mixed content, non-TLS API/websocket calls, and unencrypted email/FTP links.
3) Confirm the data is actually transmitted in the clear.
4) Sensitive data over an unencrypted channel = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send all sensitive data over TLS only, redirect HTTP to HTTPS, enable HSTS, and remove mixed/cleartext transports. Add a regression test. VERIFY nothing sensitive travels in cleartext.

items.title: Testing for Weak Cryptographic Primitives
items.description: Provide a guideline for the identification weak encryption or hashing uses and implementations.
items.how_to_fix: Use modern algorithms (AES-GCM, SHA-256+, bcrypt/argon2 for passwords), proper key management, and random IVs/salts. Add a regression test. VERIFY no weak primitives remain.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify crypto in use (hashes, ciphers, password storage, tokens) from responses, cookies, and any source access.
2) Flag weak primitives: MD5/SHA1, DES/RC4, ECB mode, unsalted/fast password hashes, hardcoded keys, and static IVs.
3) Confirm the weak primitive protects something sensitive.
4) Use of a weak cryptographic primitive = finding (high).

items.title: Test Business Logic Data Validation
items.description: Identify data injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit values that are syntactically valid but logically impossible: negative quantity/price, huge amounts, past dates, another user's IDs, or out-of-sequence states.
2) Bypass client-side validation with a proxy and send directly to the server.
3) See whether the app enforces the business rules server-side.
4) Logically invalid data accepted = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce business rules and value constraints server-side (never trust client validation). Add a regression test. VERIFY logically invalid data is rejected.

items.title: Test Ability to Forge Requests
items.description: Review the project documentation looking for guessable, predictable, or hidden functionality of fields.
items.how_to_fix: Validate all fields server-side against expected values/state and don't trust hidden or guessable fields. Add a regression test. VERIFY forged/guessed values are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect requests for hidden, guessable, or predictable fields (prices, IDs, status, discount codes).
2) Forge requests with values the UI would never send (arbitrary price, another user's id, admin-only flags, skipped steps).
3) See whether the server accepts these forged requests.
4) A forged request that succeeds = finding.

items.title: Test Integrity Checks
items.description: Review the project documentation for components of the system that move, store, or handle data.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find data the client can edit that the server should protect (prices, roles, totals in hidden fields, cookies, or client-stored state).
2) Tamper it and submit; check whether the server re-validates/recomputes or blindly trusts the client value.
3) Also test whether audit logs can be altered.
4) Server trusting client-modifiable data = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Recompute/validate critical values server-side, sign or store trusted state server-side, and protect logs. Add a regression test. VERIFY tampered values are rejected.

items.title: Test for Process Timing
items.description: Review the project documentation for system functionality that may be impacted by time.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid timing-dependent logic, use atomic transactions/locking to prevent race conditions, and equalize response timing for sensitive checks. Add a regression test. VERIFY timing/races can't be abused.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for timing-sensitive logic: response-time differences that leak info (valid vs invalid user), and race conditions on concurrent actions.
2) Fire many parallel requests at limited actions (redeem coupon, withdraw funds, apply once) to trigger a TOCTOU race (double spend).
3) Measure timing oracles on auth/lookup endpoints.
4) An exploitable timing gap or race = finding.

items.title: Test Number of Times a Function Can Be Used Limits
items.description: Identify functions that must set limits to the times they can be called.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify actions meant to be used a limited number of times (redeem a coupon once, one vote, single free trial, one withdrawal).
2) Repeat the action beyond the intended limit (replay the request, reset state, use parallel requests).
3) See whether the server enforces the usage cap.
4) An action usable beyond its limit = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-user/per-resource usage limits server-side with atomic checks. Add a regression test. VERIFY the action can't exceed its limit.

items.title: Testing for the Circumvention of Work Flows
items.description: Review the project documentation for methods to skip or go through steps in the application process in a different order from the intended business logic flow.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce and validate workflow state/sequence server-side so steps can't be skipped or reordered. Add a regression test. VERIFY out-of-order steps are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) Map multi-step workflows (checkout, KYC, approval) and their intended order.
2) Try to skip or reorder steps: jump straight to the final step's endpoint, complete a purchase without payment, or approve without review.
3) See whether the server enforces step order/state.
4) A workflow step that can be skipped/reordered = finding.

items.title: Test Defenses Against Application Misuse
items.description: Generate notes from all tests conducted against the system.
items.how_to_fix: Add abuse detection/response (rate limits, lockouts, alerts, blocking) for anomalous behavior. Add monitoring. VERIFY misuse triggers a defensive response.
items.how_to_find:
1) Behave like an attacker: send many invalid inputs, tamper params repeatedly, hit forced-browse paths, and trip validation.
2) Observe whether the app detects and responds (rate limiting, lockout, alerts, blocking) or silently tolerates abuse.
3) A mature app reacts to obvious misuse.
4) No defensive response to clear misuse = finding.

items.title: Test Upload of Unexpected File Types
items.description: Review the project documentation for file types that are rejected by the system.
items.how_to_fix: Validate file type by content (magic bytes) not just extension/MIME, enforce an allowlist, and reject mismatches server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY disallowed types are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
1) On upload features, submit file types the app shouldn't accept (e.g. .php, .svg, .html where only images are expected).
2) Try bypasses: fake extension, spoofed Content-Type, double extension (shell.php.jpg), and magic-byte tricks.
3) See whether the server accepts and where the file lands.
4) An unexpected file type accepted = finding.

items.title: Test Upload of Malicious Files
items.description: Identify the file upload functionality. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Store uploads outside the web root with non-executable permissions and random names, validate content, scan for malware, and serve via a handler. Add a regression test. VERIFY uploaded files can't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload files that could be dangerous if mishandled: a web shell (.php/.jsp), an SVG/HTML with embedded script (stored XSS), a zip-bomb/oversized file, or a polyglot.
2) Then try to access the uploaded file's URL to see if it executes or renders script.
3) Prove impact non-destructively (benign marker).
4) A malicious file that executes/renders = finding.

items.title: Test Payment Functionality
items.description: Determine whether the business logic for the e-commerce functionality is robust.
items.how_to_fix: Compute prices/totals server-side from trusted data, validate payment status with the gateway server-side, and use atomic transactions. Add a regression test. VERIFY prices/quantities/coupons can't be tampered.
items.how_to_find:
1) On checkout/payment flows, tamper with amounts, currency, quantity (negative), item IDs, discount/coupon codes, and shipping in the request.
2) Test race conditions on coupon/gift-card redemption and whether the server re-validates the final price and payment status.
3) Try skipping the payment step or replaying a success callback.
4) Any price/payment manipulation = finding (critical) - use test/sandbox only.

items.title: Testing for DOM-Based Cross Site Scripting
items.description: Identify DOM sinks. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: 1) Look for JS that reads from sources (location.hash, location.search, document.referrer, postMessage) and writes to sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval, setTimeout). 2) Inject payloads via the fragment/query (e.g. #) and see if they execute purely client-side. 3) DevTools/DOM Invader helps trace source->sink. 4) Client-side script execution from a controllable source = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid dangerous sinks; use textContent/safe DOM APIs, encode for context, sanitize with Trusted Types, and add CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY the payload no longer executes.

items.title: Testing for JavaScript Execution
items.description: Identify sinks and possible JavaScript injection points.
items.how_to_fix: Never pass user input to eval/Function/setTimeout-with-string; use safe alternatives and validate/encode. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected JS isn't executed.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find inputs that flow into JS execution sinks (eval, Function(), setTimeout/setInterval with strings, script src).
2) Inject JS expressions (e.g. ;alert(1) or arithmetic) and check whether they execute in the browser.
3) Trace the source-to-sink path with DevTools.
4) Arbitrary JS execution from input = finding.

items.title: Testing for HTML Injection
items.description: Identify HTML injection points and assess the severity of the injected content.
items.how_to_find: 1) Inject benign HTML (e.g. test, , ) into fields that are reflected/stored and rendered. 2) If the markup renders instead of being escaped, you can inject HTML (content spoofing, phishing forms) - often a step short of XSS. 3) Note the context and whether script tags/handlers are also allowed. 4) Rendered attacker HTML = finding.
items.how_to_fix: HTML-encode output in the correct context and sanitize rich text with an allowlist. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected HTML renders as inert text.

items.title: Testing for Client-side URL Redirect
items.description: Identify injection points that handle URLs or paths.
items.how_to_fix: Don't build redirects from user-controlled URLs; use an allowlist of destinations or relative paths only. Add a regression test. VERIFY external redirects are blocked.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params that set a redirect target (?next=, ?url=, ?returnUrl=) used by client-side JS (location = ...).
2) Point them at an external site (//evil.com) or javascript: URI and see if the browser navigates there.
3) Open redirects enable phishing and can leak tokens; javascript: can mean XSS.
4) Redirect to an attacker-controlled URL = finding.

items.title: Testing for CSS Injection
items.description: Identify CSS injection points. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Don't reflect user input into CSS/style contexts; if unavoidable, strictly validate and encode. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected CSS can't exfiltrate data or alter layout maliciously.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find places where input is reflected into a <style> block or style attribute.
2) Inject CSS (e.g. attribute selectors with background:url() to a collaborator) to test data exfiltration, UI redressing, or history-sniffing style tricks.
3) Watch for OOB requests confirming CSS is parsed.
4) Attacker-controlled CSS affecting the page/exfiltrating data = finding.

items.title: Testing for Client-side Resource Manipulation
items.description: Identify sinks with weak input validation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find JS that builds resource references (script src, link href, iframe src, XHR/fetch URL) from user-controllable input (URL params, hash, postMessage).
2) Point them at an attacker-controlled origin and see if the page loads/executes remote resources.
3) This can escalate to XSS or data theft.
4) Loading an attacker-controlled resource = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Validate/allowlist resource URLs, avoid building them from untrusted input, and use CSP + Subresource Integrity. Add a regression test. VERIFY remote resources can't be injected.

items.title: Testing Cross Origin Resource Sharing
items.description: Identify endpoints that implement CORS. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_fix: Use a strict server-side allowlist of trusted origins, never reflect arbitrary Origin, and only send Allow-Credentials with specific origins (never with *). Add a regression test. VERIFY untrusted origins are denied.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send requests with an Origin header (including a random/evil one) and inspect Access-Control-Allow-Origin/Allow-Credentials in the response.
2) Dangerous configs: reflecting any Origin, Allow-Origin: * with credentials, trusting null, or weak regex (evil-example.com).
3) If a malicious origin is trusted with credentials, cross-origin data theft is possible.
4) An over-permissive CORS policy = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing for Cross Site Flashing
items.description: Decompile and analyze the application's code.
items.how_to_fix: Retire Flash/SWF content; if any remains, restrict allowScriptAccess/allowNetworking and validate FlashVars. VERIFY no exploitable SWF is served.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check for any legacy Flash (.swf) content (mostly obsolete now).
2) If present, decompile with a SWF decompiler and look for unsafe FlashVars/ExternalInterface use and lax allowScriptAccess/allowNetworking that let attacker input reach ActionScript sinks.
3) Test whether crafted params cause script execution in the SWF context.
4) An exploitable SWF = finding.

items.title: Testing for Clickjacking
items.description: Assess application vulnerability to clickjacking attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check response headers for X-Frame-Options and CSP frame-ancestors.
2) Build a test page that loads the target in an <iframe>; if it renders, it's framable.
3) For sensitive actions, this enables clickjacking (tricking users into clicking hidden controls).
4) A framable sensitive page with no frame protection = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set CSP frame-ancestors 'self' (or a strict allowlist) and X-Frame-Options: DENY/SAMEORIGIN on sensitive pages. Add a regression test. VERIFY the page can't be framed cross-origin.

items.title: Testing WebSockets
items.description: Identify the usage of WebSockets. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Use wss://, validate the Origin on the handshake, authenticate/authorize every message server-side, and validate message input. Add a regression test. VERIFY cross-origin/unauthorized WS use is blocked.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify WebSocket usage (ws://, wss://) and intercept messages with Burp.
2) Test: is wss (TLS) used? is the Origin validated on handshake (else Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking)? is authz enforced per message?
3) Tamper/replay messages and inject payloads (XSS/injection) into WS data.
4) Missing origin check, cleartext ws, or unauthenticated messages = finding.

items.title: Testing Web Messaging
items.description: Assess the security of the message's origin.
items.how_to_fix: Always verify event.origin against an allowlist in message handlers, avoid targetOrigin '*' when sending sensitive data, and never pass message data to dangerous sinks. Add a regression test. VERIFY messages from untrusted origins are ignored.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find postMessage handlers (addEventListener('message', ...)) and check whether they validate event.origin.
2) If not, send crafted messages from another origin and see if the handler acts on them or feeds them into sinks (innerHTML/eval).
3) Also check senders using targetOrigin '*' with sensitive data.
4) A message handler that trusts any origin = finding.

items.title: Testing Browser Storage
items.description: Determine whether the site is storing sensitive data in client-side storage.
items.how_to_fix: Don't store secrets/PII in localStorage/sessionStorage/IndexedDB; keep session tokens in HttpOnly cookies and minimize client-side data. Add a regression test. VERIFY no sensitive data is stored client-side.
items.how_to_find:
1) In DevTools > Application, inspect localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, and Web SQL.
2) Look for sensitive data stored client-side: tokens, passwords, PII, API keys - all readable by any JS (so exposed to XSS).
3) Note anything sensitive persisted in the browser.
4) Sensitive data in client-side storage = finding.

items.title: Testing for Cross Site Script Inclusion
items.description: Locate sensitive data across the system. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_fix: Don't return sensitive data as executable/JSONP script, require anti-CSRF tokens, and prefer non-script JSON with proper content type. Add a regression test. VERIFY sensitive endpoints can't be included cross-site as script.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find endpoints that return sensitive data as JavaScript/JSONP (dynamic script responses).
2) From an attacker page, include the endpoint via <script src> while the victim is logged in and see if you can read leaked values (global vars, callback data).
3) XSSI leaks data cross-origin when responses are script-includable.
4) Sensitive data readable via script inclusion = finding.

items.title: Testing for Reverse Tabnabbing
items.description: Check whether testing for reverse tabnabbing is implemented and enforced in production.
items.how_to_fix: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to target=_blank links (modern browsers default to noopener, but set it explicitly). Add a regression test. VERIFY opened pages can't access window.opener.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find links/buttons that open a new tab (target=_blank) to external or user-supplied URLs.
2) Check whether they include rel=noopener/noreferrer; without it, the opened page can use window.opener.location to redirect the original tab to a phishing page.
3) Test with a page that sets window.opener.location.
4) target=_blank without noopener to untrusted URLs = finding.

items.title: Testing for Client-side Template Injection
items.description: Identify the client-side framework and its version used by the application.
items.how_to_fix: Don't bind user input into client-side templates; treat it as data, use frameworks' safe interpolation, and avoid v-html/ng-bind-html on untrusted input. Add a regression test. VERIFY template expressions aren't evaluated.
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify the client-side framework (Angular, Vue) and its version.
2) Inject template expressions ({{7*7}}, {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}}) into inputs bound into templates.
3) If the browser evaluates them (49 appears, or script runs), it's client-side template injection - can escalate to XSS.
4) Evaluated client-side template expression = finding (high).

items.title: API Reconnaissance
items.description: Find all API endpoints supported by the backend server code, documented or undocumented.
items.how_to_fix: Publish and enforce an API inventory, remove/retire undocumented and deprecated endpoints, and require auth on every route. VERIFY only intended endpoints are reachable.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate API endpoints from JS bundles, mobile apps, swagger/openapi docs, and proxy traffic.
2) Fuzz for undocumented routes/versions (/api/v1 vs /api/v2, /internal, /admin) with wordlists.
3) Note methods, params, and auth requirements for each - shadow/deprecated endpoints are common weak points.
4) Undocumented/forgotten endpoints reachable = finding to investigate.

items.title: API Broken Object Level Authorization
items.description: The objective of this test is to identify whether the API enforces proper **object-level authorization** checks, ensuring that users can only access and manipulate objects they are authorized to interact with.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find API calls that reference objects by id (/api/users/123, /api/orders/456).
2) As one user, swap the id to another user's object and check whether the API returns/modifies it (BOLA/IDOR - the #1 API risk).
3) Test all methods (GET/PUT/DELETE) and nested IDs.
4) Accessing another user's object via the API = finding (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce object-level authorization server-side on every request, checking the authenticated user owns/may access the object. Add a regression test. VERIFY cross-user object access is denied.

items.title: Testing for Excessive Data Exposure
items.description: Identify API responses that contain more data than what the client application displays or requires.
items.how_to_fix: Return only the fields the client needs (server-side response shaping/DTOs), never rely on the client to hide data. Add a regression test. VERIFY responses exclude sensitive fields.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect raw API responses (not just the rendered UI) for fields the client never displays: password hashes, tokens, internal flags, other users' PII.
2) APIs often return whole objects and let the UI filter - so sensitive data leaks to anyone reading the response.
3) Compare response body vs what's shown.
4) Sensitive fields returned but unused = finding.

items.title: API Broken Function Level Authorization
items.description: The goal of this test is to determine if the API enforces **role or privilege-based access control** to restrict users from accessing or executing functions they are not authorized to use.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce role/privilege checks server-side on every function/route (deny by default), especially admin endpoints. Add a regression test. VERIFY low-priv users can't call privileged functions.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate privileged endpoints (admin, management, DELETE/PUT actions).
2) As a low-privilege or unauthenticated user, call them directly (and try changing GET to POST/PUT/DELETE) to see if role checks are missing (BFLA).
3) Guess admin routes by pattern (/api/admin/*).
4) A privileged function callable by an unauthorized user = finding (critical).

items.title: Testing GraphQL
items.description: Assess that a secure and production-ready configuration is deployed.
items.how_to_fix: Disable introspection in production, enforce authz per resolver/field, add query depth/complexity limits and rate limiting, and validate input. Add a regression test. VERIFY introspection is off and resolvers enforce authz.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find the GraphQL endpoint (/graphql) and run an introspection query to dump the schema.
2) Test: is introspection enabled in prod? are per-field authz checks enforced (try querying sensitive fields/mutations as a low-priv user)? is there depth/complexity limiting (send a deeply nested query to test DoS)?
3) Test injection through arguments and batching/alias abuse for rate-limit bypass.
4) Introspection on, missing authz, or no query-cost limits = finding.
